original_text: Year 351 BC was a year of the pre-Julian Roman calendar. At the time it was known as the Year of the Consulship of Peticus and Crispinus (or, less frequently, year 403 Ab urbe condita). The denomination 351 BC for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the prevalent method in Europe for naming years. Events By place Persian Empire Encouraged by a failed effort at invasion of Egypt by King Artaxerxes III, Phoenicia and Cyprus revolt against Persia. Greece Demosthenes tries to get the Athenians to cease depending on paid mercenaries and return to the old concept of a citizen army. He also delivers his First Philippic, warning Athenians of the folly of believing that Philip's ill health will save Athens from the Macedonians. In response, Athens' citizens votes for increased armaments. Roman Republic The Etruscans are badly defeated by the Romans and abandon their attacks on the city and sue for peace. First use of the heavy throwing spear, the pilum, (according to Livy) in battle against the Gauls. Gaius Marcius Rutilus becomes the first Roman plebeian to be elected to the office of censor. Births Deaths References
simplified_kindergarten:
It was the year 351 BC. Long ago, people called it the Year of Peticus and Crispinus. Or sometimes, year 403. Today, we use '351 BC' to talk about this year.

Many things happened in different places.
In the land of Persia, King Artaxerxes III tried to take over Egypt. He did not win. So, Phoenicia and Cyprus fought against Persia.

In Greece, Demosthenes told people in Athens to stop paying soldiers. He wanted them to use their own people as soldiers again. He gave a speech called the First Philippic. He told people in Athens not to think that Philip being sick would save them from the Macedonians. After this, people in Athens voted to get more weapons.

In the Roman land, the Romans beat the Etruscans very badly. The Etruscans stopped fighting the city. They asked for peace. People first used a big throwing spear. It was called the pilum. A man named Livy said this. They used it in a fight against the Gauls. Gaius Marcius Rutilus was the first common Roman person to be chosen as a censor.
simplified_primary_school:
The year 351 BC was a very long time ago. Back then, people called it the year of two leaders, Peticus and Crispinus. We call it 351 BC today. This name started being used much later, when people in Europe began using a new way to name years.

In the Persian Empire, King Artaxerxes III tried to invade Egypt but failed. Because of this, two places called Phoenicia and Cyprus fought against Persia.

In Greece, a man named Demosthenes wanted the people of Athens to stop using soldiers who were paid. He wanted them to use their own citizens as soldiers, like they did a long time ago. He also gave a speech. He warned the people of Athens not to think that King Philip of Macedon's sickness would save them from his army. So, the people of Athens voted to make more weapons.

In the Roman Republic, the Etruscans lost badly to the Romans. They stopped attacking the city and asked for peace. A new heavy spear, called a pilum, was used for the first time in a fight against the Gauls. A writer named Livy said this happened. Gaius Marcius Rutilus became the first ordinary Roman person to be chosen for an important job called censor.
simplified_secondary_school:
The year 351 BC was part of the ancient Roman calendar, before the Julian calendar was introduced. At that time, Romans often identified it by the names of their consuls, calling it the "Year of the Consulship of Peticus and Crispinus." Less commonly, they referred to it as year 403 "Ab urbe condita," meaning "from the founding of the city." The way we refer to it today as "351 BC" began to be used in the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini (AD) calendar system became the main way to name years across Europe.

During 351 BC, several important events took place.

In the Persian Empire, Phoenicia and Cyprus revolted against Persia. This rebellion was sparked by the failed attempt of King Artaxerxes III to invade Egypt.

In Greece, the orator Demosthenes tried to convince the Athenians to stop relying on paid soldiers (mercenaries) and instead return to their traditional system of a citizen army. He also delivered his "First Philippic" speech, where he warned Athenians that it would be foolish to believe that King Philip of Macedon's poor health would protect Athens from the Macedonians. In response to his warnings, the citizens of Athens voted to increase their military armaments.

In the Roman Republic, the Romans severely defeated the Etruscans, who then stopped their attacks on the city of Rome and asked for peace. It was also in this year, according to the historian Livy, that the *pilum* – a heavy throwing spear – was first used in battle against the Gauls. Furthermore, Gaius Marcius Rutilus made history by becoming the first Roman plebeian (a common citizen, not an aristocrat) to be elected to the important office of censor.

Canadians are voting on the 49 songs that best define their nation, for President-elect Barack Obama's benefit. Obama's taste in music runs the gamut from old-school R&B to blues and classical. "His playlist could definitely benefit from some Canadian content," the station said. Listeners can pick from 100 songs. They range from classical (Montreal Symphony Orchestra), to classic rock (The Band, Rush), to more contemporary fare (Feist, Arcade Fire). Canada's public radio, CBC Radio 2, is asking listeners to choose 49 songs that will best represent the country to President-elect Barack Obama. Canadians are voting online for this project, titled "49 Songs from North of the 49th Parallel."

Voting closes on Friday. CBC Radio 2 will then reveal and play the selected songs on Tuesday, the day of Barack Obama's presidential inauguration.

Denise Donion, CBC's executive director, stated on the station's website, "One of the best ways to know Canada is through the depth and breath of our artistic expression." The station pointed out that Obama's musical tastes are broad, ranging from old-school R&B to blues and classical music. They added that "His playlist could definitely benefit from some Canadian content."

Listeners can select from a list of 100 songs. These options include classical pieces (such as the Montreal Symphony Orchestra), classic rock (like The Band and Rush), and more contemporary artists (such as Feist and Arcade Fire). The complete list of songs is available at http://www.cbc.ca/radio2/obamasplaylist/.

original_text: Cheddite is a class of explosive materials invented in 1897 by E. A. G. Street of the firm of Berges, Corbin et Cie and originally manufactured in the town of Chedde in Haute-Savoie, France in the early twentieth century. Closely related to Sprengel explosives, cheddites consisted of a high proportion of inorganic chlorates mixed with nitroaromatics (e.g. nitrobenzene or dinitrotoluene) plus a little paraffin or castor oil as a moderant for the chlorate. Several different types were made, and they were principally used in quarrying. Due to availability of ingredients and easy production process it was also the most common explosive material manufactured by the Polish Underground State in occupied Poland during World War II; it was used for production of the R wz. 42 and Filipinka hand grenades. Since the 1970s, Cheddite is the commercial name for primers CX series (CX 50, CX 1000 and CX 2000) for shotgun cartridges. See also Sprengel explosive Boxer Primer References Explosives

Cheddite is a special boom-boom powder. A man named Street made it. He made it in 1897. It was first made in a town called Chedde. This town is in France. This was a long, long time ago.

It is like another boom-boom powder. It was made of many powders. It had a powder called chlorate. It had other powders too. It also had a little oil. The oil made it work just right.

They made many kinds of it. People used it to break big rocks.

It was easy to get the stuff to make it. It was easy to make. So, people in Poland made a lot of it. This was during a big war. The war was called World War II. They used it to make small bombs. The bombs were called R wz. 42 and Filipinka.

Now, for a long time, Cheddite is a name for small parts. These parts help guns shoot. They are called CX 50, CX 1000, and CX 2000. They go in shotgun bullets.

It was like other explosives called Sprengel explosives. Cheddite was made mostly of special salts called chlorates. These were mixed with other chemicals like nitrobenzene or dinitrotoluene. A little bit of paraffin or castor oil was added to help control the chlorate.

Many different kinds of Cheddite were made. People mostly used them to break up rocks in quarries.

It was easy to get the things needed to make it, and it was easy to make. So, it was the most common explosive made by a group called the Polish Underground State in occupied Poland during World War II. They used it to make hand grenades called R wz. 42 and Filipinka.

Since the 1970s, Cheddite has been the brand name for small parts called primers. These primers are for shotgun cartridges and come in types like CX 50, CX 1000, and CX 2000.
simplified_secondary_school:
Cheddite is a class of explosive materials invented in 1897 by E. A. G. Street, who worked for the firm Berges, Corbin et Cie. It was first manufactured in the early twentieth century in the town of Chedde, Haute-Savoie, France.

Cheddites are closely related to Sprengel explosives. They were made from a high proportion of inorganic chlorates mixed with nitroaromatics, such as nitrobenzene or dinitrotoluene. A small amount of paraffin or castor oil was also added to control the chlorate's reaction.

Several different types of cheddite were produced, and they were mainly used in quarrying. During World War II, cheddite became the most common explosive made by the Polish Underground State in occupied Poland. This was due to the availability of its ingredients and its easy production process. It was used to make R wz. 42 and Filipinka hand grenades.

Since the 1970s, "Cheddite" has also been the commercial name for CX series primers (CX 50, CX 1000, and CX 2000) used in shotgun cartridges.

original_text: Kabul, Afghanistan (CNN)As the avalanches continue in mountainous northeastern Afghanistan, the death toll keeps rising. For a third day in a row, heavy snowstorms caused avalanches in the Panjshir province on Thursday, bringing the total to 168, officials said. They began Tuesday, continued Wednesday and two more avalanches took place Thursday, said provincial Gov. Abdul Rahman Kabiri. Authorities fear the toll will grow much higher since rescue personnel are yet to reach the worst affected areas. Panjshir is a small, mountainous province north of the capital, Kabul. Its residents are generally poor, earning their living as farmers, shepherds or operators of small businesses such as groceries.

original_text: (CNN) -- Brazilian football legend Pele's condition has improved, according to a Thursday evening statement from a Sao Paulo hospital. There had been earlier concerns about Pele's health after Albert Einstein Hospital issued a release that said his condition was "unstable." Thursday night's release said Pele was relocated to the intensive care unit because a kidney dialysis machine he needed was in ICU. "He is now temporarily on renal support without any additional support," the statement said. The 74-year-old's representative and Pele's Twitter account dispute that the great is in ICU, saying he had been moved to a "special care" room with greater privacy. "I want to take this opportunity to let you know that I am doing fine," his Twitter feed stated. Pele was admitted to the medical facility earlier this week with a urinary tract infection. He recently underwent surgery to remove kidney stones at the same institution. Pele is considered one of the greats of world soccer, winning three World Cups for Brazil in 1958, 1962 and 1970. The Brazilian is also one of the most globally recognized faces in sports. CNN's Barbara Arvanitidis contributed to this report.

original_text: (CNN) -- Singer Chris Brown's arrest on a domestic violence charge prompted Wrigley to suspend its Doublemint gum campaign that uses Brown as a spokesman. "Wrigley is concerned by the serious allegations made against Chris Brown," Wrigley spokeswoman Jennifer Luth said. Brown was arrested Sunday night in connection with an alleged domestic violence incident in Hollywood early Sunday morning, police said. He was released on a $50,000 bond and given a March 5 court date. A Doublemint commercial features Brown singing about the gum to the tune of his hit song "Forever." "We believe Mr. Brown should be afforded the same due process as any citizen," Luth said. "However, we have made the decision to suspend the current advertising featuring Brown and any related marketing communications until the matter is resolved."

original_text: LONDON, England (CNN) -- A massive fire engulfed the four-story offices of a major film company in central London on Friday, sending clouds of white smoke over the city skyline, the London fire department said. Streets in London were cordoned off while firefighters tackled the blaze. Two firefighters were taken to the hospital with minor injuries as a result of the blaze in the Future Films building on Dean Street in the city's Soho district, London ambulance services said. The London Fire Brigade dispatched 12 engines and 55 firefighters to battle the fire, which began before 2 p.m. (9 a.m. ET). As a result, an adjacent street -- the W1 -- has been closed to traffic and pedestrians. When reached by CNN, Future Films founder and managing director Stephen Margolis had no comment about the fire. Future Films has been involved in the production of more than 120 films since Margolis created the company in 2000, including "Bend it like Beckham" (2002), "The Importance of Being Earnest" (2002), and "Transsiberian" (2008). CNN's Per Nyberg and Laura Perez Maestro contributed to this report.

simplified_secondary_school: The seriation problem involves rearranging the rows of a matrix so that all its columns have the same shape, for example, they are monotone increasing. We propose a statistical method for this problem, assuming the matrix we observe contains errors (noise). We then investigate the best possible accuracy (minimax rate) for estimating the true matrix. Specifically, for columns that are either unimodal (increase then decrease) or monotone (always increasing or decreasing), we demonstrate that the least squares estimator is nearly perfect (optimal, apart from small logarithmic factors) and can adjust to matrices with a natural internal arrangement. Lastly, for the monotonic case, we introduce an estimator that is quick to calculate and analyze its effectiveness both in theory and through experiments. Our research combines ideas from estimating data with specific shape requirements and recent studies on learning how to rearrange items, like cleaning up noisy graphs or ranking things.

original_text: Mexico City (CNN) -- Mexico's military has seized more than $15.3 million in cash from a vehicle in the border city of Tijuana, authorities said Tuesday. In addition to the money, troops also found jewelry and packages of cocaine in the vehicle, the Attorney General's Office said in a statement. Soldiers found the stash during patrols Friday, the statement said. Authorities believe the vehicle was heading to a home used as a financial hub by the Sinaloa Cartel, the statement said. On Tuesday, officials showed reporters in Mexico City stacks of $100 bills that they said were seized in the operation. The cash is the second largest quantity of money the Mexican military has seized since President Felipe Calderon took office in December 2006, the statement said. In 2008, soldiers seized more than $26 million in Sinaloa state. Calderon announced a government crackdown on cartels shortly after taking office.

original_text: Islamabad, Pakistan (CNN) -- Explosions near Pakistan's border with Afghanistan killed a dozen people Friday, authorities said. A car bomb outside the home of a former lawmaker killed 10 and wounded 30 in Quetta, the capital of Balochistan province, said Nazir Kurt, a senior police official there. The lawmaker's son, who was the target of the attack, survived the blast, he said. Earlier, a bomb blast killed two people and wounded three in northwest Pakistan's tribal region, a senior Pakistani government official said. The bomb was planted on a road near the village of Dara Bazar in the Bajaur Agency, one of the seven districts that make up Pakistan's tribal region, said Islam Zeb, a senior administrator in the district. The bomb was remotely detonated, Zeb said. It was was not immediately clear who was targeted in the blast or who was killed. original_text: Neoplagiaulacidae is a family of mammal within the extinct order Multituberculata. Fossil remains are known from the Upper Cretaceous through to the latest Eocene/early Oligocene. Representatives have been found in North America, Europe and Asia. They are the last multituberculates known. Neoplagiaulacinae (Ameghino 1890) has been seen as a sub-family within Ptilodontidae (Cope, 1887). More recent thinking has it as a family. The family is part of the suborder of Cimolodonta within the superfamily of Ptilodontoidea. They are notable as the last known multituberculates.

This group was originally named Neoplagiaulacinae by Ameghino in 1890 and was classified as a subfamily within the Ptilodontidae family, named by Cope in 1887. However, more recent scientific understanding now recognizes it as a distinct family, Neoplagiaulacidae. Other names that have been used for this family include Ectypodidae and Ectypodontidae, both proposed by Sloan & Van Valen in 1965.

Most of the fossils discovered for this family are limited to teeth. Taxonomically, Neoplagiaulacidae is part of the suborder Cimolodonta, which itself is placed within the superfamily Ptilodontoidea.

The wounded man was airlifted to a hospital, and residents of a dozen neighboring homes were evacuated, Vacaville city spokesman Mark Mazzaferro said. We can measure how well a moving liquid mixes things. We do this by seeing how much it stops a dye from clumping up. New dye keeps coming in, and old dye keeps going out.

This mixing measure compares how much the dye changes when it only spreads slowly by itself, to how much it changes when the liquid is stirred. How well stirring works is linked to how much faster things spread out because of the stirring, compared to just spreading out slowly by themselves. This also depends on a number called the Péclet number.

People recently learned that the best mixing for a certain Péclet number also depends on where the dye comes in and goes out. So, how well a liquid mixes things, or how fast things spread in it, usually depends on where the stuff being mixed comes from and goes to.

We did computer tests with tiny particles. Our results show that how well things mix really does depend on where the dye comes in and goes out, for different Péclet numbers, in our pretend liquid.

We can measure how well a flow mixes (which means how much it improves molecular diffusion) by looking at how much it reduces the unevenness of a substance's concentration. This substance, called a 'passive scalar,' is continuously added and removed at specific points.

This 'mixing enhancement' is calculated by comparing two things: first, how much the substance's concentration varies when only molecular diffusion is mixing it; and second, how much it varies when the flow is actively stirring it. We compare these variations once the system has settled into a stable state.

This way of measuring stirring effectiveness is connected to how much 'eddy diffusivity' (mixing caused by fluid motion) improves upon simple molecular diffusion. It also depends on something called the Péclet number, which tells us how important fluid movement is compared to molecular spreading.

Scientists recently found that even at a specific Péclet number, the best possible mixing doesn't just depend on that number. It also depends on *how* and *where* the substance is added and removed (the 'structure of the sources and sinks'). This means that how efficiently a flow mixes, or its 'effective diffusivity,' usually depends on where the substance being mixed is introduced and taken away.

In this study, we used computer simulations that track individual particles to confirm this idea. Our results show, with specific numbers, how the mixing enhancement in a simplified flow depends on the Péclet number and the arrangement of the sources and sinks.

original_text: Cairo, Egypt (CNN) -- Two men with political ties to Egypt's former leader were arrested on charges related to orchestrating an assault on protesters in Tahrir Square by attackers riding horses and camels. A local prosecutor's office on Saturday identified the two as Abdel Nasser al-Jabari and Youssef Khattab -- both of whom were members of parliament and ex-President Hosni Mubarak's National Democratic Party. The assault is considered by many a turning point in the effort to topple the Mubarak regime. Mubarak's supporters -- some of whom wielded whips -- rode horses and camels and charged into the crowd of anti-government protesters in Tahrir Square last month. From Journalist Mohamed Fadel Fahmy .

This paper describes a step-by-step method for reconstructing images from CT scans that use low radiation and fewer views. This method works by minimizing how much the reconstructed image differs from the original data, while also applying a "total variation" (TV) rule to improve image quality. We developed a very fast step-by-step calculation method to solve this problem. Here's how we developed this method.

First, we transformed the original problem of finding the smallest value into a "saddle point" problem (also known as a primal-dual problem) using a mathematical technique called Lagrangian duality. Then, we applied specific step-by-step methods designed for these primal-dual problems. Second, we prepared our step-by-step calculation formula by using a "ramp filter," which is a component of the filtered backprojection (FBP) reconstruction method. We did this carefully so that the final solution to the problem remained unchanged. The resulting method looks similar to what's called an iterative FBP algorithm, and it quickly finds the exact best solution to the problem.

We found a special rule for some numbers that tell us things about a "graph." A graph is like a drawing with dots and lines. Our rule helps us guess how big or small these graph numbers can be. This rule works for different counting numbers, like 1, 2, 3, and so on.

This rule uses a special measurement called "Ollivier-Ricci curvature." This curvature tells us how much a graph bends or curves. Two people named Bauer and Jost first thought of this idea. Our rule uses the smallest possible value for this curvature on a special kind of graph called a "neighborhood graph."

When we use the number 1 in our rule, it becomes like an older rule made by someone named Ollivier. Our rule also gives a new and very good guess for the biggest graph number.

We also found that for any graph, if a certain number is big enough, our guesses are always helpful. Our guess for the smallest graph number is even better than Ollivier's old guess for many graphs. This is true for all graphs that have a certain special feature.

Neighborhood graphs always have many loops. A loop is a line that starts and ends at the same dot.

To better understand how curved neighborhood graphs are, we took a very good rule about curvature from two people named Jost and Liu. We made this rule work for graphs that can have loops. We then showed how this rule is connected to how often you see triangles and loops in different parts of the graph.

We have found a way to estimate the range of values (called the spectrum) for a special mathematical tool known as the normalized Laplace operator on a finite graph. These values, represented as $\lambda_1$ through $\lambda_{n-1}$, are always within a specific range defined by the formula: (1 - k[t])^(1/t) <= $\lambda_1$ <= ... <= $\lambda_{n-1}$ <= 1 + (1 - k[t])^(1/t), for any integer t greater than or equal to 1.

In this formula, k[t] represents the smallest possible value (a lower bound) for the Ollivier-Ricci curvature. This curvature is a way to measure "how curved" a graph is, and it's calculated on a specific type of graph called a neighborhood graph (using the notation @xmath5=g). The concept of Ollivier-Ricci curvature was first introduced by Bauer and Jost.

Specifically, when t equals 1, our estimate for the smallest eigenvalue ($\lambda_1$) matches Ollivier's original estimate. Our work also provides a new, very precise (sharp) upper limit for the largest eigenvalue ($\lambda_{n-1}$).

Furthermore, we show that for any finite graph, when k[t] is large enough, the lower bound of our estimate, (1 - k[t])^(1/t), is greater than zero. This means our estimates for the smallest and largest eigenvalues are always meaningful and useful (nontrivial). The lower estimate for the smallest eigenvalue also improves upon Ollivier's estimate for all graphs that contain loops (connections from a point to itself). By their very definition, neighborhood graphs often have many loops.

To better understand the Ollivier-Ricci curvature on neighborhood graphs, we extended a precise method for estimating curvature, originally developed by Jost and Liu. This extended method can now be used for graphs that may have loops. We then showed how this curvature relates to how often triangles and loops appear in a specific local area of the graph.

simplified_primary_school: When people buy and sell things online, it can be hard to trust others, especially if someone might cheat. A good way to build trust is by using what people say about others, instead of needing special checkers. People who want to buy things later look at what others said about past sales. If someone cheats and it's found out, they get in trouble. So, it's not smart to cheat. It's very important to make sure that smart buyers have good reasons to tell the truth. Instead of giving people money to tell the truth, we found that telling the truth can be a smart thing to do if buyers come back to buy things many times. In electronic markets where there's a risk of people cheating (moral hazard), reputation systems offer an effective way to build trust, as an alternative to official verification authorities. Future clients make their business decisions by considering feedback from past transactions; if cheating is truthfully reported, it is punished, making such behavior irrational. Therefore, it's important to ensure that rational clients have the right incentives to report honestly.

Instead of using side-payment schemes that explicitly reward truthful reports, we show that honesty can become a rational behavior when clients have a repeated presence in the market. To achieve this, we describe a mechanism that supports a stable situation (an equilibrium) where truthful feedback is obtained. We then identify the set of most efficient stable outcomes (Pareto-optimal equilibria) of the mechanism, and calculate an upper limit on the percentage of false reports the mechanism can record. An important reason for the existence of this limit is that rational clients can establish a reputation for reporting honestly.

original_text: There is also a minor planet called 666 Desdemona. Desdemona is an inner satellite of Uranus. It was discovered from the images taken by Voyager 2 on 13 January 1986, and was given the temporary designation S/1986 U 6. Desdemona is named after the wife of Othello in William Shakespeare's play Othello. It is also designated Uranus X. Desdemona belongs to Portia Group of satellites, which also includes Bianca, Cressida, Juliet, Portia, Rosalind, Cupid, Belinda and Perdita. These satellites have similar orbits and photometric properties. Other than its orbit, radius of 32 km and geometric albedo of 0.08 virtually nothing is known about Desdemona. At the Voyager 2 images Desdemona appears as an elongated object, the major axis pointing towards Uranus. The ratio of axes of Desdemona's prolate spheroid is 0.6 ± 0.3. Its surface is grey in color. Desdemona may collide with one of its neighboring moons Cressida or Juliet within the next 100 million years. It is named after a lady called Desdemona. She is a character in a play by William Shakespeare, called Othello.
It also has another name, Uranus X.
Desdemona is part of a group of moons called the Portia Group. Other moons in this group are Bianca, Cressida, Juliet, Portia, Rosalind, Cupid, Belinda, and Perdita.
These moons all travel in a similar way around Uranus. They also look similar when light shines on them.
We know how it travels around Uranus. We know it is about 32 kilometers from its middle to its edge. And we know it reflects a little bit of sunlight, about 0.08. But we know almost nothing else about Desdemona.
In the pictures from Voyager 2, Desdemona looks like a long, stretched-out shape. Its longest part points towards Uranus.
It is shaped like a rugby ball. Its length is about 0.6 times its width, but this number could be a bit different.

simplified_secondary_school:
Desdemona is an inner moon of Uranus, also known as Uranus X. (There is also a minor planet called 666 Desdemona.) This moon was discovered on January 13, 1986, from images taken by the Voyager 2 spacecraft, and was given the temporary designation S/1986 U 6. It is named after the wife of Othello in William Shakespeare's play.

Desdemona belongs to the Portia Group of moons, which also includes Bianca, Cressida, Juliet, Portia, Rosalind, Cupid, Belinda, and Perdita. These moons share similar orbits and photometric properties (how they reflect light).

Beyond its orbit, a radius of 32 kilometers, and a geometric albedo of 0.08 (meaning it reflects 8% of the sunlight that hits it), very little else is known about Desdemona. In the images taken by Voyager 2, Desdemona appears as an elongated object, with its longest side (major axis) pointing towards Uranus. Its shape is a prolate spheroid (like a rugby ball), with the ratio of its axes being 0.6 ± 0.3. Its surface is grey in color. Scientists predict Desdemona may collide with one of its neighboring moons, Cressida or Juliet, within the next 100 million years.

We looked very carefully at the special math parts of this idea when the space has a certain size. We found out that one part of the math for this idea tells us about the energy of a certain state. Another part of the math tells us how fast that state breaks apart or forms.

This new way of thinking lets us, for the very first time, perfectly describe how gas, liquid, and mixed forms of this idea look in a certain space. simplified_secondary_school: We present a precise mathematical solution for a simpler version of the statistical multifragmentation model. This model has a rule: the largest piece formed cannot be bigger than the total space of the system. We thoroughly analyzed the unusual points (singularities) in the isobaric partition function for this model, specifically when the system has a limited size. We show that the real part of any simple pole in the isobaric partition function gives the free energy of a specific state. Its imaginary part, depending on its sign, tells us the inverse of how long it takes for that state to decay or form. Our new mathematical framework allows us, for the first time, to precisely define the finite-volume versions of gaseous, liquid, and mixed phases within this model. We do this using the fundamental rules of statistical mechanics, and in doing so, we highlight errors in previous studies. We also discuss how the system's limited size affects large fragments, and the role of metastable (unstable) states. 25.70.pq, 21.65.+f, 24.10.pa

original_text: (CNN) -- The World Health Organization announced Tuesday it is still considering increasing its pandemic alert level to phase 6 because of growing worldwide cases of the H1N1 virus, or swine flu. WHO considers raising the pandemic alert level to 6 as cases of H1N1 increase worldwide. "Globally, we are at phase 5, but we are nearing phase 6," said Dr. Keiji Fukuda, WHO's Assistant Director General. "As this continues to spread internationally, some countries are moving from isolated to sustained community spread." Phase 6 is a declaration that many member countries have long feared could mean economic disaster. It is the highest on the WHO's pandemic alert system, and is described by the organization as a global pandemic. Fukuda was quick to remind journalists that the designation does not reflect the severity of the disease, but how widespread it is. "Our overall assessment of severity is moderate," he said, "because although the overall number of serious and fatal cases is relatively limited ... we really don't have a full handle on the number of people with serious illness." Fukuda said nearly 19,000 cases of the H1N1 virus have been reported in 64 countries, resulting in 117 deaths.

Phase 6 is the highest level in the WHO's pandemic alert system and is described by the organization as a global pandemic. Many member countries have long feared that reaching this phase could lead to economic disaster.

So far, nearly 19,000 cases of the H1N1 virus have been reported in 64 countries, resulting in 117 deaths.

original_text: Prionessus is a genus of extinct mammal from the Paleocene of Central Asia. It was a member of the extinct order Multituberculata within the suborder Cimolodonta and superfamily Taeniolabidoidea. The genus was named by Matthew W.D. and Granger W. in 1925 and is based on a single species. The species Prionessus lucifer was named by Matthew W.D. and Granger W. in 1925. Fossil remains have been found in the Late Paleocene Nomogen and Khashat Formations of Gashato, Naran and Nomogen in Bayan Ulan of Mongolia and China. References Further reading Matthew and Granger (1925), "Fauna and Correlation of the Gashato Formation of Mongolia". Am. Museum Novitates 189, p. 1-12. Kielan-Jaworowska Z. and Hurum J.H. (2001), "Phylogeny and Systematics of Multituberculate Mammals". Paleontology 44, p. 389-429. Much of this information has been derived from MESOZOIC MAMMALS: Eucosmodontidae, Microcosmodontidae and Taeniolabidoidea, an Internet directory Cimolodonts Paleocene mammals Paleocene genus extinctions Paleogene mammals of Asia Prehistoric mammal genera

It was part of a group called Multituberculata. This group is also not alive now. It was also part of smaller groups. These groups were called Cimolodonta and Taeniolabidoidea.

Two people named Matthew and Granger gave it this name. They named it in 1925. There is only one kind of Prionessus. This one kind is called Prionessus lucifer. Matthew and Granger named it in 1925 too.

We found its old bones. We found them in places like Gashato, Naran, and Nomogen. These places are in Mongolia and China.

Prionessus was a type of animal that lived a very long time ago. It was a mammal, like us, but it is not alive anymore. It lived in a place called Central Asia during a time called the Paleocene.

It belonged to a big group of animals called Multituberculata. These animals are all extinct now. It also belonged to smaller groups called Cimolodonta and Taeniolabidoidea.

Two people, Matthew W.D. and Granger W., gave the name Prionessus to this group of animals in 1925. They found only one kind of animal in this group. They named that specific animal Prionessus lucifer in 1925 too.

Scientists found old bones of Prionessus lucifer. These bones were found in rock layers called Nomogen and Khashat Formations. These places are in Mongolia and China, in areas like Gashato, Naran, Nomogen, and Bayan Ulan. This was during the later part of the Paleocene time.
simplified_secondary_school:
*Prionessus* is a genus of extinct mammal that lived in Central Asia during the Paleocene epoch. It was part of the extinct order Multituberculata, belonging to the suborder Cimolodonta and the superfamily Taeniolabidoidea.

The genus *Prionessus*, along with its only known species, *Prionessus lucifer*, was named in 1925 by Matthew W.D. and Granger W.

Fossil remains have been found in rock layers, known as the Nomogen and Khashat Formations, which date to the Late Paleocene period. These formations are located in the Gashato, Naran, and Nomogen areas of Bayan Ulan, spanning parts of Mongolia and China.

original_text: in magnets with non - collinear spin configuration the expectation value of the conventionally defined spin current operator contains a contribution which renormalizes an external magnetic field and hence affects only the precessional motion of the spin polarization . this term , which has been named angular spin current by sun and xie [ phys . rev b * 72 * , 245305 ( 2005 ) ] , does not describe the translational motion of magnetic moments . we give a prescription how to separate these two types of spin transport and show that the translational movement of the spin is always polarized along the direction of the local magnetization . we also show that at vanishing temperature the classical magnetic order parameter in magnetic insulators can not carry a translational spin current , and elucidate how this affects the interpretation of spin supercurrents . = 10000
simplified_kindergarten: Magnets have tiny parts. We call them spins. Sometimes spins point in different ways. We see how these spins move. This movement changes how an outside magnet works. It makes the spins wobble. It does not make them move forward. This is called "angular spin current." We show how to tell wobbling from moving. When spins move, they always go the same way as the magnet's own pull. When it is very, very cold, some magnets cannot make spins move. This helps us learn about special spin flows.
simplified_primary_school: In magnets where the tiny internal magnets (spins) do not all point in the same direction, a measurement of "spin current" shows something special. This special part changes how an outside magnetic field works. It only makes the spins wobble or spin around, like a top. It does not make them move from one place to another. Scientists Sun and Xie called this special part "angular spin current." It does not show spins moving from one place to another. We show how to tell the difference between these two ways spins can act. We also show that when spins move from place to place, they always line up with the direction of the magnet they are in. We also show that when it is extremely cold, the way magnets are ordered in special materials (magnetic insulators) cannot make spins move from place to place. We explain how this changes what we understand about "spin supercurrents."
simplified_secondary_school: In magnets where the spins (tiny magnetic directions) are not all lined up, the standard calculation of spin current includes a part that effectively changes an external magnetic field. This part only affects the wobbling motion of the overall spin direction, not its physical movement. This specific component, named 'angular spin current' by Sun and Xie in 2005, does not describe magnetic moments (spins) moving from one place to another. We offer a way to separate these two types of spin movement. We show that when spins do move translationally, their movement is always aligned with the direction of the local magnetism. We also demonstrate that at extremely low temperatures, the overall magnetic arrangement in magnetic insulators cannot carry a translational spin current. We then explain how this affects how we understand 'spin supercurrents' (spin currents that flow without resistance).

These excitements don't spread out, and they come from places that don't send out waves. Outside forces can hold these excitements in one spot. They can also help us guide these excitements as they move. But we must guide them slower than the waves of the outside force spread. I show how to make many examples of these. This includes excitements that don't spread out and only last a short time. It also includes excitements that go in one direction, and ones that seem to spread out very slowly. External fields can be used to trap these excitations in a specific location. These fields can also dynamically guide or 'steer' such excitations, provided the steering is slower than the speed at which the field's own waves propagate. I present clear examples of how to create these situations, including excitations that don't travel and only last for a limited time, excitations that move in a specific direction, and excitations that appear to move slower than their actual speed. Through these dynamic lattice constructions, I show that for non-propagating excitations to occur, they do not necessarily need to constantly oscillate over time or remain fixed in one spot.

original_text: The Crossosomatales are an order, first recognized as such by APG II. They are flowering plants included within the Rosid eudicots. Description Species assigned to the Crossosomatales have in common flowers that are positioned solitarily, with the base of the calyx, corolla, and stamens fused into a tube-shaped floral cup, sepals overlapping, the outermost smaller than the inner. Insides of the casings of pollen grains have horizontally extended thin regions (or endo-apertures). The gynoecium is placed on a short stalk, papillae on the stigma consist of two or more cells, ovary locules taper upwards, and the protective cell layer (or integument) surrounding the ovule leaves a zigzag opening (or micropyle). Some cell clusters have bundles of long yellow crystals, mucilage cells are present, and seeds have a smooth, woody coating. Taxonomy The relationships between orders within the Malvid clade, according to the APG system, is represented by the following tree. Within the Crossosomatales, the APG III system of 2009 recognises families represented in the following tree. References Angiosperm Phylogeny website Angiosperm orders

Inside the tiny covers of pollen, there are thin, flat parts.

The female part of the flower sits on a short stem. The tiny bumps on top of it have two or more cells. The little rooms inside the ovary get smaller at the top. The cover around the baby seed has a zigzag hole.

Some cells have long yellow crystals. There are sticky cells. The seeds have a smooth, hard cover.

How these plants are grouped is shown in a family tree. A group called APG III in 2009 showed how the families inside Crossosomatales are grouped in another tree.

All plants in this group have flowers that grow alone. The bottom parts of the flower – the sepals, petals, and pollen parts – are joined together to make a cup shape. The sepals overlap, and the outside ones are smaller than the inside ones. Inside the little cases that hold pollen, there are thin, flat parts that go sideways. The female part of the flower sits on a short stem. The tiny bumps on the top of the female part are made of two or more cells. The little rooms inside the part that holds seeds get narrower at the top. The skin that protects the baby seed has a zigzag hole. Some groups of cells have many long, yellow crystals. They also have cells that make a sticky goo. Their seeds have a smooth, hard cover like wood.

How these plants are related to other groups, like the Malvid group, is shown in a family tree picture by a system called APG. Inside the Crossosomatales group, another system called APG III (from 2009) names even smaller groups, which are also shown in a family tree picture.
simplified_secondary_school:
The Crossosomatales are an order of flowering plants. They were first identified as a distinct group by APG II and are part of the larger Rosid eudicots.

These plants have flowers that grow individually (solitarily). The base of their sepals (calyx), petals (corolla), and pollen-producing parts (stamens) are joined together to form a tube-shaped cup. The sepals overlap, with the outer ones being smaller than the inner ones. Inside the outer covering of their pollen grains, there are thin regions that extend horizontally, called endo-apertures.

The female reproductive part (gynoecium) sits on a short stalk. The tiny bumps (papillae) on the stigma are made of two or more cells. The compartments within the ovary (ovary locules) narrow towards the top. Also, the protective layer around the ovule, called the integument, has a zigzag-shaped opening known as a micropyle.

Some groups of cells contain bundles of long yellow crystals, and the plants also have mucilage cells. Their seeds are covered by a smooth, woody coating.

The APG system describes how the Crossosomatales relate to other orders within the Malvid group. In 2009, the APG III system further identified the specific families that belong within the Crossosomatales.

original_text: Seven Mile Beach is a national park in New South Wales (Australia), 110 km southwest of Sydney. It consists of tidal flats, islands and a coastal sand barrier built from river silt in the Shoalhaven River delta. It is important areas for migratory waders and sea birds and protects a large littoral rainforest. The wettest month of the year is March, and the driest is September. The average temperature ranges from 17 to 25 degrees Celsius. There are great opportunities for bird watchers. They can observe king parrots, rainbow lorikeets, rosellas, wrens, New Holland honeyeaters and brown cuckoo doves. See also Protected areas of New South Wales References External links Seven Mile Beach National Park at NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service website National parks of New South Wales Protected areas established in 1971 1971 establishments in Australia

People can see many birds here. They can see king parrots. They can see rainbow lorikeets. They can see rosellas. They can see wrens. They can see New Holland honeyeaters. They can see brown cuckoo doves.

simplified_secondary_school: This study examines the Standard Model of particle physics, but with the addition of 'right-handed neutrinos.' However, if the right-handed neutrino we observe is the lightest one, its effect on the masses of the known neutrinos must be balanced out by the influence of a heavier right-handed neutrino. Each of these waves follows one of the math rules. We also find similar math rules when we only look at waves moving in one specific direction and try to find answers for waves moving only one way. We investigate how well various single-equation models describe the movement of internal waves in a system with two fluids. These models, which are simplified approximations (called asymptotic models), include Inviscid Burgers, Korteweg-de Vries (KdV), extended KdV, and more complex Camassa-Holm type equations.

These single-equation models can be shown to be valid in two ways. The first method simplifies the fluid's movement by imagining it as two separate waves moving in opposite directions, with each wave following one of these equations. The second method focuses on waves moving in only one direction and seeks approximate solutions that travel that way. This approach also leads to similar equations. While this second method is more limited in the types of starting conditions it can handle, it provides more accurate results.

We also present several new approximate models where the equations are linked together. These include a Green-Naghdi type model, a simpler version of it for the Camassa-Holm regime, and a model where the parts are only slightly linked. All of these models are rigorously proven to be consistent, meaning they reliably reflect the physical phenomena they describe.

original_text: New York (CNN) -- Two men have been arrested in the death of a popular television and radio disc jockey who was shot to death on Staten Island early Sunday morning, according to New York City police. Corey McGriff, known to fans as DJ "Megatron," died of a gunshot wound to the torso near his home shortly after 2 a.m. March 27. William Williams, 21, and Richard Cromwell, 20, both of Staten Island, were arrested Wednesday and charged with murder, robbery, and criminal possession of a weapon. Williams and Cromwell are expected to be arraigned Thursday according to Peter Spencer, spokesman for the Staten Island District Attorney. McGriff, 32, was best known for his appearances on the BET's music countdown show, "106 & Park." He had worked for both the show and BET.com since 2006, according to a statement released by the network. "The BET Networks Family is saddened by the news of the untimely loss of our friend and colleague, Corey 'Megatron' McGriff," the statement said. "We extend our deepest condolence to his family. He will truly be missed." CNN's Leigh Remizowski contributed to this report.

The DJ's name was Corey McGriff. People knew him as DJ "Megatron." He died from being shot in his body, near his home. This happened a little after 2 in the morning on March 27.

The two men who were caught are William Williams, who is 21, and Richard Cromwell, who is 20. Both live on Staten Island. They were caught on Wednesday. They are accused of killing someone, stealing, and having a weapon they shouldn't have.

A man named Peter Spencer, who speaks for the Staten Island District Attorney, said that Williams and Cromwell will go to court on Thursday. This is called an arraignment.

Mr. McGriff was 32 years old. Many people knew him from a music show called "106 & Park" on BET. He worked for that show and BET.com since the year 2006. BET said this in a message.

The message from BET said they were very sad that their friend and coworker, Corey 'Megatron' McGriff, died too soon. They sent their sadness to his family. They said they will truly miss him.

Leigh Remizowski from CNN helped with this story.
simplified_secondary_school:
New York City police have arrested two men in connection with the death of Corey McGriff, a popular television and radio disc jockey known to fans as DJ "Megatron."

McGriff, 32, died from a gunshot wound to the torso near his Staten Island home shortly after 2 a.m. on Sunday, March 27.

William Williams, 21, and Richard Cromwell, 20, both from Staten Island, were arrested on Wednesday. They have been charged with murder, robbery, and criminal possession of a weapon.

According to Peter Spencer, a spokesman for the Staten Island District Attorney, Williams and Cromwell are expected to be arraigned on Thursday.

McGriff was best known for his appearances on BET's music countdown show, "106 & Park." He had worked for both the show and BET.com since 2006, according to a statement released by the network.

BET Networks released a statement saying, "The BET Networks Family is saddened by the news of the untimely loss of our friend and colleague, Corey 'Megatron' McGriff. We extend our deepest condolence to his family. He will truly be missed."

simplified_secondary_school: In this study, we investigate a specific type of black hole, known as a regular Bardeen black hole, and how it behaves as a gravitational lens. We determine the extreme bending of light (called the 'strong deflection limit' for the 'deflection angle') that occurs when light passes very close to this black hole. As a real-world example, we apply our findings to the supermassive black hole located at the center of our own galaxy.

We look at little paths. They move and change. We see how many are in one spot. We use a big machine to see them. We compare them to other paths.

We look at each thing that happens. We look at them from two ways.

We look at the things that happen. We look at 'plus' paths. We look at 'minus' paths. We also look at both kinds of paths.

Something new happens. This is when gold bumps into gold very fast. It happens when more things happen. Things far apart are connected. Or they do not move as much. We see this when paths are the same kind.

This is like something we thought would happen. It is from something called 'color glass condensate'.
simplified_primary_school:
We look at how tiny particle paths wiggle and change inside a big machine called STAR TPC. We use a special math tool to do this. This tool compares what we see to a mix of other events. We look at each event in two directions, like up-down and around.

Computer models show that streams of particles make signs we can see. This happens when we use our special way of looking at the wiggles, even for small streams. We look at many different numbers of particles in an event. We see how the signs change based on how the event started. We look at the shapes of these events. We check paths of particles that are positive, negative, and both together.

Something new happens when gold atoms crash into each other at a certain energy. When there are more particles, particles that are far apart seem connected, or wiggle less than expected. We see this when we look at particles with the same charge. This looks like something scientists thought might happen with a special kind of matter called "color glass condensate."
simplified_secondary_school:
Scientists are studying the changing patterns in how crowded unidentified hadron tracks are in specific areas of the STAR TPC detector. They use a special mathematical tool called the discrete wavelet transform power spectrum technique, which compares these patterns to a reference created from mixed events. This analysis looks at each individual event in two dimensions, using coordinates called pseudo-rapidity (η) and azimuthal angle (φ) to describe the particle directions.

Computer simulations, specifically HIJING, suggest that when this dynamic texture analysis is used, particle jets and smaller mini-jets produce signals that can be seen even without needing to select for high transverse momentum (pT). By examining events with a wide range of particle numbers (multiplicities), we investigate how these signals depend on the initial conditions of the collisions. We study the patterns within these events separately for positively charged tracks, negatively charged tracks, and then for all tracks combined.

In collisions of gold nuclei (AuAu) at 200 GeV, as the number of particles produced increases, we observe a change in behavior: a long-range correlation in pseudo-rapidity (η) is seen among tracks of the same charge. This correlation means that particles far apart in pseudo-rapidity are connected, or that there are fewer fluctuations than expected when compared to mixed events. This observed effect is similar in nature to one of the predicted signs of a theoretical state of matter called the Color Glass Condensate.

original_text: The year 664 BC was a year of the pre-Julian Roman calendar. In the Roman Empire, it was known as year 90 Ab urbe condita . The denomination 664 BC for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the prevalent method in Europe for naming years. Events First naval battle in Greek recorded history, between Corinth and Corcyra. Tantamani succeeds his uncle Taharqa as king of Kush. Kushites invade Assyrian-controlled Egypt. The Assyrians under Ashurbanipal capture and sack Thebes, Egypt. Psamtik I succeeds Necho I as ruler of Lower Egypt. The Sphinx of Taharqa is completed, having been begun in 690 BC. The monument is now displayed at the British Museum, London. Births Amon, king of Judah (approximate date) Deaths Taharqa, king of Egypt Necho I, king of Egypt Duke Xuan of Qin, ruler of the state of Qin References Ancient Corcyra 660s BC

Big boats fought for the first time. Corinth and Corcyra fought.
Tantamani became king of Kush. He took over from his uncle Taharqa.
Kush people went into Egypt. Assyrian people were in charge there.
The Assyrian people took Thebes. Ashurbanipal was their leader. They hurt the city.
Psamtik I became leader of Lower Egypt. He took over from Necho I.
Taharqa's Sphinx was finished. It was started in 690 BC.
You can see it now in a museum. It is in London.

Amon was born. He was a king. We think it was around this time.

Taharqa died. He was a king in Egypt.
Necho I died. He was a king in Egypt.
Duke Xuan of Qin died. He was a leader in Qin.
simplified_primary_school:
The year 664 BC was a very old year. In the Roman Empire, people called it year 90. This meant it was 90 years after the city of Rome was built. We call this year 664 BC today. People started using "BC" to name years a long, long time ago, when it became the main way to name years in Europe.

This year, the first sea battle in Greek history that we know about happened. It was between two places called Corinth and Corcyra.

Tantamani became the new king of Kush. He took over from his uncle, Taharqa. People from Kush attacked Egypt, where the Assyrians were in charge. The Assyrians, led by Ashurbanipal, took over the city of Thebes in Egypt and damaged it.

Psamtik I became the new ruler of Lower Egypt. He took over from Necho I.

A big statue called the Sphinx of Taharqa was finished this year. It was started a long time before, in 690 BC. Today, you can see this statue in the British Museum in London.

A boy named Amon was born around this time. He would later become the king of Judah.

These people died this year: Taharqa, who was a king of Egypt. Necho I, who was also a king of Egypt. Duke Xuan of Qin, who was a leader in a place called Qin.
simplified_secondary_school:
The year we call 664 BC was known in the Roman Empire as year 90 Ab urbe condita, meaning 'from the founding of the city.' The system of naming years as 'BC' (Before Christ) became common in Europe during the early medieval period.

During 664 BC, several important events took place:
*   The first naval battle recorded in Greek history occurred between the cities of Corinth and Corcyra.
*   Tantamani became the new king of Kush, succeeding his uncle Taharqa.
*   Kushite forces invaded parts of Egypt that were controlled by the Assyrians.
*   The Assyrians, led by King Ashurbanipal, captured and looted the Egyptian city of Thebes.
*   Psamtik I took over as the ruler of Lower Egypt, following Necho I.
*   The Sphinx of Taharqa, a monument that had been started in 690 BC, was completed. This monument is now displayed at the British Museum in London.

Around this time, Amon, who would later become king of Judah, was born.

Notable deaths in 664 BC included:
*   Taharqa, the king of Egypt.
*   Necho I, also a king of Egypt.
*   Duke Xuan of Qin, who ruled the state of Qin.

original_text: The Brazilian cycling federation says it has suspended national road race champion Marcia Fernandes for two years for doping, virtually ending her chances of competing in the 2016 Rio Olympics. The federation says the 23-year-old Fernandes, who's also a member of Spain's Bizkaia-Durango cycling team, tested positive for EPO at the Brazilian championships in June. Also suspended for failing doping tests at the event were Brazil's under-23 national road race champion, Nayara Gomes Ramos. Marcia Fernandes is unlikely to take part in her home Olympics at Rio 2016, whose stadium is pictured, after testing positive for EPO and having been banned for two years by the Brazilian cycling federation . Two other cyclists, Juliana Jacobs Renner and Patrick Gabriel Oyakaua, were suspended as well. The federation revealed on Thursday that none of the athletes requested to have their 'B' samples tested.

original_text: Thematic role is a linguistic notion, which may refer to: Theta role (in syntax or at the syntax-semantics interface), the formal device for representing syntactic argument structure—the number and type of noun phrases—required syntactically by a particular verb Thematic relation (in semantics), a term to express the role that a noun phrase plays with respect to the action or state described by a governing verb

One thing is "Theta role."
This is about how words go together.
It shows how many naming words a doing word needs.
It shows what kind of naming words it needs.
The doing word needs them to make a good sentence.

simplified_primary_school:
"Thematic role" is an idea in language that can mean two things.

One meaning is "Theta role." This is a special way to show what words a verb needs to build a sentence. It tells us how many naming words (like "the boy" or "a ball") a verb needs, and what kind they should be. This is about how sentences are put together correctly.

The other meaning is "Thematic relation." This is a word to show what job a naming word (like "the boy") does for the action or feeling that the verb talks about. This is about what the words mean.
simplified_secondary_school:
"Thematic role" is a concept in linguistics that can refer to two related ideas:

1.  A **Theta role** is a formal tool used in syntax (the study of sentence structure) or at the point where syntax meets semantics (meaning). It represents a verb's *syntactic argument structure*, which means it identifies the specific number and types of noun phrases that a particular verb grammatically requires in a sentence.

2.  A **Thematic relation** is a term used in semantics (the study of meaning). It describes the specific role a noun phrase plays in relation to the action or state that a governing verb describes.

original_text: Mount Frankland National Park is a national park in the South West region of Western Australia, south of Perth. The park is part of the larger Walpole Wilderness Area that was established in 2004, an international biodiversity hotspot. Geography It covers the low granite hills to the north of the town of Walpole and is covered largely by forests of karri (Eucalyptus diversicolor) and red tingle (Eucalyptus jacksonii), two of the world's largest trees. The three tingle species are unique to the area between the park and the coast and the only eucalypts to be buttressed, a feature which reflects the moist conditions prevailing within the park. On exposed areas where continuous rain washes away soil, a low heathland is the dominant vegetation. Annual rainfall at Walpole is around . On the 422-metre high peak of Mount Frankland, though no rain gauge has ever been installed, annual rainfall is probably around . Most rain falls between May and August, but unlike drier parts of southwestern Australia, showers are not infrequent even during the summer. See also Protected areas of Western Australia References National parks of Western Australia Protected areas established in 1988

The park has low hills. The hills are made of rock. They are north of Walpole town. Big trees grow there. They are called karri trees. They are called red tingle trees. These trees are very big. They are some of the biggest trees in the world.

There are three kinds of tingle trees. They only grow near the park and the sea. Their trunks are wide at the bottom. No other gum trees are like this. This shows it is very wet in the park.

Some places are open. Rain falls a lot there. It washes the dirt away. Small plants grow there. They are called heathland plants.

A lot of rain falls in Walpole each year.

Mount Frankland is a tall hill. It is 422 meters high. No one has measured the rain there. But even more rain likely falls there each year.

Most rain comes from May to August. Other places in the South West are dry then. But it still rains often in the park, even in summer.

original_text: (CNN) -- An actor escaped death in front of an Austrian theater audience after slashing his throat in a bizarre on-stage mix-up. File image of Daniel Hoevels on stage in Salzburg . Daniel Hoevels, 30, collapsed on stage at Vienna's Burgtheater last weekend with blood pouring from his neck after cutting his throat with what was supposed to be a blunt knife, media reports said Thursday. As he fell injured the audience started to applaud, oblivious to the fact that Hoevels was really hurt, Britain's Telegraph newspaper reported. The actor was rushed to hospital with a knife wound that had narrowly missed his main artery. Fortunately Hoevels recovered after treatment and appeared on stage the following night with a bandage around his neck, Austrian media reports said. Ironically, Hoevels' character was supposed to commit suicide in the stage production of Friedrich Schiller's Mary Stuart, about Mary Queen of Scots. Austrian police are now investigating whether the incident was a mistake or a deliberate attack on the actor.

The knife was supposed to be safe. But it was sharp.
Daniel cut his neck by mistake. Blood came out.
He fell down on the stage.
The people watching clapped. They did not know Daniel was really hurt.
They took Daniel to the hospital fast.
The cut was very close to a big part in his neck.
But Daniel got better. He was lucky.
The next night, Daniel was on stage again. He had a bandage on his neck.
In the play, Daniel's part was supposed to die. It was a strange thing.
Police are looking into what happened. Was it a mistake? Or did someone try to hurt Daniel?

Luckily, Daniel got better after doctors helped him. The very next night, he was back on stage. He had a bandage around his neck. News in Austria said this.

It was strange because the person Daniel was playing in the play was supposed to kill himself. The play was called 'Mary Stuart' and was about a queen named Mary.

Police in Austria are now looking into it. They want to know if it was a mistake, or if someone meant to hurt the actor.

original_text: The Teenage Liberation Handbook: How to Quit School and Get a Real Life and Education, which was published in 1991 by Grace Llewellyn, is a book about unschooling and youth empowerment. Largely inspired by John Holt's educational philosophy, the book encourages teenagers to leave full-time school and to allow their curiosity about the world to guide their learning. It includes suggestions and resources regarding traditional academic areas, as well as chapters about talking to parents, social life, college, and exploring the world. The OSSE information showed a special light pattern. This pattern went up very high. It did not stop or change. We think this light means there are many fast, hot bits in that place.

The light patterns cannot be explained by just heat. They cannot be explained by just things moving. They are explained by a mix of heat and fast, hot bits.

The information from _ osse_ showed how the light's brightness changed with its energy. It showed a smooth pattern that went up to a certain energy level called @xmath0 kev, without any sudden stops or changes.

We think this light strongly shows that there are special fast-moving tiny bits called non-thermal electrons in that place.

The wide range of light patterns cannot be explained by two ideas about how light gets its energy, called thermal or bulk-motion. But they are well explained by light getting its energy in a mix of hot gas and gas with fast-moving parts.
simplified_secondary_school: The object GRS 1915+105 was observed by the CGRO/OSSE instrument nine times between 1995 and 2000. Eight of these observations happened at the same time as observations by the RXTE instrument. We analyzed all the data from OSSE, as well as two combined RXTE-OSSE measurements that showed the lowest and highest X-ray brightness. The OSSE data revealed a spectrum that follows a power-law pattern, reaching up to 600 keV without any sudden break or drop in intensity. We believe this emission strongly suggests that the source contains non-thermal electrons. The wide-ranging spectra cannot be explained by either thermal Comptonization (where photons scatter off hot electrons) or bulk-motion Comptonization (where photons scatter off electrons moving in a flow). Instead, they are accurately explained by Comptonization occurring in hybrid plasmas, which contain both thermal and non-thermal electrons.

original_text: (CNN) -- The Fairfax County, Virginia, health department is investigating a tuberculosis outbreak at an area school after three people tested positive for the potentially fatal bacteria, officials said Thursday. The health department has contacted 430 people who may have been exposed and need to be tested as a result of exposure to the three, the officials said. Health officials declined to say whether the three were students or teachers. They also said they did not know what strain of TB the three contracted. The first case of tuberculosis at Robert E. Lee High School was diagnosed in December 2012, and the health department opened and completed an investigation, the agency said. But because two more people from the same school were diagnosed with it in June, the department re-opened the December case and is investigating whether they are related. TB bacteria usually attack the lungs, but they can also attack other parts of the body such as the kidneys, spine and brain, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. If not treated properly, TB can be fatal.

original_text: (CNN) -- Eight people were injured in an explosion at a naval weapons station's boathouse in New Jersey Tuesday morning, the U.S. Navy said. The blast at Naval Weapons Station Earle happened at about 9 a.m. while personnel were performing maintenance on a vessel, the Navy said in a news release. Seven sailors and a Department of Defense civilian firefighter were injured. Though all eight were taken to hospitals, none had life-threatening injuries. By Tuesday night, the Navy said seven of the eight had been treated and released after suffering minor injuries, mostly from smoke inhalation. One sailor remained hospitalized and underwent surgery for a broken arm, the Navy said. "The situation is stabilized. We are investigating the accident," Navy spokesman Mike Brady said. Information on what exploded wasn't immediately available. Authorities are investigating what caused the blast, the Navy said. The workers' names were not immediately released. "The damage from the explosion was contained within the boathouse area," the Navy's release said. Naval Weapons Station Earle, which supplies ordnance to the Navy's Atlantic fleet carrier and other groups, is south of Middletown, New Jersey. CNN's Chris Lawrence contributed to this report.

original_text: The coroner who conducted the inquest into the death of Amy Winehouse has resigned, raising the possibility that the case could be reopened. Assistant Deputy Coroner Suzanne Greenaway ruled in October that Winehouse, who was found dead on July 23rd, died of accidental alcohol poisoning. Greenaway resigned in November after officials discovered that she had not been a registered lawyer in the United Kingdom for the required five years. Her resignation was not made public until today. Adding to the intrigue is the fact that Greenaway was appointed to her post by her husband, Andrew Reid, who serves as a London coroner himself. In a statement, Reid said that he was under the impression that his wife's tenure as a registered lawyer in her native Australia qualified her for the post. simplified_secondary_school:
ECM can refer to various terms across different fields:

*   In **business, economics, and management**, ECM can mean:
    *   **Equity capital markets:** Financial markets where companies raise money by selling shares.
    *   **Enterprise content management:** Systems for managing a company's information and documents.
    *   **Engineering change management:** The process of managing changes to products or systems in engineering.
    *   **European Common Market:** An earlier name for the European Economic Community, focused on economic integration.
    *   **European Championships Management:** The organization that manages European sports championships.

*   In **science and medicine**, ECM can refer to:
    *   **Ectomycorrhiza:** A type of symbiotic relationship between fungi and plant roots.
    *   **Electron cloud model:** A model describing the probable locations of electrons around an atom's nucleus.
    *   **Engineered Cellular Magmatics:** A specific term in materials science or geology.
    *   **Erythema chronicum migrans:** A skin rash often associated with Lyme disease.
    *   **Extracellular matrix:** The network of molecules outside cells that provides support and structure.

*   In **mathematics and computing**, ECM can stand for:
    *   **Elliptic curve method:** A method used in number theory and cryptography.
    *   **Error correction model:** An econometric model used to analyze long-term relationships between variables.
    *   **Error correction mode:** A feature in technology that detects and corrects data errors.
    *   **European Congress of Mathematics:** A major conference for mathematicians in Europe.

*   In **technology and engineering**, ECM can mean:
    *   **Electrochemical machining:** A method of removing metal using an electrochemical process.
    *   **Electronic contract manufacturing:** Companies that manufacture electronic products for other businesses.
    *   **Electronic countermeasure:** Technology used to disrupt or deceive enemy electronic systems.
    *   **Electronically commutated motor:** A type of electric motor that uses electronic controls.
    *   **Energy conservation measure:** Actions taken to reduce energy consumption.
    *   **Engine control module:** The computer that controls an engine's performance.

*   For **other specific organizations and concepts**, ECM can refer to:
    *   **Editio Critica Maior:** A critical edition of the Greek New Testament.
    *   **ECM Records:** A record label known for jazz and classical music.
    *   **ECM Real Estate Investments:** A defunct real estate developer based in Luxembourg.
    *   **Edinburgh City Mission:** A Christian organization in Scotland.
    *   **Elektrani na Severna Makedonija:** A power company in North Macedonia.
    *   **Episcopal Conference of Malawi:** A body of Catholic bishops in Malawi.
    *   **Every Child Matters:** A former UK Government initiative focused on children's well-being.
    *   **Every Child Ministries:** A Christian charity supporting children in Africa.
    *   **Exceptional case-marking:** A concept in linguistics related to how grammatical cases are assigned.

original_text: The Zeta (Serbian Cyrillic: Зета, ) is a river in Montenegro. Its source is under the mountain of Vojnik. The river flows eastwards for until it empties into the river Morača just north of Podgorica. Its drainage basin area is . Geography One theory is that the name "Zeta" derives from an early Greek "Zenta", later romanized in "Senta". The Zeta River is the most significant tributary of the Morača river. The Perućica hydroelectric power plant near Nikšić (307 MW, 970 GWh) uses the waters of the Zeta River to generate power. After that, the river meanders through the Bjelopavlići Valley and Danilovgrad Municipality, until it empties into the Morača a few miles north of Capital City of Podgorica. Historical monumental Carev Most (the Tsar's Bridge) exists over Zeta to the south of Nikšić town, and the ancient castle of Pandurica is situated nearby. References Rivers of Montenegro Morača Zeta (geographical region)

There is a big power plant near Nikšić. It is called Perućica.
It uses the Zeta river's water to make power.

The river winds around. It goes through a valley called Bjelopavlići. It also goes through Danilovgrad.
Then it goes into the Morača river again, near Podgorica.

There is an old bridge over the Zeta river. It is called Carev Most. It is near Nikšić.
There is also an old castle nearby. It is called Pandurica.

After the power plant, the river winds through the Bjelopavlići Valley and the Danilovgrad area. Then it flows into the Morača river again, a few miles north of Podgorica, the main city.

simplified_secondary_school:
The Zeta (Serbian Cyrillic: Зета) is a river in Montenegro. It begins under the mountain of Vojnik and flows eastwards.

One theory suggests the name "Zeta" comes from the early Greek "Zenta," which was later Romanized to "Senta."

The Zeta River is the most significant tributary of the Morača River. Its waters are used by the Perućica hydroelectric power plant near Nikšić, which generates 307 MW and 970 GWh of power.

After passing the power plant, the river meanders through the Bjelopavlići Valley and Danilovgrad Municipality. It eventually empties into the Morača River a few miles north of Podgorica, the capital city.

Along the Zeta River, south of Nikšić, the historic Carev Most (Tsar's Bridge) crosses the river, and nearby is the ancient castle of Pandurica.

original_text: (CNN) -- The smell of smoke was reported in a plane that landed at California's Oakland International Airport late Wednesday, officials said. SkyWest Flight 4454 arrived from Los Angeles International Airport on time, landed and taxied to the gate normally. There were 75 passengers and 4 crew members on board the CRJ 900 aircraft at the time. The reported smell of smoke was noticed shortly after landing, said Delta Air Lines spokesman Russell Cason. SkyWest is a Delta subsidiary. There were no injuries or damage reported, and passengers disembarked at the gate, airport spokesman Scott Witner said. Authorities could not find the source of the smoke, and fire officials have left the scene, he said. CNN's Maria White contributed to this report .

original_text: By . Oliver Todd . Follow @@oliver_todd . FIFA have fined the Algerian FA £33,000 (50,000 Swiss francs) after fans set off smoke bombs and flares in their group stage game against Russia. The governing body announced the fine on Monday, saying the Algerian FA 'has been held liable for the improper conduct among its own group of spectators.' Algeria fans are known for bringing fireworks into stadiums and could potentially bring more pyrotechnics to their last-16 tie with Germany. Lighting up the World Cup: But Algerian fans have cost their FA a £33,000 fine for their antics . Big bang: Algerian fans celebrate their first goal against Russia with the red flare . At a World Cup warm-up against Romania in Geneva on June 4, the referee threatened to abandon the match because of Algeria fans’ disorder. Russia goalkeeper Igor Akinfeev was also targeted with a laser pen before Algeria scored in a 1-1 draw at Curitiba last Thursday. Red hot atmosphere: Algerians are known to bring fireworks to their national team's matches . Green dot: Russian goalkeeper Igor Akinfeev was targeted by a laser when he came up against Algeria .

His name is Igor Akinfeev.
This happened when he played Algeria.
It was before Algeria scored a goal.
The game ended with one goal for each team.

People know that Algeria's fans often bring fireworks to games. They might bring more to their next big game against Germany.

Before the World Cup, on June 4, Algeria played a practice game against Romania. The referee almost stopped that game because Algeria's fans were causing trouble.
