The governing body announced the fine on Monday, stating that the Algerian FA was responsible for the improper behavior of its spectators.

Algeria fans are known for bringing fireworks into stadiums, and there is a possibility they could bring more pyrotechnics to their upcoming last-16 match against Germany.

This is not the first time Algerian fans have caused issues. In a World Cup warm-up game against Romania in Geneva on June 4, the referee threatened to stop the match due to fan disorder. Additionally, during the 1-1 draw against Russia in Curitiba last Thursday, Russia's goalkeeper Igor Akinfeev was targeted with a laser pen before Algeria scored.

original_text: Keerbergen () is a municipality located in the Belgian province of Flemish Brabant. The municipality comprises only the town of Keerbergen proper. On January 1, 2006, Keerbergen had a total population of 12,444. The total area is 18.39 km² which gives a population density of 677 inhabitants per km². History The oldest reference to Keerbergen appeared the eleventh century as Chierberghe, which was donated in 1036 by the lord of Incourt to the bishop of Liège. References External links Official website - Available only in Dutch Gazetteer Entry Municipalities of Flemish Brabant

We have a drawing with dots and lines. One dot is special. We make a special math group from it. This group comes from a special math table.

In this paper, we make the idea of the critical group bigger. It can come from any special math table. The numbers in these tables are special.

We find special math tables. They have numbers only on a line. These tables are like other special math tables. These other tables are like the ones for special drawings. Like a line of dots, a circle of dots, or all dots connected. The numbers in these tables are special.

We mostly look at when the special numbers are just whole numbers. Or when they are parts of other special math tables.

We use these special math tables with numbers on a line. We find the critical group for very special drawings. These drawings are changed in special ways. They start as lines of dots, circles of dots, or all dots connected.

We also find the critical group for other special math tables. These tables come from drawings that are changed in special ways. Like drawings with two groups of dots. simplified_primary_school:
When you have a drawing with dots and lines, and one dot is special, we can find its 'critical group'. This group is the 'cokernel of its reduced laplacian matrix'.

In this paper, we make the idea of the critical group work for more kinds of math tables (matrices). Now, it can be the 'cokernel' of any 'matrix' where the numbers inside come from a 'commutative ring with identity'.

We also find special 'diagonal matrices' that are like other 'matrices'. These other 'matrices' are bigger versions of the 'reduced laplacian matrix' for special drawings called 'paths', 'cycles', and 'complete graphs'. The numbers in these matrices also come from a 'commutative ring with identity'.

We mostly look at times when the numbers are whole numbers (like 1, 2, 3) or from smaller groups of 'matrices'.

Using these special 'diagonal matrices', we figure out the critical group for drawings made in a special way. These are called '@xmath4-cones of the @xmath5-duplications' of the 'path', the 'cycle', and the 'complete graph' drawings.

Also, as an extra result, we find the critical group for other 'matrices'. These are for drawings like the '@xmath4-cones of the @xmath5-duplication of the bipartite complete graph' (with @xmath4 dots in each part), and the 'bipartite complete graph' (with @xmath6 dots, but missing some lines).
simplified_secondary_school:
The critical group of a graph @xmath0 with a special vertex @xmath1 is defined as the cokernel of its reduced Laplacian matrix @xmath3. This article generalizes the concept of the critical group to be the cokernel of any matrix whose entries are from a commutative ring with an identity element.

We find diagonal matrices that are equivalent to generalized versions of the reduced Laplacian matrices for paths, cycles, and complete graphs. These matrices are defined over an arbitrary commutative ring with an identity element. We are mainly interested in cases where this base ring is the ring of integers or specific subrings of matrices.

Using these equivalent diagonal matrices, we calculate the critical group for the @xmath4-cones of the @xmath5-duplications of paths, cycles, and complete graphs. As a side result, we also calculate the critical group for other matrices. These include the @xmath4-cones of the @xmath5-duplication of the bipartite complete graph (with @xmath4 vertices in each partition), and the bipartite complete graph with @xmath6 vertices minus a matching.

original_text: (CNN) -- An explosion at a restaurant in downtown Djibouti killed three people and injured several more, according to a Ministry of Health official and a doctor at a local hospital. The two sources, who do not want to be named, confirmed three people died and an unknown number of others were wounded. The sources do not believe it was a suicide bombing. The Ministry of Health official confirmed that the restaurant where the attack occurred is La Chaumiere, a popular restaurant among Westerners who visit the small Horn of Africa nation. Both sources confirmed that foreigners were among the dead, but they would not give specifics on nationalities. The U.S. Embassy is Djibouti issued an alert to Americans following the attack at the busy restaurant. The alert advises citizens to "exercise heightened security measures." It also recommends Americans to "limit movements, avoid areas frequented by Westerners, implement additional personal security measures, and review personal crisis response plans." CNN's Shelby Lin Erdman contributed to this report .

People have studied a lot about how tiny parts (electrons) strongly push or pull each other in long, thin materials. These materials are like special strings of carbon atoms, such as polyenes. This pushing and pulling changes how these materials act when light shines on them. But we don't know as much about how these tiny parts push and pull in flat, sheet-like materials.

In this work, we show what we found using ideas and tests. We found that the tiny parts (electrons) in some special flat, ring-shaped molecules push each other away a little bit. These molecules have a balanced shape. We also show that when light makes these molecules excited, their energy levels are arranged in the opposite way from what older, simpler ideas would predict. The strong interactions between electrons in organic materials that are almost one-dimensional and have a special type of electron bonding (pi-conjugation), like polyenes, polyacetylenes, and polydiacetylenes, have been studied in great detail, especially regarding how they behave when exposed to light. However, much less is understood about these electron interactions in two-dimensional pi-conjugated systems.

In this study, we provide evidence, both from theory and experiments, for moderately repulsive electron interactions in several finite polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon molecules that have D2h symmetry. We show that the order of the energy levels of these molecules when they are excited is the opposite of what simpler theories (like one-electron and mean-field theories) would predict. Our findings show both similarities and differences in how important and how strong these electron interactions are in these two-dimensional molecules compared to what is seen in polyenes.

This text updates our analysis of the Unitarity Triangle (UT), a tool used to study particle physics, both within the Standard Model (SM) and for theories beyond it.

Within the SM, by combining direct measurements of the UT's sides and angles, we find that the triangle is very well-defined and consistent. This consistency confirms that the CKM matrix is the primary source of flavour mixing (how quarks change types) and CP-violation (differences between matter and antimatter). It also suggests that any effects from "new physics" (NP) – theories beyond the SM – can only be tiny adjustments to the CKM picture.

To investigate NP effects more broadly, we expanded the UT analysis to include data from specific particle transitions, such as @xmath0. We then simultaneously adjusted the parameters for both the CKM matrix and potential NP to find the combination that best fits all the available data.

While we found no evidence of NP effects in @xmath1-@xmath2 and @xmath3-@xmath4 mixing, there is a hint of NP in @xmath5-@xmath6 mixing, observed at the @xmath7 level of statistical significance.

By extending the UT analysis beyond the SM, we can also set limits on the coefficients (strengths) of the most general @xmath8 effective Hamiltonian, which describes potential new interactions. These limits, in turn, help us determine the energy scale at which new physics might exist.

original_text: understanding a visual scene goes beyond recognizing individual objects in isolation . relationships between objects also constitute rich semantic information about the scene . in this work , we explicitly model the objects and their relationships using scene graphs , a visually - grounded graphical structure of an image . we propose a novel end - to - end model that generates such structured scene representation from an input image . the model solves the scene graph inference problem using standard rnns and learns to iteratively improves its predictions via message passing . our joint inference model can take advantage of contextual cues to make better predictions on objects and their relationships . the experiments show that our model significantly outperforms previous methods on generating scene graphs using visual genome dataset and inferring support relations with nyu depth v2 dataset .

It makes these maps from a picture we give it.
The program figures out the maps.

Our program can use clues from the picture.

simplified_primary_school:
When we look at a picture, we don't just see separate things. We also see how the things are connected. This tells us more about the picture.

In this work, we use "scene graphs" to show the things and how they are connected. A scene graph is like a map for a picture. We made a new computer program that can make these scene graphs from a picture.

The program uses special computer brains called RNNs to figure out the scene graph. It learns to get better by sending messages to itself again and again. Our program uses clues from the whole picture to guess better about the things and how they are connected.

We did some tests. Our program is much better than old programs. It is better at making scene graphs using the Visual Genome pictures, and better at finding out what is holding what up using the NYU Depth V2 pictures.
simplified_secondary_school:
Understanding a visual scene involves more than just recognizing individual objects by themselves; the relationships between these objects also provide a lot of meaningful information about the scene. In this research, we clearly represent these objects and their relationships using 'scene graphs.' A scene graph is a visual diagram that shows the objects in an image and how they are connected.

We've developed a new, complete model that can create these structured scene representations (scene graphs) directly from an input image. This model solves the problem of figuring out the scene graph by using standard recurrent neural networks (RNNs). It then learns to make its predictions better step-by-step through a process called 'message passing.'

Our model, which considers everything together, can use surrounding information to make more accurate predictions about objects and their relationships. Experiments show that our model performs much better than older methods at creating scene graphs when tested with the Visual Genome dataset. It also does much better at identifying 'support relations' (like one object holding up another) using the NYU Depth V2 dataset.

We found a number answer for a problem. The problem was about a special kind of space and time that has 5 parts and looks like a ball. We used a big idea called Einstein-Yang-Mills-Gauss-Bonnet theory. We solved it with a special math method.

We found that the Gauss-Bonnet part really changes how the answer, which is like a tiny particle, acts. It makes more 'nodes' in the YM field.

If we add a special number called a 'negative cosmological constant' to our idea, we found that there is no 'horizon'. Also, nothing strange or broken happens in our idea.

simplified_secondary_school: We used computer calculations to find a solution for a 5-dimensional, spherically symmetric spacetime within the Einstein-Yang-Mills-Gauss-Bonnet theory, using a two-point boundary value method. We found that the Gauss-Bonnet part of the theory significantly influences how the particle-like solution behaves: it increases the number of nodes in the Yang-Mills (YM) field. When a negative cosmological constant is included in the model, we found that there is no horizon and no singular behavior. However, if the cosmological constant is positive, the model does show singular behavior.

The year 632 BC was part of the pre-Julian Roman calendar. In the Roman Empire, it was known as year 122 *Ab urbe condita*. The name 632 BC for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the *Anno Domini* calendar era became the main method for naming years in Europe.

During this year, Cylon, an Athenian nobleman, seized the Acropolis in a failed attempt to become king. Suizei, who would later become emperor of Japan, was born (he died in 549 BC). Among those who died were Cheng Dechen, the Chinese prime minister of Chu, and Cylon, the Athenian nobleman and usurper.

original_text: Belfast, Northern Ireland (CNN) -- Security was tight in Northern Ireland on Saturday for a contentious annual march by a Protestant group. Saturday's march by the Apprentice Boys through the mainly Catholic city of Londonderry is the largest of several they hold during the year, mainly in summer. The march commemorates the Siege of Derry in 1689, when thousands of Protestants died. Before the Londonderry parade was due to begin at 12:30 p.m. (7:30 a.m. ET), local members were also holding smaller "feeder" parades in their own areas. A feeder parade in the Ardoyne area of north Belfast, a flashpoint for sectarian violence, happened peacefully Saturday morning. Trouble flared in Ardoyne and other areas of Belfast last month after a decision to allow another Protestant parade to pass through a mainly Catholic neighborhood on July 12, a day when Protestants march to celebrate the victory of England's King William III over his ousted Catholic predecessor, James II, in 1689. Dozens of officers were hurt in days of violence surrounding the annual event, known as The Twelfth. Journalist Peter Taggart contributed to this report.

The walk remembers a time long ago. It was in 1689. It was called the Siege of Derry. Many Protestants died then.

The big walk in Londonderry started at 12:30 in the afternoon. Before that, other people had smaller walks. They walked in their own towns.

One small walk was in Ardoyne. Ardoyne is in Belfast. Sometimes, people fight there. But this walk was calm. No one fought. It was on Saturday morning.

Last month, there was trouble in Ardoyne. And in other parts of Belfast. This was because of another walk. A Protestant group walked through a Catholic area. This was on July 12.

On July 12, Protestants remember a king. King William won a fight long ago in 1689. He won against King James. King James was Catholic.

Many police got hurt. There were fights for many days around that walk. That walk is called The Twelfth.

This march, held by the Apprentice Boys through the mainly Catholic city of Londonderry, is the largest of several they hold each year, mostly in summer. It commemorates the Siege of Derry in 1689, an event where thousands of Protestants died.

Before the main Londonderry parade began at 12:30 p.m. (local time), local members also held smaller "feeder" parades in their own areas. One such feeder parade in Ardoyne, an area in north Belfast known for sectarian violence, happened peacefully on Saturday morning.

That day, known as "The Twelfth," is when Protestants celebrate King William III of England's victory over his ousted Catholic predecessor, James II, in 1689. original_text: polyhomeostatic adaption occurs when evolving systems try to achieve a target distribution function for certain dynamical parameters , a generalization of the notion of homeostasis . here we consider a single rate encoding leaky integrator neuron model driven by white noise , adapting slowly its internal parameters , the threshold and the gain , in order to achieve a given target distribution for its time - average firing rate . for the case of sparse encoding , when the target firing - rated distribution is bimodal , we observe the occurrence of spontaneous quasi - periodic adaptive oscillations resulting from fast transition between two quasi - stationary attractors . we interpret this behavior as self - organized stochastic tipping , with noise driving the escape from the quasi - stationary attractors .
simplified_kindergarten:
Things change. They want some numbers to be a special way. This is like keeping things steady, but for more things.

We look at a pretend brain cell. It gets little random pushes. It slowly changes its inside parts. These parts are called 'threshold' and 'gain.' It wants its 'talk rate' to be a special way.

If the cell does not talk much, and it wants to talk in two main ways, we see it wiggle. It changes all by itself. It quickly jumps between two places it likes to be. These places are almost still.

We think the cell changes itself in a random way. The random pushes make it leave the places it likes to be.
simplified_primary_school:
When things that are changing try to make many of their moving parts follow a special pattern, we call this a bigger kind of 'keeping things steady'.

We looked at a model of one brain cell. It got random pushes. This cell slowly changed its inside parts, like its trigger point and how strong its signals were. It did this to make its average signal-sending speed follow a certain pattern.

When the cell didn't send many signals, and it wanted its signal-sending speed to have two main patterns, we saw it started to swing back and forth on its own, almost like a regular rhythm. This happened because it quickly jumped between two almost steady states.

We think this is like a random tipping point that happened by itself. The random pushes made it leave those almost steady states.
simplified_secondary_school:
"Polyhomeostatic adaptation" describes how evolving systems try to achieve a specific desired pattern for how several of their changing properties behave. This is a more general idea than simple homeostasis, which usually means keeping just one thing stable.

In this study, we look at a simplified model of a neuron (a brain cell) that processes information based on how often it fires. This neuron is constantly influenced by random "noise." It slowly adjusts two internal settings: its "threshold" (the amount of input it needs to fire) and its "gain" (how strongly it responds to input). The goal is for its average firing rate over time to match a specific target pattern.

When the neuron uses "sparse encoding" (meaning it fires only when necessary) and its target firing rate pattern has two distinct peaks (it aims to be either very low or very high), we observe something interesting. The neuron's adaptation doesn't just settle; instead, it spontaneously starts to swing back and forth in a somewhat regular way. This occurs because the system rapidly switches between two states that are almost stable. We interpret this behavior as "self-organized stochastic tipping," where the random "noise" drives the system to escape from these almost-stable states.

original_text: The Premier League has announced that next season's competition will begin on August 8. The Barclays Premier League season will be followed by the European Championship, and the Premier League's move could allow England manager Roy Hodgson additional time to prepare his side, should they qualify for the tournament. In a short Twitter posting, the Premier League stated: 'The 2015/16 BPL season will kick off on Saturday 8 August'. Next season's Premier League campaign will start on August 8 . The Premier League announced the news on Twitter on Wednesday . The move could allow Roy Hodgson more time to prepare his squad for Euro 2016, if England qualify . The season will finish early in the calendar due to the scheduling of the Euros and Champions League final. The FA Cup third round will be the first fixture to be played next year due to how the 2016 calendar falls resulting in less congestion during the holiday period. The date for the final day of the season has yet to be announced. It will not be the earliest start to a Premier League campaign, with that remaining the August 7 opening day of the 1999-2000 season.

The Premier League season will finish earlier than usual. This is because of the European Championship and another big game called the Champions League final.

Next year, the first game will be the FA Cup third round. This is because of how the days fall in 2016, which means there will be fewer games around the holidays.

The Premier League announced on Wednesday that its next season, 2015/16, will kick off on Saturday, August 8. This early start could give England manager Roy Hodgson more time to prepare his team for the European Championship (Euro 2016), should England qualify. The season is set to finish earlier than usual because of the scheduling of Euro 2016 and the Champions League final.

The Premier League shared this news in a short Twitter post, stating: "The 2015/16 BPL season will kick off on Saturday 8 August".

While the start date is confirmed, the final day of the season has not yet been announced. This will not be the earliest start to a Premier League season; the 1999-2000 campaign began even earlier, on August 7.

Separately, the FA Cup third round will be the first fixture played next year. This is because of how the 2016 calendar falls, which aims to reduce congestion during the holiday period.

original_text: The Bertone Blitz is an electric sports car produced in 1992 by the company Bertone, which worked under Ferrari. It is propelled from 0 to 62 mph (0 to 100 km/h) in six seconds via two 36 horsepower (27 kW) (continuous output) DC motors applying a combined total of 95 Nm (70 ft·lbf) of torque. It has a weight of 650 kg (1433 lb), 260 kg (572 lb) of that weight is in lead acid batteries. The typical range of this battery electric vehicle is 60 to 80 miles (100 to 130 km) per charge and it can be recharged within 4 to 6 hours. An eruption usually means when a volcano bursts open. But the word 'eruption' can also mean other things.

It can mean when gas bursts out of a lake. It can also mean when hot water bursts out from under the ground, like a geyser.

In space, an eruption can be when the sun bursts with light, called a solar flare. It can also be when a star suddenly gets very bright, like a nova. For example, a star called Eta Carinae had a 'Great Eruption' between 1837 and 1855, and a 'Lesser Eruption' between 1887 and 1895.

In medicine, an eruption means when a tooth pushes through the gum. It can also mean certain skin problems that show up on your body.

In movies and TV, 'Eruption' is the name of a TV film from New Zealand made in 2010. It is also the nickname of a Filipino actor, model, and rugby player named Eric Tai (born in 1984) when he is on the TV show 'It's Showtime'.

In music, 'Eruption' was a British band from the 1970s and 1980s. There was also a German band called 'Eruption' that played experimental music for a short time. An album by Kluster is named 'Eruption'. A song without words called 'Eruption' is on Van Halen's first album. Another song called 'Eruption' is by Emerson, Lake & Palmer, from their song 'Tarkus'. A very long song, 23 minutes, called 'Eruption' is by Focus, from their album 'Focus II' or 'Moving Waves'.

There is a radio station in London called Eruption Radio.

You might also know the 'Diet Coke and Mentos eruption', which is like a soda geyser. And there's a song by Snoop Dogg from 2007 called 'Sexual Eruption'.
simplified_secondary_school:
An eruption most often refers to volcanoes. However, the word 'eruption' is also used in other fields:

In geology, an eruption can be a 'limnic eruption,' which is when dissolved carbon dioxide bursts out from a body of water. It can also be a 'hydrothermal explosion,' where underground water bursts out, especially in a geyser eruption.

In astronomy, 'eruption' can mean a 'solar eruption' or 'flare' from the Sun. It also describes a 'nova' – a sudden brightening of a star. For example, the star Eta Carinae had a 'Great Eruption' from 1837 to 1855 and a 'Lesser Eruption' from 1887 to 1895.

In medicine, 'tooth eruption' is when teeth emerge through the gums. Many skin conditions are also referred to as 'eruptions'.

In film and television, 'Eruption' is the title of a 2010 New Zealand television film. It's also the screen name of Eric Tai, a Filipino actor, model, and rugby union player (born 1984), when he appears on the show 'It's Showtime'.

In music, 'Eruption' is the name of a British disco/R&B band from the 1970s and 1980s, and also a short-lived German experimental band. It's also the title of an album by Kluster. Several musical pieces are called 'Eruption': an instrumental track from Van Halen's first album, a part of the song 'Tarkus' by Emerson, Lake & Palmer, and a 23-minute piece by Focus from their album 'Focus II' (also known as 'Moving Waves').

For radio, 'Eruption Radio' is a station based in London.

The term 'eruption' also describes a 'Diet Coke and Mentos eruption,' sometimes called a 'soda geyser'. Finally, 'Sexual Eruption' is the title of a 2007 song by Snoop Dogg.

They also measured "differential cross sections" – a way to quantify how often specific interactions happen – and compared these results to calculations from the theory of Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD).

original_text: by making use of the spatial shape of paired photons , parametric down - conversion allows the generation of two - photon entanglement in a multidimensional hilbert space . how much entanglement can be generated in this way ? in principle , the infinite - dimensional nature of the spatial degree of freedom renders unbounded the amount of entanglement available . however , in practice , the specific configuration used , namely its geometry , the length of the nonlinear crystal and the size of the pump beam , can severely limit the value that could be achieved . here we show that the use of quasi - phase - matching engineering allows to increase the amount of entanglement generated , reaching values of tens of ebits of entropy of entanglement under different conditions . our work thus opens a way to fulfill the promise of generating massive spatial entanglement under a diverse variety of circumstances , some more favorable for its experimental implementation .
simplified_kindergarten:
We use how two light pieces look. This helps us make two tied light pieces.

How many tied light pieces can we make?

If everything was perfect, we could make so many tied light pieces.

But in real life, how we set things up makes it hard. The shape, a special crystal, and the light we use. These stop us from making many tied light pieces.

We found a clever way. It helps us make a lot more tied light pieces. We can do this in different ways.

simplified_primary_school: A special method uses the shape of two light particles (photons) to link them together. This linking is called entanglement, and it can happen in many complex ways. How much of this special linking can we make? In theory, we could make an endless amount of this linking because the light particles can be arranged in countless ways in space. But in real life, how we set things up can stop us from making a lot of this linking. Things like the shape of the setup, the length of a special material called a nonlinear crystal, and the size of the light beam we use all play a part. Our work shows that using a clever design for this special material helps us make much more linking. We were able to get tens of 'ebits' of linking in different situations. Our discovery helps us reach the goal of making a huge amount of linked light particles. We can do this in many different ways, and some ways are easier to try out in experiments.
simplified_secondary_school: Parametric down-conversion uses the spatial arrangement of two photons to create entanglement between them. This entanglement can exist in many dimensions. How much entanglement can be created this way? Theoretically, because the spatial arrangement of photons has infinite possibilities, there is no limit to the amount of entanglement that can be created. However, in practice, the specific setup used – including its geometry, the length of the special crystal, and the size of the laser beam – can greatly limit the amount of entanglement produced. We show that using a technique called quasi-phase-matching engineering can increase the amount of entanglement generated. We achieved values of tens of ebits (a measure of entanglement) under various conditions. Our work therefore makes it possible to create a large amount of spatial entanglement in many different situations, some of which are more practical for experiments.

We use a special math tool to guess how well we can see tiny changes in atoms when they move unevenly or are near strong gravity. A science idea called the Unruh-DeWitt model helps us study how an atom moves when it's always speeding up and is a little bit connected to an invisible energy field that has no weight. When our measuring tool moves very fast, just like the atom, we find out how the special math tool changes over a special kind of time. We learned that when we measure tiny things that move very fast, the ability to see tiny changes always gets worse over time. To make these fast, tiny measurements better, we think about using two flat mirrors that stand at right angles to each other.
simplified_secondary_school: This study examines how relativistic effects influence the accuracy of quantum measurement techniques used with particle detectors, such as two-level atoms. Quantum Fisher Information is employed to determine how sensitive the phase of these atoms is when they are undergoing non-inertial motion (accelerating or rotating) or are in gravitational fields. The Unruh-DeWitt model is suitable for studying the behavior of an atom that is accelerating at a constant rate and is weakly connected to a massless scalar vacuum field (a fundamental field in empty space). When the measuring device shares the same relativistic motion as the atom, we can then determine how the quantum Fisher Information changes over Rindler proper time (a specific measure of time for accelerating observers). A key finding is that the phase sensitivity consistently decreases, which is a defining characteristic of relativistic quantum estimation. To enhance relativistic quantum metrology, we propose considering the presence of two reflecting flat surfaces positioned perpendicular to each other.

simplified_secondary_school:
This information covers genetic disorders, including the type of mutation and the specific chromosome affected, where this information is known.

Although the phrase "disease-causing gene" is commonly used, it's actually an abnormality in the parents' genes that leads to these conditions in a child. There are over 6,000 known genetic disorders in humans.

The most common types of genetic mutations are:
*   **P – Point mutation:** A small change, such as an insertion or deletion, that occurs entirely within one gene.
*   **D – Deletion:** One or more genes are missing.
*   **Dup – Duplication:** One or more genes are copied, meaning there are extra copies.
*   **C – Whole chromosome extra, missing, or both:** An entire chromosome is either added, missing, or there's a combination of both.
*   **T – Trinucleotide repeat disorders:** A gene becomes longer because a specific three-nucleotide sequence is repeated too many times.

We found a new way to understand how liquids work with heat. This way looks at how all the tiny parts in a liquid move together.

We used computers to watch tiny parts move in many different liquids, like special gases, normal liquids, and melted metals. We found clear proof that a liquid's energy and how much heat it needs to get hotter can be explained by how often its tiny parts 'hop' around. This 'hopping' changes with how hot the liquid is.

Our ideas matched what the computers showed about how liquids act with heat. This was true for a very big range of temperatures, spanning tens of thousands of kelvin. This range included liquids that were not too hot, and also very hot fluids that act like both liquids and gases.

simplified_secondary_school: We have developed a new approach to liquid thermodynamics, which is based on the concept of collective modes. We conducted extensive computer simulations of noble, molecular, and metallic liquids. These simulations provided direct evidence that a liquid's energy and specific heat are accurately described by how the Frenkel (or hopping) frequency changes with temperature. The match between our predicted and calculated thermodynamic properties was observed over a remarkably wide temperature range, spanning tens of thousands of Kelvin. This range includes both subcritical liquids and supercritical fluids. We also discuss the structural crossover and the inter-relationships between structure, dynamics, and thermodynamics of liquids and supercritical fluids.

We directly calculate the curvature of the Hitchin connection within geometric quantization on a symplectic manifold, using only differential geometric techniques. Specifically, we show that this curvature acts as a first-order operator on the quantum spaces. Projective flatness occurs if the Kähler structures do not have holomorphic vector fields.

Finally, these results are applied to quantum Chern-Simons theory, for both compact and complex gauge groups.

original_text: the bose - hubbard model exhibits a rich phase diagram consisting both of insulating regimes where diagonal long range ( solid ) order dominates as well as conducting regimes where off diagonal long range order ( superfluidity ) is present . in this paper we describe the results of quantum monte carlo calculations of the phase diagram , both for the hard and soft core cases , with a particular focus on the possibility of simultaneous superfluid and solid order . we also discuss the appearance of phase separation in the model . the simulations are compared with analytic calculations of the phase diagram and spin wave dispersion .
simplified_kindergarten:
The Bose-Hubbard model has many parts. Some parts are like a solid block. Things stay in place there. Other parts are like a super liquid. Things move around there.

In this paper, we show what we found. We used a special math game to look at the parts of the model. We looked at hard things and soft things. We especially looked to see if the super liquid and solid block could be there at the same time.

We also talk about when parts in the model split up.

We checked our math game with other math. We checked the parts and how waves move.
simplified_primary_school:
The Bose-Hubbard model shows a map with many different parts. Some parts are like insulators, where things are stuck in a solid way. Other parts are like conductors, where things flow like a super-liquid.

In this paper, we tell what we found from special computer tests about this map. We looked at two types of situations, called hard core and soft core. We especially wanted to see if the super-liquid and solid parts could happen at the same time.

We also talk about if different parts of the model can separate.

We checked our computer tests against other math calculations about the map and spin waves.
simplified_secondary_school: The Bose-Hubbard model has a complex phase diagram. This diagram shows both insulating states, where a "solid" arrangement (diagonal long-range order) is most important, and conducting states, where "superfluidity" (off-diagonal long-range order) is present. In this paper, we present results from quantum Monte Carlo simulations that map out this phase diagram for both hard-core and soft-core versions of the model. We particularly focus on whether superfluidity and solid order can exist at the same time. We also discuss when different phases separate within the model. Our simulation results are then compared with theoretical calculations of the phase diagram and spin wave dispersion.

"Sund" can refer to several things.

In northern Europe, "Sund" is a word often used for a strait, which is a narrow waterway connecting two larger bodies of water. Over a hundred such straits in northern Europe are named Sund. Specific places named Sund include a municipality in Åland, Finland; a former municipality in Vestland county, Norway; a village in Hemnes municipality, Nordland county, Norway; a town in the Faroe Islands; a village in Trosa Municipality, Sweden; and a hamlet in Ydre Municipality, Sweden. The Øresund is a well-known strait situated between the Baltic Sea and the North Sea. There is also a place called Sunds in Denmark.

Additionally, "Sund" can refer to Sudden unexplained nocturnal death, which is a type of Sudden arrhythmic death syndrome.

Northampton hooker Dylan Hartley is returning to play against the Ospreys in the European Cup this Sunday, after a three-week suspension. This was his fifth ban, imposed for elbowing Leicester centre Matt Smith.

During his three-week lay-off, Hartley kept busy by painting Northampton’s changing rooms in the club’s colours. His teammate Phil Dowson joked that Hartley might have hoped to add "psychological value" to the room, but admitted the painting was "awful" and had to be redone.

Hartley, 28, now hopes to show he has learned from his mistake. He aims to deliver strong performances before England’s Six Nations tournament begins against Wales on February 6.

Dowson added that Hartley understands his error and is keen to get back into action. He sees this as a significant chance for Hartley to demonstrate his maturity and experience. Dowson noted that while Hartley has sometimes reacted poorly under pressure, he has also responded very strongly on other occasions. Hartley was previously seen playing against New Zealand's Ma'a Nonu in Hamilton last June.

original_text: the fields of occultation and microlensing are linked historically . early this century , occultation of the sun by the moon allowed the apparent positions of background stars projected near the limb of the sun to be measured and compared with their positions six months later when the sun no longer influenced the light path to earth . the measured shift in the stellar positions was consistent with lensing by the gravitational field of the sun during the occultation , as predicted by the theory of general relativity . this series of lectures explores the principles , possibilities and challenges associated with using occultation and microlensing to discover and characterize unseen planets orbiting distant stars . the two techniques are complementary in terms of the information that they provide about planetary systems and the range of system parameters to which they are most sensitive . although the challenges are large , both microlensing and occultation may provide avenues for the discovery of extra - solar planets as small as earth . # 1#1 _get ref _
simplified_kindergarten:
Occultation and microlensing are connected.
Many years ago, the moon hid the sun. People looked at stars near the sun. They saw where the stars seemed to be. Six months later, they looked at the stars again. The sun was not in the way then.
The stars had moved a little. This showed the sun's pull bent the starlight. A big idea called General Relativity said this would happen.
These talks are about occultation and microlensing. We learn how they work. We learn what they can do. We learn what is hard. We use them to find planets we cannot see. These planets go around far-away stars.
These two ways help each other. They give us different facts about planets. They are good at finding different kinds of planets.
It is hard to do. But microlensing and occultation can help us find planets. They can find planets as small as our Earth. These planets are around other stars.
simplified_primary_school:
Occultation and microlensing are two ways to study space that have been connected for a long time.

A long time ago, the Moon moved in front of the Sun. This is called an occultation. Scientists looked at stars that seemed to be near the edge of the Sun. They wrote down where these stars were. Six months later, the Sun was in a different place. It was not blocking the light from those stars anymore. Scientists looked at the stars again and wrote down where they were.

They saw that the stars had moved a little bit. This movement showed that the Sun's gravity had bent the starlight when the Moon was in front of it. This was just what a big science idea called "general relativity" said would happen.

These talks are about how we can use occultation and microlensing. We can use them to find planets that we cannot see. These planets go around stars far away. The talks also look at what we can learn about these planets and what makes it hard to find them.

Occultation and microlensing work well together. They give us different kinds of clues about planets and their stars. Each way is good at finding different things about these planets.

It is hard to do this work. But both microlensing and occultation might help us find planets outside our solar system. Some of these planets could be as small as Earth.
simplified_secondary_school: The fields of occultation and microlensing have a historical connection. Early in the 20th century, a solar eclipse (when the Moon blocks the Sun) allowed scientists to measure the apparent positions of background stars that appeared close to the Sun's edge. These measurements were then compared to the stars' positions six months later, when the Sun was no longer affecting the light traveling from those stars to Earth. The observed shift in the stars' positions matched what was predicted by Einstein's theory of general relativity: that the Sun's gravity would bend the starlight, causing a "lensing" effect during the eclipse. This series of lectures explores the basic ideas, potential uses, and difficulties involved in using both occultation and microlensing to find and study unseen planets orbiting distant stars. These two techniques complement each other because they offer different types of information about planetary systems and are best at detecting different characteristics of these systems. Even though there are significant challenges, both microlensing and occultation could provide ways to discover exoplanets (planets outside our solar system) that are as small as Earth.

original_text: a new , improved split - step backward euler ( ssbe ) method is introduced and analyzed for stochastic differential delay equations(sddes ) with generic variable delay . the method is proved to be convergent in mean - square sense under conditions ( assumption [ olc ] ) that the diffusion coefficient @xmath0 is globally lipschitz in both @xmath1 and @xmath2 , but the drift coefficient @xmath3 satisfies one - sided lipschitz condition in @xmath1 and globally lipschitz in @xmath2 . further , exponential mean - square stability of the proposed method is investigated for sddes that have a negative one - sided lipschitz constant . our results show that the method has the unconditional stability property in the sense that it can well reproduce stability of underlying system , without any restrictions on stepsize @xmath4 . numerical experiments and comparisons with existing methods for sddes illustrate the computational efficiency of our method . + * ams subject classification : * 60h35,65c20,65l20 . + * key words : * split - step backward euler method , strong convergence , one - sided lipschitz condition , exponential mean - square stability , mean - square linear stability
simplified_kindergarten:
A new math way is here. It is called SSBE. We show it. We look at it closely. It helps with special math problems. These are SDDEs. They have a changing delay.

This math way works well. It gets the right answer. This is true if some rules are met. A part of the math, @xmath0, follows a rule for @xmath1 and @xmath2. Another part, @xmath3, follows a different rule for @xmath1. It follows the first rule for @xmath2.

Also, we check if this math way stays steady. It should not go wild. We check this for SDDE problems. These problems have a special negative number.

We found this math way is always steady. It shows how steady the real problem is. We do not need to worry about the step size. This is called @xmath4.

We tried it with numbers. We looked at other math ways. Our math way works fast. It is good at using computers.
simplified_primary_school:
A new and better way, called the 'split-step backward Euler' (SSBE) method, has been made. We looked at this method for special math problems called 'stochastic differential delay equations' (SDDEs), which have a changing wait time.

We proved that this method gives answers that get closer and closer to the right ones over time. This happens if some rules are met. For example, a part of the problem called the 'diffusion coefficient' (@xmath0) must be smooth and predictable in relation to both @xmath1 and @xmath2. Also, another part called the 'drift coefficient' (@xmath3) must be smooth and predictable in a special way for @xmath1, and generally smooth and predictable for @xmath2.

We also checked how steady and reliable this method is over a long time. This was for SDDE problems that have a special number called a 'negative one-sided Lipschitz constant'.

Our results show that this method is always steady and reliable. It can correctly show how steady the original math problem is, no matter how big or small the 'stepsize' (@xmath4) is.

We tried out our new method with numbers and compared it to other ways people already use for SDDE problems. This showed that our new method works faster and better.
simplified_secondary_school:
A new and improved mathematical technique, called the Split-Step Backward Euler (SSBE) method, is presented and studied. This method is designed for solving Stochastic Differential Delay Equations (SDDEs), which are equations that involve delays that can change over time.

We prove that this method is "mean-square convergent," meaning its solutions get closer to the true solutions over time, on average. This proof holds true under specific conditions (called assumption [OLC]): the "diffusion coefficient" (a part of the equation) must have a "globally Lipschitz" property in both its variables (meaning it doesn't change too rapidly), while the "drift coefficient" (another part) must satisfy a "one-sided Lipschitz condition" in one variable and be "globally Lipschitz" in the other.

We also examine the "exponential mean-square stability" of our method. This means we check if the method remains stable over long periods, especially for SDDEs that have a specific characteristic: a negative "one-sided Lipschitz constant."

Our findings demonstrate that the SSBE method has "unconditional stability." This means it can accurately show the stability of the original mathematical system it's modeling, without needing to use a very small "step size" (a parameter in numerical methods). Through numerical tests and comparisons with other methods used for SDDEs, we show that our method is computationally efficient.

original_text: we report on the development of a numerical code to calculate the angle - dependent synchrotron + synchrotron self - compton radiation from relativistic jet sources with partially ordered magnetic fields and anisotropic particle distributions . using a multi - zone radiation transfer approach , we can simulate magnetic - field configurations ranging from perfectly ordered ( unidirectional ) to randomly oriented ( tangled ) . we demonstrate that synchrotron self - compton model fits to the spectral energy distributions ( seds ) of extragalactic jet sources may be possible with a wide range of magnetic - field values , depending on their orientation with respect to the jet axis and the observer . this is illustrated with the example of a spectral fit to the sed of mrk 421 from multiwavelength observations in 2006 , where acceptable fits are possible with magnetic - field values varying within a range of an order of magnitude for different degrees of b - field alignment and orientation .
simplified_kindergarten:
We made a computer program. It helps us see how light shines. This light comes from big fast jets in space. These jets have magnets. The magnets can be a bit messy.

We can make pretend magnets. Some are in a line. Some are all mixed up.

Our pretend light can match real light from far away jets. The magnets can be strong or weak. It depends on how they point. It also depends on how we look at them.

We show this with a jet called Mrk 421. We looked at it in 2006. Our pretend light matched well. The magnets could be very different strengths. This was true for different ways the magnets pointed.
simplified_primary_school:
We made a computer program to figure out a special kind of light. This light comes from very fast jets in space and comes out at different angles. These jets have magnetic fields that are a bit tidy and a bit messy, and tiny bits inside them are not spread out evenly.

We used a way to look at how light moves in many parts. This helps us pretend to see how magnetic fields are set up, from very tidy (all going one way) to very messy (all mixed up).

We show that our ideas about this special light can match how much light and energy comes from jets outside our galaxy. This can work with many different strengths of magnetic fields, depending on which way they are pointing compared to the jet and to us.

We show this using an example of a jet called Mrk 421. We looked at it with many kinds of light in 2006. Our ideas matched the light and energy from Mrk 421. Good matches can happen even if the magnetic field strengths are ten times stronger or ten times weaker, depending on how the magnetic fields are lined up and pointing.
simplified_secondary_school:
We developed a computer program to calculate the synchrotron and synchrotron self-Compton radiation emitted from very fast, 'relativistic' jets in space. This radiation changes depending on the angle from which it is observed. The program also considers that these jets have magnetic fields that are partly organized and particles that are not spread evenly in all directions. Using a method that divides the jet into many zones to track how radiation moves, our program can simulate different ways magnetic fields are arranged, from perfectly straight (all pointing in one direction) to completely messy (tangled).

We show that when we use our synchrotron self-Compton model to match the observed 'spectral energy distributions' (SEDs – which show the total energy emitted at different wavelengths) from jets in other galaxies, many different magnetic field strengths can produce a good match. This depends on how the magnetic field is angled both within the jet and relative to us, the observers. For example, we applied our model to the SED of a specific jet source called Mrk 421, using data collected in 2006 from various types of light. We found that even with acceptable matches to the data, the magnetic field strength could vary by as much as ten times, depending on how aligned and oriented the magnetic field was.

original_text: Riddarfjärden (, "The Knight Firth") is the easternmost bay of Lake Mälaren in central Stockholm. Stockholm was founded in 1252 on an island in the stream where Lake Mälaren (from the west) drains into the Baltic Sea (to the east); today the island is called Stadsholmen and constitutes Stockholm's Old Town. The panorama picture featured in this article was taken from the heights of Södermalm, west of Stadsholmen, looking down on Riddarfjärden. Left to right are viewable: Västerbron bridge Kungsholmen Island Stockholm City Hall, a red brick building with a bell tower, where the Nobel Prize dinner is served The tower of Klara Kyrka on Norrmalm, with its green copper roof five white sky scrapers between Sergels torg and Hötorget construction cranes iron tower of Riddarholmen Church on Riddarholmen Island yellow tower of Storkyrkan on Stadsholmen, in front of the flat roof of the Stockholm Palace narrow tower of Tyska Kyrkan on Stadsholmen distant radio and TV tower Kaknästornet Seasons Riddarfjärden throughout the year. See also Geography of Stockholm External links Bays of Sweden Mälaren Geography of Stockholm Landforms of Stockholm County

original_text: Verden () is a Kreis (district) in the centre of Lower Saxony, Germany. Adjoining it are (from the northwest clockwise) the districts of Osterholz, Rotenburg, Heidekreis, Nienburg and Diepholz, as well as the city of Bremen. Geography The Aller River enters the district in the east and joins the Weser in the center of the district. In the north, the Wümme River passes from west to the east across the district's territory. The western half of the district is occupied by suburbs in the Bremen metropolitan area, e.g. the town of Achim. History The district dates back to the two Ämter of Verden and Achim, which were created in 1852 and 1859. After the Kingdom of Hanover became part of Prussia, they were recreated as districts (Kreis), and merged in 1932. In 1939 two municipalities of the district were added to Bremen, in 1972 Thedinghausen (previously an exclave of the district of Braunschweig) was added. Twinnings The city and rayon of Bagrationovsk (Russia) The city and municipality of Górowo Ilaweckie (Poland) Coat of arms Towns and municipalities References External links Official homepage Districts of Lower Saxony

A river called Aller flows into Verden from the east. It then meets another river called Weser in the middle of Verden. In the north part of Verden, a river called Wümme flows from the west side to the east side.

The Aller River enters the district from the east and joins the Weser River in the center. In the north, the Wümme River flows from west to east across the district's territory. The western half of the district contains suburbs of the Bremen metropolitan area, including the town of Achim.

The district's history dates back to two administrative units, called Ämter, which were established in Verden in 1852 and Achim in 1859. After the Kingdom of Hanover became part of Prussia, these units were re-established as districts (Kreis) and then merged in 1932. In 1939, two municipalities from the district were added to Bremen. Later, in 1972, Thedinghausen was added to the district; it had previously been an exclave (a separate territory) of the district of Braunschweig.

Verden is twinned with the city and rayon of Bagrationovsk in Russia, and the city and municipality of Górowo Ilaweckie in Poland.

Trees from Madagascar were once thought to be Genipa, but they are actually a different kind of tree. They are now called Hyperacanthus. Scientists are not sure if Genipa spruceana is truly different from Genipa americana.

The large flowers grow in clusters called terminal cymes. The calyx (the outer part of the flower) is tubular. The corolla (the petals) can be shaped like a trumpet or a short cylinder, and it has 5 to 6 lobes. The stamens (male parts) are found at the top of the corolla.

The fruit is a berry that is almost spherical or egg-shaped. It is smooth, fleshy, and has a thick rind. Inside, the seeds are large and flat.

It's important to note that some species from Madagascar, which Drake originally described, are not part of the Rubiaceae tribe Gardenieae, unlike the Genipa species found in the New World. Instead, they belong to the tribe Octotropideae. These particular species were later moved to a different group called the genus Hyperacanthus. Scientists are unsure if Genipa spruceana is truly a separate species from Genipa americana.

The species currently recognized within the Genipa genus are:
Genipa americana L.
Genipa infundibuliformis Zappi & J.Semir
Genipa spruceana Steyerm.

This genus naturally grows in the tropical forests of America, including Florida.

If a statement like "A if and only if B" is true, then we can know two other things are true: "If A, then B" is true, and "If B, then A" is also true.

For example, let's say it's true that "I'm breathing if and only if I'm alive." This means two things are true: "If I'm breathing, then I'm alive." And also, "If I'm alive, then I'm breathing."

Biconditional elimination refers to two valid rules in propositional logic. These rules allow you to conclude a conditional statement from a biconditional statement.

A biconditional statement, often written as "P ↔ Q," means "P if and only if Q." A conditional statement, written as "P → Q," means "if P, then Q."

For instance, if it's true that "I'm breathing if and only if I'm alive," then you can logically conclude two separate statements:
1. "If I'm breathing, then I'm alive." (This is P → Q)
2. "If I'm alive, then I'm breathing." (This is Q → P)

So, the two rules are:
*   From "P ↔ Q," you can conclude "P → Q."
*   From "P ↔ Q," you can also conclude "Q → P."

In a formal proof, if you have a statement like "P ↔ Q" on one line, you are allowed to write either "P → Q" or "Q → P" on a subsequent line.

These rules can also be expressed using different formal notations. For example, in sequent notation, they are written as "P ↔ Q ⊢ P → Q" and "P ↔ Q ⊢ Q → P." The symbol "⊢" here means that the statement on the right can be logically derived or concluded from the statement on the left within a logical system.

Another way to represent these rules is as statements that are always true (tautologies) in propositional logic: "(P ↔ Q) → (P → Q)" and "(P ↔ Q) → (Q → P)." These statements show that the truth of the biconditional always guarantees the truth of the corresponding conditionals.

original_text: A prill is a small aggregate or globule of a material, most often a dry sphere, formed from a melted liquid. Prilled is a term used in mining and manufacturing to refer to a product that has been pelletized. The pellets are a neater, simpler form for handling, with reduced dust. The material to be prilled must be in a solid state at room temperature and a low-viscosity liquid when melted. Prills are formed by allowing drops of the melted prill substance to congeal or freeze in mid-air after being dripped from the top of a tall prilling tower. Certain agrochemicals such as urea are often supplied in prilled form. Fertilizers (ammonium nitrate, urea, NPK fertilizer) and some detergent powders are commonly manufactured as prills. Melted material may also be atomized and then allowed to form smaller prills that are useful in cosmetics, food, and animal feed. See also Shot (pellet) References Agricultural chemicals Metallurgy

The stuff for prills must be hard when it is not hot. It must be runny like water when it is hot.

To make prills, hot drops fall. They get cold in the air. They turn hard. They fall from a very tall tower.

Some farm chemicals, like urea, are often made as prills. Plant food and some soap powders are often made into prills.

Hot stuff can also be sprayed into tiny bits. These bits make very small prills. These tiny prills are used in makeup, food, and animal food.

original_text: (CNN) -- Former Cuban leader Fidel Castro weighed in on the Arizona mass shooting that killed six and injured 14, including Rep. Gabrielle Giffords, in a column distributed Monday. The column, titled "An Atrocious Act," condemns the shooting, but Castro didn't miss the chance to criticize the right wing of U.S. politics. He said Giffords was an enemy of the Tea Party and described her as a supporter of immigration reform, stem cell research and alternative energy, "measures that are hated by the far right." But despite the differences between the United States and Cuba, the Communist former president said he was saddened by the news. "Even those of us who don't share his (President Barack Obama's) political or philosophical ideas in the least sincerely hope that no children, judges, congressmen or any U.S. citizen should die in such an absurd and unjustifiable way," he wrote.

We look closely at these patterns and use the idea of things looking the same from different spots. We show clear answers for two special 3D shapes, and we found three new 3D shapes. simplified_secondary_school: The large-scale patterns and their strengths (low multipole amplitudes) observed in the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) radiation can be explained by mathematical "selection rules." These rules arise from the idea that the universe's overall shape might be "multiply-connected," meaning it could loop back on itself in complex ways. Our method involved a "multipole analysis" of these patterns, using "harmonic bases" (mathematical building blocks for patterns on a sphere) and including "point symmetry" (where patterns look the same from different angles around a central point). We provide specific results for two models of the universe's shape, known as "cubic 3-spherical manifolds," focusing on their simplest mathematical forms. Furthermore, we identified three new "spherical 3-manifolds," which are other possible shapes for the universe. This research was conducted at the Institute for Theoretical Physics at the University of Tübingen, Germany.

original_text: Buggenhout () is a municipality in the Belgian province of East Flanders in the Denderstreek. The municipality comprises the towns of Buggenhout proper, Briel, Opdorp, and Opstal. On December 31, 2013, Buggenhout had a total population of 14,444. The total area is 25.25 square kilometres, which gives a population density of 572 inhabitants per square kilometre. Buggenhout is also known for its forest, the "Buggenhoutbos", which is the largest forest of East Flanders. The geographic centre of Flanders lies in Opdorp. Buggenhout has two breweries, Bosteels Brewery (known for the "Tripel Karmeliet", "Deus" (a champagne beer), "Kwak") and De Landtsheer, now called Brouwerij Malheur (known for "Malheur"). Etymology The original meaning of the name is "beechwood" or "beech forest" (in modern Dutch: Beukenhout). The name is derived from the Middle Dutch Bucghenhout from Old Dutch Bukenholt. Buken comes from Proto-Germanic * (Modern Dutch: , Old English: (beeches)), holt from *hulta (Modern Dutch: , Old English: (forest)). Buggenhout still contains a small forested area called Buggenhoutbos. References External links Official website Municipalities of East Flanders

original_text: Amoz (), also known as Amotz, was the father of the prophet Isaiah, mentioned in Isaiah 1:1; 2:1 and 13:1, and in II Kings 19:2, 20; 20:1. Nothing else is known for certain about him. There is a Talmudic tradition that when the name of a prophet's father is given, the father was also a prophet, so that Amoz would have been a prophet like his son. Though it is mentioned frequently as the patronymic title of Isaiah, the name Amoz appears nowhere else in the Bible. The rabbis of the Talmud declared, based upon a rabbinic tradition, that Amoz was the brother of Amaziah (אמציה), the king of Judah at that time (and, as a result, that Isaiah himself was a member of the royal family). According to some traditions, Amoz is the "man of God" in 2 Chronicles 25:7–9 (Seder Olam Rabbah 20), who cautioned Amaziah to release the Israelite mercenaries that he had hired. References Prophets in Judaism Hebrew Bible people Isaiah

original_text: Everyone has their reasons for travelling - be it to get the ultimate tan, taste exotic dishes or dive into history - but most of us have the same goal in mind. But precise motivations vary from continent to continent. North Americans, for example, hope to strengthen relationships with loved ones, while Europeans really just want to brag about their experiences. Travellers are also nine times more likely to crave unique rather than pampered experiences, while 38 per cent of global holidaymakers expand their diets to include the new foods they tried during their travels. Five per cent of travellers say a trip motivated them to get engaged or married. And it's no surprise that holidaymakers are finding plenty of rewards from seeing the world. original_text: Markku Tapani Toikka (born 5 April 1955 in Somero, Finland) is a Finnish stand-up comedian and actor. His acting roles include "Rahikainen" in Aki Kaurismäki's Crime and Punishment and "Pekka" in another Aki Kaurismäki film Calamari Union. In Calamari Union, Pekka appears for most of the film to gibber in English in an apparent schizophrenic delusion about his days as a soldier of fortune. This viewer interpretation is subverted towards the end of the movie, as a group of Latin American freedom fighters wreak their vengeance by shooting him like a dog. As a stand-up comic, his shtick has largely been that of the balding middle aged man going through an identity crisis. He lives in Helsinki with his wife, Ulla-Maija, and two children, son Toivo Toikka, who plays in band called "Security" and acts in amateur movies, and daughter named Tuuli. simplified_primary_school:
We studied how shaping a beam of fast tiny particles (atoms) changes the patterns they make when they bounce off crystal surfaces. This bouncing is called 'grazing scattering'. We used a special way of thinking about it, called SIVR.

With SIVR, we describe the incoming atoms very carefully. We use details about how the beam is shaped. These details tell us how much of the surface the atoms hit together at the same time.

We used this method to study helium atoms hitting a LiF(001) surface. The atoms first went through a rectangular hole.

Just like in real experiments, our SIVR results showed that the patterns change a lot depending on how wide the hole (slit) is that shapes the atom beam. This was especially true when looking at the patterns from different directions around the surface.

We also found that how long the hole (aperture) is changes the patterns. Longer holes made the patterns even more complex, adding new wavy shapes.
simplified_secondary_school:
We are studying how the way a beam of fast atoms is shaped and focused (its "collimating conditions") affects the diffraction patterns created when these atoms skim across the surface of a crystal. This study uses a special method called the Surface Initial Value Representation (SIVR) approximation, which combines elements of quantum and classical physics.

With the SIVR method, we include a realistic description of the incoming atom, taking into account the collimating parameters. These parameters determine the size of the crystal surface area that the beam illuminates in a way that allows for wave interference.

We applied this model to helium atoms that first passed through a rectangular opening and then collided with a Lithium Fluoride (001) surface. Just as experiments have shown, the SIVR results (called spectra), when measured at different angles around the surface (azimuthal angle), are very sensitive to how wide the collimating slit is. We also discovered that the length of the collimating opening affects how the atoms scatter at different angles relative to the surface (polar angle distributions). Longer collimating slits introduce additional interference patterns in these distributions.

This style of socks has also become popular among American teens and college students who are fans of Japanese anime and manga. These socks come in a variety of styles, defined by the knitting pattern of the upper portion of the sock. The two most popular styles are the traditional 2×2 rib knit (pictured) and tube-style loose socks, which are thigh-high length tube socks worn pushed down around the ankles. simplified_kindergarten:
We look at tiny dots.
We see how tiny parts inside them touch.
We compare this to simple old toys.

simplified_primary_school: We look at how tiny electric parts push and pull inside very small lights called quantum dots. We compare this to a simpler way of understanding how things move. This helps us see how energy is organized inside these dots. We found that when these tiny parts push and pull, it makes a system that would normally be simple become very hard to predict. We show an example of two tiny parts moving inside a box with strong walls, like balls on a pool table. We also show that a real quantum dot with three tiny parts, in a flat space, has interesting energy steps. These steps change depending on how much the parts push and pull, and if we use magnets. This work is about quantum dots, how things can be hard to predict (chaos), and how energy steps are arranged. This work was done by Sergio E. Ulloa.
simplified_secondary_school: This research examines how the interactions between electrons affect the energy levels inside semiconductor quantum dots. We analyze this by comparing it to a classical system that models these dots, specifically looking at how orderly or predictable the classical system's behavior is (its "integrability"). We found that when particles interact, a system that would otherwise be simple becomes very unpredictable (strongly "non-integrable") in the classical model. As an example, we present a classical system with two particles confined within a two-dimensional "billiard" with hard walls. Similarly, a related two-dimensional quantum dot problem involving three particles is shown to have interesting energy level characteristics (spectral properties) that change depending on the strength of the particle interactions and any applied magnetic fields.

original_text: In Greek mythology, Electryone (Ancient Greek: Ἠλεκτρυώνην) or Alectrona (Doric form) was a daughter of Helios and Rhodos, and sister to the Heliadae. She died a virgin and was worshipped as a heroine on the island of Rhodes. She was possibly a goddess of the sunrise, or of man's waking sense. The Doric form of her name is akin to the Greek word for "rooster" (Alectrona, the feminine genitive of Αλεκτορ, Alektor, the ancient Greek word for "rooster"), while the Attic form Electryone is akin to the word for "amber" (Ἠλέκτρα, Elektra), as in the amber color of sunrise. A marble tablet from the 3rd century BC found in Ialyssus contains an inscription about the regulations for visitors to the temple of Alectrona. Genealogy Notes References Graves, Robert; The Greek Myths, Penguin Books Ltd. (1960 edition). 42. c, 4. Smith, William; Dictionary of Greek and Roman Biography and Mythology, London (1873). "Electryo'ne" Numismatic Chronicle, Volume 18 Greek goddesses Greek Virgin goddesses Greek mythological heroes Children of Helios Demigods in classical mythology Women in Greek mythology Rhodian characters in Greek mythology Characters in Greek mythology
simplified_kindergarten:
In old Greek stories, there was a girl. Her name was Electryone. Some people called her Alectrona. Her dad was Helios. Her mom was Rhodos. She had brothers called the Heliadae.

She never got married. People on Rhodes island thought she was special. They honored her.

Maybe she was a goddess. She might have been the goddess of the sun coming up. Or she helped people wake up.

Her name Alectrona sounds like the Greek word for "rooster." Her name Electryone sounds like the word for "amber." Amber is a color like the sun when it comes up.

A very old stone tablet was found. It was in a place called Ialyssus. It had words on it. The words told people what to do at Alectrona's temple.
simplified_primary_school:
In old Greek stories, Electryone, or Alectrona as some called her, was the daughter of Helios and Rhodos. She was also the sister of the Heliadae.

She never married or had children. People honored her like a hero on the island of Rhodes.

Maybe she was a goddess of the sunrise, or of when people wake up.

Her name Alectrona sounds like the Greek word for 'rooster'. And her name Electryone sounds like the word for 'amber'. Amber is a color like the sunrise.

A marble stone from a very old time (the 3rd century BC) was found in a place called Ialyssus. It had writing on it. The writing told people the rules for visiting Alectrona's temple.
simplified_secondary_school:
In Greek mythology, Electryone, also known by her Doric name Alectrona, was the daughter of the sun god Helios and the nymph Rhodos. She was also a sister to the Heliadae.

Electryone died a virgin and was honored as a heroine on the island of Rhodes. She may have been a goddess associated with the sunrise or with the human sense of waking up.

The Doric form of her name, Alectrona, is related to the ancient Greek word for "rooster." Her Attic name, Electryone, is connected to the Greek word for "amber," possibly referring to the amber-like colors seen at sunrise.

Evidence of her worship includes a marble tablet from the 3rd century BC, discovered in Ialyssus, which details the rules for visitors to Alectrona's temple.

original_text: (CNN) -- A day after North Korea test-fired two missiles, South Korea captured a fishing boat from the North that had crossed into South Korean waters, officials say. The boat crossed the sea demarcation line that separates the two Koreas and was captured by the South Korean navy Thursday, the South Korean Ministry of Defense said. The action comes as tensions between the two Koreas are rising once again. On Wednesday, North Korea tested two medium-range ballistic missiles, firing them into the ocean. South Korea says it believes the missile tests are a reaction to ongoing U.S.-South Korean military drills. North Korea had harsh words for the South, accusing it of provocations. "The puppet military gangsters perpetrated such provocations as firing bullets and shells from the areas around the five islands including Paekryong and Big Yonphyong islands and scattering leaflets hurting the dignity of the supreme leadership of the DPRK on March 24," the North Korean news agency KCNA reported. North Korea ups stakes with latest missile launch . Report: North Korea fires two missiles off eastern coast .

People say this happened.

The boat went over a line in the sea.
This line keeps North and South Korea apart.
South Korea's navy caught the boat on Thursday.
South Korea's army leaders said this.

North and South Korea are not getting along.
They are getting more angry.

On Wednesday, North Korea shot two big rockets.
They went into the sea.

South Korea thinks North Korea shot rockets because the U.S. and South Korea are practicing war games.

North Korea said mean things to South Korea.
North Korea said South Korea made them angry.

North Korea's news said South Korea's army shot guns and bombs near some islands.
They also threw papers that made North Korea's leader sad.
This was on March 24.

They said South Korea shot guns and bombs near islands like Paekryong and Big Yonphyong. They also said South Korea threw papers that said mean things about North Korea's leader.
