original_text: integral field spectroscopy ( ifs ) provides a spectrum simultaneously for each spatial sample of an extended , two - dimensional field . it consists of an integral field unit ( ifu ) which slices and re - arranges the initial field along the entrance slit of a spectrograph . this article presents an original design of ifu based on the advanced image slicer concept @xcite . to reduce optical aberrations , pupil and slit mirrors are disposed in a fan - shaped configuration that means that angles between incident and reflected beams on each elements are minimized . the fan - shaped image slicer improves image quality in terms of wavefront error by a factor 2 comparing with classical image slicer and , furthermore it guaranties a negligible level of differential aberration in the field . as an exemple , we are presenting the design lam used for its proposal at the nirspec / ifu invitation of tender .
simplified_kindergarten:
IFS shows light colors.
It shows them for every tiny spot.
It does this at the same time.
It looks at a big, flat area.

It has an IFU.
The IFU cuts the area.
It moves the cut pieces.
It puts them on a tiny opening.
This opening is on a light machine.

We made a new IFU.
It uses a special way to cut pictures.

We want clear pictures.
So, we put small mirrors.
They are shaped like a fan.
This helps the light bounce well.
It makes the picture clear.

This fan-shaped cutter makes pictures much better.
It is twice as good as old cutters.
All parts of the picture look clear.
No blurry spots.

For example, we used this for a plan.
It was for a special IFU project.
It was called NIRSpec.
simplified_primary_school:
IFS is a special way to look at light. It shows us all the different colors of light from every tiny spot in a big, flat area, all at the same time. It uses a part called an IFU. This IFU cuts the picture into thin strips and lines them up so they can go into another machine called a spectrograph.

This paper talks about a new way to make an IFU. It uses an idea called an 'advanced image slicer'. To make the picture clearer and stop blurry parts, small mirrors are placed like a fan. This means the light bounces off the mirrors at very small angles, which helps keep the picture clear.

This fan-shaped slicer makes the picture much better. It makes the light waves twice as good as older slicers. Also, it makes sure there are almost no blurry parts that are different across the picture. For example, we show the design called 'LAM' that was suggested for a project called NIRSpec/IFU.
simplified_secondary_school:
Integral Field Spectroscopy (IFS) is a technique that collects a spectrum for every point across a wide, two-dimensional area all at the same time. It uses a special component called an Integral Field Unit (IFU). This IFU cuts the image into thin slices and then rearranges them so they fit along the narrow opening (slit) of a spectrograph.

This article describes a new design for an IFU that uses an advanced version of an "image slicer." To make sure the image is very clear and doesn't have distortions (called optical aberrations), the mirrors that shape the light (pupil and slit mirrors) are arranged in a fan shape. This design minimizes the angles at which light hits and reflects off each mirror.

This fan-shaped image slicer improves the quality of the image by reducing errors in the light waves (wavefront error) by half compared to older image slicers. Additionally, it ensures there are almost no differences in distortion across the entire image area. For example, we are showing the design that was proposed for the NIRSpec/IFU project.

Scientists found that when they tried to figure out how many tiny particles called protons come from space, it looked different. They did this by looking at other tiny particles called muons, found on the ground and deep underground. They used different computer programs (like qgsjet 01, qgsjet ii, nexus 3.97, and sibyll 2.1) to guess how particles crash into each other. The number of protons and their pattern changed depending on which computer program they used.

To get the right pattern for the muons, the number of protons from space needs to have a pattern that changes. This is true for all the computer programs, except for one called sibyll 2.1. This changing pattern should have a sudden break when the protons have a very, very high amount of energy (around 10^15 TeV). The pattern before this break should be like what another experiment (atic-2) found.

To be sure this sudden change is real, scientists need to better understand how particles act when protons hit air in a certain way. When scientists reconstruct the primary proton spectrum (the energy distribution of protons from space) using data from muons detected at sea-level and underground, they rely on different interaction models like QGSJET 01, QGSJET II, NEXUS 3.97, and SIBYLL 2.1. This reconstruction shows that both the strength (intensity) and the shape of the proton spectrum depend on which model is used.

To accurately match the observed shape of the muon spectrum, the flow of primary protons needs to have an energy distribution that doesn't follow a simple, constant pattern. For all models except SIBYLL 2.1, this means there should be a "break" (a sudden change in slope) in the proton energy distribution at energies around 10^15 TeV. The slope of this distribution before the break should be similar to what was measured by the ATIC-2 experiment.

To confirm if this "break" truly exists, scientists need a better understanding of how particles are produced when protons collide with air molecules in the atmosphere, especially in the "fragmentation region" (the part of the collision where the original proton breaks up). However, current experimental data on primary nuclei, and on muons and hadrons in the atmosphere, is not sufficient to achieve this improved understanding.

original_text: ''' is the probability distribution of the value of signal fading, relative to a specified reference level. In the case of phase interference fading, the time distribution of the instantaneous field strength usually approximates a Rayleigh distribution when several signal components of equal amplitude are present. The field strength is usually measured in volts per meter. The fading distribution may also be measured in terms of power level, where the unit of measure is usually watts per square meter and the expression is in decibels. References Radio frequency propagation fading
simplified_kindergarten:
This is about how a signal gets weak. It shows how much the signal changes. It also shows how often it changes. We compare it to a normal signal.

Sometimes, signals get weak because of other signals. Then, how strong the signal is at different times often looks like a special pattern. This happens when many signals are there, and they are all the same strength.

We measure how strong the signal is. We use 'volts per meter' to do this.

We can also measure how much power the signal has. We use 'watts per square meter' for this. We also talk about it in 'decibels'.
simplified_primary_school:
It shows how often a signal gets weaker, compared to a normal level.

When signals get weaker because their waves crash into each other, the strength of the signal at any moment often looks like a special pattern called a Rayleigh distribution. This happens when many parts of the signal are there and they are all equally strong.

We usually measure how strong the signal is in 'volts per meter'.

We can also measure how much the signal gets weaker by its power. We usually use 'watts per square meter' for this, and we talk about it in 'decibels'.
simplified_secondary_school: A fading distribution shows the likelihood of different signal fading values, measured against a specific reference level. When fading occurs due to phase interference, and several signal components of equal amplitude are present, the instantaneous field strength over time usually approximates a Rayleigh distribution. Field strength is typically measured in volts per meter. The fading distribution can also be measured in terms of power level, where the unit is usually watts per square meter and the measurement is expressed in decibels.

We use a special computer program called Superbox to see this very clearly. We especially want to find out if these super-clusters join together, and how they do it. Then we look closely at the new thing they make when they join. This new thing can look like a small, round ball. Or it can be long and 'fluffy' like some small galaxies called dSph galaxies. We are investigating how a group of young, massive star clusters—which we refer to as a 'super-cluster'—behaves when influenced by the gravitational pull of a larger host galaxy. To do this, we use a highly detailed computer simulation program called Superbox.

Our specific aim is to determine if and how these super-clusters merge together. We then conduct a thorough study of the object that results from this merger.

The resulting merged object can take one of two forms: either a compact, spherical shape, or an elongated, 'fluffy' shape similar to dwarf spheroidal (dsph) galaxies. Which shape it takes depends on how concentrated the super-cluster was initially.

simplified_primary_school:
NDC or ndc can mean many different things.

It can be a special code for Nanded Airport in India.

For schools and learning, NDC can mean a National Defence College. There are different ones. It can also mean the NATO Defence College in Rome, Italy. Or it can be Notre Dame College in Dhaka, Bangladesh, or Notre Dame College in Mymensingh, Bangladesh.

For governments, NDC can mean the National Defence Commission in North Korea. It can also mean the National Development Council in India, or the National Development Council in Taiwan. Sometimes it means a big meeting called the National Dialogue Conference, which was about problems in Yemen.

NDC can also mean "Nationally Determined Contribution." This is about what countries promise to do to help the Earth's weather. Or it can be "New Deal for Communities" in the UK. This was a plan to help neighborhoods.

For health, NDC can mean an old health company called NDC Health. It can also mean a National Drug Code, which is a special number for medicines in the US.

In politics, NDC can mean a political party in Ghana called the National Democratic Congress. It can also mean a political party in Grenada, also called the National Democratic Congress. It might be a group of people in the U.S. Democratic Party called the New Democrat Coalition. Or it can be the North District Council, which helps run a part of Hong Kong.

Other times, NDC can mean the National Development Complex in Pakistan. It can be a way airlines share information, called New Distribution Capability. It can also be a special contract in Welsh Rugby Union, called National Dual Contract. Or it can be a place where mail is sorted for the United States Postal Service, called a Network Distribution Center. It can mean a way to sort books in Japanese libraries, called Nippon Decimal Classification. It can also be a group of fighters called Nduma Defense of Congo. Lastly, it can be a song from 2002 by an Australian band named Jebediah, called N.D.C.

Louie McCarthy-Scarsbrook of St Helens lifts the Super League trophy aloft after the Grand Final victory . ouie McCarthy-Scarsbrook (centre) of St Helens celebrates his side's opening try scored by Sia Soliola . simplified_secondary_school:
UIN can refer to different things:

**Types of Identification Numbers:**

*   **Common User Identification Number:** This is an ID used in many computer security systems and services, such as ICQ. It helps identify a unique user in a computing system.
*   **Universal Identification Number:** This is used to identify people. Examples include national identification numbers or India's Permanent Account Number (PAN), which is managed by the Unique Identification Authority of India.
*   **University Identification Number (UID):** Universities use this to identify their students, faculty (teachers), and alumni (former students).
*   **Unit Identification Number:** This term has two main uses:
    *   In the military, it identifies a specific military unit.
    *   The Department of Education uses it, for example, within the Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System.
*   **Unique Identification Number:** This is similar to the Universal Identification Number, used for both people and inventory. It is often replaced by a Universally Unique Identifier (UUID).

**Other Meanings of UIN:**

*   **User Intelligent Network:** This refers to a type of network that uses artificial intelligence, such as in personal communications networks.
*   It is also the airport code for Quincy Regional Airport in Illinois, United States.

original_text: Sandy (San-Dŷ meaning saint house) is an area in the county of Carmarthenshire in south-west Wales, on the western border of Llanelli town, about 5 miles east of Burry Port. The area is home to Sandy Water Park, a large project which has seen acres of disused industrial land converted to parkland and a lake. It now forms a part of the more recent Millennium Coastal Park. Sandy is adjacent to Stradey, which once contained the famous Stradey Park stadium, former home to Llanelli RFC and the Llanelli Scarlets. original_text: "Orwellian" is an adjective describing a situation, idea, or societal condition that George Orwell identified as being destructive to the welfare of a free and open society. It denotes an attitude and a brutal policy of draconian control by propaganda, surveillance, disinformation, denial of truth (doublethink), and manipulation of the past, including the "unperson"—a person whose past existence is expunged from the public record and memory, practiced by modern repressive governments. Often, this includes the circumstances depicted in his novels, particularly Nineteen Eighty-Four but political doublespeak is criticized throughout his work, such as in Politics and the English Language. The New York Times has said the term is "the most widely used adjective derived from the name of a modern writer". This often looks like things in his books, especially "Nineteen Eighty-Four." But he also talked about "political doublespeak" (when leaders say things that hide the truth) in many of his writings, like "Politics and the English Language."

original_text: this is the second paper in a series on light scattering from optically anisotropic scatterers embedded in an isotropic medium . the apparently complex t - matrix theory involving mixing of angular momentum components turns out to be an efficient approach to calculating scattering in these systems . we present preliminary results of numerical calculations of the scattering by spherical droplets in some simple cases . the droplets contain optically anisotropic material with local radial or uniform anisotropy . we concentrate on cases in which the scattering is due only to the local optical anisotropy within the scatterer . for radial anisotropy we find non - monotonic dependence of the scattering cross - section on the degree of anisotropy can occur in a regime for which both the rayleigh and semi - classical theories are inapplicable . for uniform anisotropy the cross - section is strongly dependent on the angle between the incident light and the optical axis , and for larger droplets this dependence is non - monotonic .

It is about light.
Light bounces off things.

This math idea works well.

We used a computer.
We found new things.
We looked at how light bounces off round drops.
We looked at easy examples.

The drops have special stuff inside.
This stuff is not the same all over.
It can be not the same from the middle out.
Or it can be not the same in one way.

We only looked at times when light bounces because of the special stuff inside the drop.

When the stuff is not the same from the middle out:
How much light bounces changes in a funny way.
This happens when other old math ideas do not work.

When the stuff is not the same in one way:
How much light bounces changes a lot.
It changes with how the light hits it.
For bigger drops, it changes in a funny way.

This is the second story about how light bounces off special tiny things. These tiny things are hidden inside something else that light goes through the same way everywhere.

A math idea that looks hard helps us figure out how light bounces off these special things. It's a good way to do it.

We show what we found first by using computers to do math. We looked at how light bounces off round drops in some easy examples.

These drops have special stuff inside them. This special stuff is either arranged like spokes on a wheel, or it all points in the same direction.

We only looked at times when the light bounced off because of the special stuff inside the drops.

When the special stuff is like spokes on a wheel, we saw that how much light bounces off doesn't just go up or down smoothly. This happens when other old ideas about light don't work.

When the special stuff all points in the same direction, how much light bounces off changes a lot. It depends on the angle between the light hitting it and the direction the special stuff points. For bigger drops, this change doesn't just go up or down smoothly.
simplified_secondary_school:
This is the second paper in a series investigating how light scatters from objects that are 'optically anisotropic' (meaning light behaves differently depending on its direction inside them) when these objects are placed in a 'uniform' surrounding medium. The 't-matrix theory,' which might seem complicated because it deals with combining different ways light can interact (called 'angular momentum components'), has proven to be an efficient way to calculate how light scatters in these systems. We present early results from computer calculations of light scattering by spherical droplets in a few basic scenarios. These droplets are made of optically anisotropic material, where the anisotropy is arranged either 'radially' (like spokes from the center) or 'uniformly' (the same in all directions throughout the droplet). We focus on situations where the light scattering happens *only* because of this local optical anisotropy inside the droplet.

When the anisotropy is radial, we found that the 'scattering cross-section' (a measure of how much light is scattered) doesn't change smoothly with the 'degree of anisotropy' (how strong the anisotropy is). Instead, it can go up and down in a complex way. This happens in conditions where simpler theories, like 'Rayleigh' and 'semi-classical' theories, don't apply. For uniform anisotropy, the scattering cross-section depends strongly on the angle between the incoming light and the droplet's 'optical axis' (a special direction within the material). For larger droplets, this dependence on the angle is also non-monotonic, meaning it doesn't change smoothly.

original_text: MOSCOW, Russia -- Russia declared an end Thursday to its 10-year anti-terror "operation" in the autonomous republic of Chechnya. Russian forces have been operating in Chechnya since Boris Yeltsin's order in 1999. The end to the offensive could see the withdrawal of thousands of troops from the Muslim-majority region, where Russia has fought two wars since the collapse of the Soviet Union in December 1991. The head of the Federal Security Service "canceled the decree declaring a counterterrorist operation in the territory of the republic as of midnight of April 16," Russia's anti-terror committee said. It said it did so to create "the conditions for the future normalization of the situation in the republic, its reconstruction and development of its socio-economic sphere," it said in a statement. The late president Boris Yeltsin ordered the counter-terrorist operation in 1999. Since then, the region has been relatively stable.

original_text: The Springfield/Ozark Mountain Ducks were a minor league team that previously played in Ozark, Missouri, a suburb of Springfield. The team was a member of the Texas-Louisiana League, later named the Central Baseball League from 1998 to 2003. The team played in Price Cutter Park. The Mountain Ducks folded after the 2004 season, with the rights to the club's spot in the CBL being sold to the Pensacola Pelicans of the discontinued Southeastern League. original_text: Fexhe-le-Haut-Clocher (; ) is a municipality of Wallonia located in the province of Liège, Belgium. On January 1, 2006, Fexhe-le-Haut-Clocher had a total population of 3,019. original_text: this letter considers the problem of signal denoising using a sparse tight - frame analysis prior . the @xmath0 norm has been extensively used as a regularizer to promote sparsity ; however , it tends to under - estimate non - zero values of the underlying signal . to more accurately estimate non - zero values , we propose the use of a non - convex regularizer , chosen so as to ensure convexity of the objective function . the convexity of the objective function is ensured by constraining the parameter of the non - convex penalty . we use admm to obtain a solution and show how to guarantee that admm converges to the global optimum of the objective function . we illustrate the proposed method for 1d and 2d signal denoising .
simplified_kindergarten:
This paper talks about cleaning signals. It uses a special rule to do it.
People use a math rule a lot. It helps make signals simple.
But this rule makes some signal parts too small.
To get the right numbers, we use a new rule. This rule is non-convex.
We picked it so the main math problem is convex.
We make sure the main math problem stays convex. We do this by limiting a part of our non-convex rule.
We use a tool called ADMM to find the answer.
We show ADMM always finds the best answer for the main math problem.
We show how our new way cleans signals. It works for line signals and picture signals.
simplified_primary_school:
This paper talks about how to make signals clear. It uses a special math idea called a 'sparse tight-frame analysis prior' to do this.

A math tool called the 'L0 norm' has been used a lot to help make signals sparse. But it often makes the important parts of the real signal seem smaller than they truly are.

To guess these important parts more correctly, we suggest using a different math tool called a 'non-convex regularizer'. We chose it carefully so that the main math problem we are trying to solve stays 'convex' (which means it's easier to find the best answer).

We make sure the main math problem stays convex by putting limits on a setting for our new math tool.

We use a method called ADMM to find an answer. We also show how to make sure that ADMM always finds the very best answer for the main math problem.

We show how our new method works for making 1D and 2D signals clear.
simplified_secondary_school:
This paper looks at the problem of removing noise from signals. It uses a specific mathematical assumption about the signal's structure, called a sparse tight-frame analysis prior. The @xmath0 norm is often used in these methods to encourage signals to be 'sparse' (meaning they have many zero values). However, it often makes the non-zero parts of the signal seem smaller than they actually are.

To get a more accurate estimate of these non-zero values, we suggest using a different kind of mathematical tool, called a non-convex regularizer. This new tool is carefully chosen so that the main mathematical problem we're trying to solve (the 'objective function') remains 'convex', which makes it easier to find the best solution. We ensure the objective function stays convex by limiting the possible values of a specific setting (a 'parameter') within our non-convex regularizer.

We use a method called ADMM to find the solution, and we explain how to make sure that ADMM always finds the absolute best possible solution (the 'global optimum') for our problem. We demonstrate how this method works for removing noise from both one-dimensional (1D) and two-dimensional (2D) signals.

original_text: a novel application of the correlation matrix formalism to study dynamics of the financial evolution is presented . this formalism allows to quantify the memory effects as well as some potential repeatable intradaily structures in the financial time - series . the present study is based on the high - frequency deutsche aktienindex ( dax ) data over the time - period between november 1997 and december 1999 and demonstrates a power of the method . in this way two significant new aspects of the dax evolution are identified : ( i ) the memory effects turn out to be sizably shorter than what the standard autocorrelation function analysis seems to indicate and ( ii ) there exist short term repeatable structures in fluctuations that are governed by a distinct dynamics . the former of these results may provide an argument in favour of the market efficiency while the later one may indicate origin of the difficulty in reaching a gaussian limit , expected from the central limit theorem , in the distribution of returns on longer time - horizons . epsf , , , ,
simplified_kindergarten:
We have a new way to study money.
It helps us see how money changes.
It helps us see if old changes affect new changes.
It also helps us find patterns that happen every day.
We looked at numbers from DAX.
These numbers were from 1997 to 1999.
Our new way works well.
We found two new things about DAX.
First, old changes do not affect DAX for very long. It is a shorter time than we thought.
Second, some small changes in DAX happen again and again. They move in their own special way.
The first thing might mean the market is fair.
The second thing might show why money changes do not always look a certain way over a long time.
simplified_primary_school:
A new way to use a math tool is shown. It helps us understand how money markets change.

This tool can measure how things that happened in the past still affect money today. It can also find patterns that happen again and again in one day in money market numbers.

This study looked at very detailed numbers from the German stock market, called DAX. It used numbers from November 1997 to December 1999. This showed how good the new way is.

With this, two important new things were found about how the DAX changes:
1. How long things from the past affect the future is much shorter than other common ways of looking at it suggest.
2. There are small patterns that happen again and again when prices move. These patterns follow their own special rules.

The first finding might mean that the market learns new things very quickly. The second finding might explain why it's hard for money market changes over longer times to look like a special bell-shaped curve, which a math rule says they should.
simplified_secondary_school:
This study introduces a new way to use a mathematical tool called the correlation matrix to understand how financial markets change. This method helps measure 'memory effects' – how past events influence future ones – and identify patterns that might repeat within a single trading day in financial data. The research uses detailed, high-frequency data from the German stock market index (DAX) collected between November 1997 and December 1999, demonstrating the method's effectiveness.

Using this approach, two important new findings about the DAX's behavior were identified:
(i) The 'memory effects' (how long past events influence future ones) are actually much shorter than what standard autocorrelation function analysis usually suggests.
(ii) There are short-term patterns that repeat within the market's ups and downs, and these patterns follow their own unique rules.

The first finding (shorter memory effects) could support the idea that financial markets are efficient. The second finding (repeatable patterns) might explain why market returns over longer periods don't always follow the 'normal' bell-curve distribution (a Gaussian limit) that the Central Limit Theorem often predicts.

original_text: we revisit a one - step control problem over an adversarial packet - dropping link . the link is modeled as a set of binary channels controlled by a strategic jammer whose intention is to wage a ` denial of service ' attack on the plant by choosing a most damaging channel - switching strategy . the paper introduces a class of zero - sum games between the jammer and controller as a scenario for such attack , and derives necessary and sufficient conditions for these games to have a nontrivial saddle - point equilibrium . at this equilibrium , the jammer s optimal policy is to randomize in a region of the plant s state space , thus requiring the controller to undertake a nontrivial response which is different from what one would expect in a standard stochastic control problem over a packet dropping channel .
simplified_kindergarten:
We look at a problem. We want to control something. It is on a bad path that drops messages.

The path has many small parts. A bad guy controls them. He is smart. He wants to stop our machine. He picks the worst way to use the parts.

This paper talks about a game. A bad guy plays. Our controller plays too. One wins, one loses. This is how the bad guy attacks. The paper tells us what must be true for the game to have a clear end.

When the game ends, the bad guy acts in a random way. He does this to our machine. So our controller must do something new. This is not what we thought before. It is different from other problems with bad paths.
simplified_primary_school:
We are looking again at a problem where we try to control something quickly. This happens on a connection that tries to stop our messages from getting through.

We imagine this connection is like many on/off paths. A smart bad guy, called a jammer, controls these paths. He wants to stop our machine from working. He does this by picking the worst way to turn the paths on and off.

This paper talks about a type of game between the jammer and the person trying to control. In this game, if one wins, the other loses by the same amount. This game shows how the attack can happen. The paper finds out what needs to be true for these games to have a special, stable way that they end.

When the game reaches this stable point, the jammer's best plan is to act randomly in certain parts of how the machine is doing. This means the person controlling has to do something special and not simple. This is different from what you would usually expect in a normal problem where messages might get lost by chance.
simplified_secondary_school: We are studying a specific type of control problem where decisions are made one step at a time. This problem involves sending data over a link that an enemy tries to disrupt by dropping packets. The data link is imagined as several on/off channels. An intelligent enemy, called a jammer, controls these channels. The jammer's goal is to launch a "denial of service" attack on the system (which we call the "plant") by choosing the most damaging way to switch these channels on and off. This paper introduces a type of "zero-sum game" to model this attack, where the jammer and the system's controller are the players. It then finds the exact conditions required for these games to have a specific, non-obvious stable solution, known as a "saddle-point equilibrium." At this stable point, the jammer's best strategy is to act randomly within certain possible conditions of the system. This forces the controller to respond in a complex way, which is different from what would be expected in a typical control problem where data is lost randomly.

Petty Officer Jonathan Campos, a sailor accused of murder, was found dead in his cell at a military jail in southern California, officials announced Friday. A military statement confirmed that Campos died from self-inflicted asphyxiation in the brig at Marine Corps Base Camp Pendleton in San Diego.

Campos had been charged with the murder of Seaman August Provost, who was shot to death on June 30. Provost was killed while standing guard as a sentry for the Assault Craft Unit 5 compound at Camp Pendleton. Authorities also reported that Provost's body was set on fire at his guard post.

Provost's family believed that his sexual orientation was a reason for his killing. However, a spokesman for Camp Pendleton, where the shooting occurred, stated that early in the investigation, there was no indication the killing was a hate crime.

Along with the murder charge, Campos was facing charges related to drug use, larceny, burglary, theft, arson, and the unlawful handling of a deceased individual.

Quintus Fabius Vibulanus became an important leader (a consul) in the Roman Republic. It was the first of three times he would be a consul.

In a place called Sicily, Hiero I, who was the ruler (Tyrant) of Syracuse, died. His brother, Thrasybulus, then became the ruler (Tyrant) of Syracuse.

A writer named Aeschylus wrote a play called 'Seven Against Thebes.' He won a prize called the Dionysia for it. Another play by Aeschylus, called 'The Persians,' was shown in Sicily.

The year 467 BC was part of the pre-Julian Roman calendar system. At that time, it was officially known as the Year of the Consulship of Mamercus and Vibulanus. Less frequently, it was referred to as year 287 Ab urbe condita. The designation "467 BC" for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the main method for naming years across Europe.

During this year, several important events occurred. In the Roman Republic, Quintus Fabius Vibulanus began his first of three terms as consul. In Sicily, Thrasybulus became the Tyrant of Syracuse following the death of his brother, Hiero I.

In the world of literature, the playwright Aeschylus wrote "Seven Against Thebes" and won the Dionysia festival. Another of Aeschylus's plays, "The Persians," was produced in Sicily. Astronomers also recorded the first known sighting of Halley's Comet.

Among the notable deaths of the year was Hiero I, the Tyrant of Syracuse.

Nestorianism is a Christian belief system, or Christological doctrine, developed by Nestorius. This doctrine led to a major disagreement known as the Nestorian controversy and a split called the Nestorian Schism. In 431, the Council of Ephesus officially condemned Nestorianism as a heresy.

The terms "Nestorian" or "Nestorians" can also refer to several other things:

*   The Church of the East, which originated in the Sassanid Empire. This church once accepted Nestorian doctrine and separated from mainstream Christian orthodoxy during the Nestorian Schism.
*   The Assyrian Church of the East.
*   The Ancient Church of the East.
*   The "Nestorian" script, also called East Syriac Maḏnḥāyā, which is a form of the Syriac alphabet.
*   The "Nestorian" Stele, a historical monument located in China.

The Nestorian controversy was one of several Christological controversies that took place during the 4th and 5th centuries, focusing on the nature of Christ. It's important to note that "Church of the East" can refer to several different churches. While they all trace their history back to the original Church of the East, they have developed different beliefs over time.

simplified_secondary_school:
Chelsea, a Barclays Premier League club, has announced that goalkeeper Mark Schwarzer has signed a new one-year contract. The 41-year-old former Australia international joined Chelsea last summer on a free transfer from west London neighbours Fulham. He was brought in to provide back-up for first-choice goalkeeper Petr Cech. Last season, Schwarzer made 12 starts and one substitute appearance, which was in the Champions League semi-final first leg against Atletico Madrid.

Earlier this week, Chelsea confirmed that Portuguese goalkeeper Hilario will leave the club when his contract ends. Defender Ashley Cole and England midfielder Frank Lampard are also departing Chelsea this summer. However, Blues manager Jose Mourinho has already started to rebuild his squad. He has signed former Arsenal captain Cesc Fabregas from Barcelona. Additionally, Atletico Madrid's Spain international forward Diego Costa, valued at around £32 million, is another player Chelsea is reportedly targeting.

simplified_primary_school:
The year 96 BC was a time when Romans used their own way to count years. Back then, people called it the Year of the Consulship of Ahenobarbus and Longinus. This was because two important leaders, Ahenobarbus and Longinus, were in charge. Sometimes, it was also called year 658 Ab urbe condita.

We call it 96 BC now. People started using this name much later, in the early Middle Ages. That's when people in Europe began using the Anno Domini calendar, which is how we name years today.

Here are some things that happened:

**Roman Republic**
The leaders (called Consuls) were Gaius Cassius Longinus and Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus.
The king of Cyrene, Ptolemy Apion, gave his land to the Roman people.

**Greece**
Seleucus VI Epiphanes became king of the Seleucid Empire. This happened after his father, Antiochus VIII Grypus, died. Seleucus also won a battle against Antiochus IX Cyzicenus.

**Asia**
A new time called the Taishi era began in the Han Dynasty.

**People born this year:**
Galeria Copiola, a Roman dancer. She died in 9 AD.

**People who died this year:**
Antiochus VIII Grypus, who was a king of the Seleucid Empire.
Antiochus IX Cyzicenus, who was also a king of the Seleucid Empire.
Gongsun Ao, a Chinese general from the Han Dynasty.
Ptolemy Apion, who was the king of Cyrenaica (which is now called Libya).
simplified_secondary_school:
The year 96 BC was part of the pre-Julian Roman calendar. At that time, Romans knew it as the Year of the Consulship of Ahenobarbus and Longinus, or less often, as year 658 Ab urbe condita (from the founding of the city). The name "96 BC" for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar system became the main way to name years in Europe.

**Events:**

*   **Roman Republic:** Gaius Cassius Longinus and Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus served as consuls. Ptolemy Apion, the ruler of Cyrene, left his territory to the people of the Roman Republic.
*   **Greece:** Seleucus VI Epiphanes became king of the Seleucid Empire. He took the throne after his father, Antiochus VIII Grypus, died, and after he defeated Antiochus IX Cyzicenus in battle.
*   **Asia:** The Taishi era began in the Han Dynasty of China.

**Births:**

*   Galeria Copiola, a Roman dancer (died in 9 AD).

**Deaths:**

*   Antiochus VIII Grypus, king of the Seleucid Empire.
*   Antiochus IX Cyzicenus, king of the Seleucid Empire.
*   Gongsun Ao, a Chinese general of the Han Dynasty.
*   Ptolemy Apion, king of Cyrenaica (modern Libya).

original_text: we establish a general relation between dispersion forces . first , based on qed in causal media , leading - order perturbation theory is used to express both the single - atom casimir - polder and the two - atom van der waals potentials in terms of the atomic polarizabilities and the green tensor for the body - assisted electromagnetic field . endowed with this geometry - independent framework , we then employ the born expansion of the green tensor together with the clausius - mosotti relation to prove that the macroscopic casimir - polder potential of an atom in the presence of dielectric bodies is due to an infinite sum of its microscopic many - atom van der waals interactions with the atoms comprising the bodies . this theorem holds for inhomogeneous , dispersing , and absorbing bodies of arbitrary shapes and arbitrary atomic composition on an arbitrary background of additional magnetodielectric bodies .
simplified_kindergarten:
We look at how some forces are linked.

First, we use special science rules. These rules help us talk about forces.
One force is for one tiny atom. We call it Casimir-Polder.
Another force is for two tiny atoms. We call it van der Waals.
We can tell how strong these forces are. We use how atoms can stretch. And we use how light and energy are around other things.

Using these ideas that work for all shapes, we then use more special math tools.
We show that the big Casimir-Polder force for one atom, when other things are near, comes from adding up many, many, many tiny van der Waals forces.
These tiny forces are between that atom and all the tiny atoms inside those other things.

This is true for all kinds of things.
Things can be any shape. They can be made of any tiny atoms.
And it works even if other special things are around them.
simplified_primary_school:
We found a general rule about dispersion forces, which are forces that pull tiny things together. First, using a special science idea about how light and matter work, we used a math trick. This trick helped us show how strong the Casimir-Polder force (for one tiny atom) and the van der Waals force (for two tiny atoms) are. We showed this by looking at how easily atoms can be wiggled and by using a special map of how light and energy move around things.

With this way of thinking that works no matter what shape things are, we then used more special math ideas. We used them to show that the big Casimir-Polder force on one atom, when it's near other things made of special materials, is really made up of endless tiny van der Waals forces. These tiny forces happen between that one atom and all the other tiny atoms inside those other things.

This rule works for things that are different inside, that can spread light, or soak it up. It works no matter what shape they are or what kinds of atoms they are made of. And it works even if there are other special things around them.
simplified_secondary_school:
We establish a general relationship between dispersion forces. First, using a fundamental approximation method called leading-order perturbation theory, based on Quantum Electrodynamics (QED) in causal media (where effects always follow causes), we describe both the force on a single atom near a body (the Casimir-Polder potential) and the force between two atoms (the van der Waals potentials). These forces are expressed in terms of how easily an atom's electron cloud can be distorted (atomic polarizabilities) and a mathematical tool called the Green tensor, which describes the electromagnetic field influenced by surrounding objects.

Using this framework, which works independently of the objects' specific shapes, we then apply specific mathematical techniques: the Born expansion of the Green tensor and the Clausius-Mosotti relation. This allows us to prove that the overall Casimir-Polder potential – the force on an atom in the presence of larger dielectric bodies – is actually the result of an infinite sum of its microscopic van der Waals interactions with every individual atom that makes up those bodies.

This theorem applies to bodies that are inhomogeneous (not uniform), dispersing (interact with light differently at different frequencies), and absorbing. It holds true for objects of any shape and any atomic composition, even when there are additional magnetodielectric bodies in the background.

original_text: Alypius of Antioch was a geographer and a vicarius of Roman Britain, probably in the late 350s AD. He replaced Flavius Martinus after that vicarius' suicide. His rule is recorded is Ammianus XXIII 1, 3. Life He came from Antioch and served under Constantius II and was probably appointed to ensure that nobody with western associations was serving in Britain during a time of mistrust, rebellion and suppression symbolised by the brutal acts of the imperial notary Paulus Catena. He may have had to deal with the insurrection of the usurper named Carausius II. Alypius was afterwards commissioned to rebuild the Temple in Jerusalem as part of Julian's systematic attempt to reverse the Christianization of the Roman Empire by restoring pagan and, in this case, Jewish practices. Among the letters of Julian are two (29 and 30) addressed to Alypius; one inviting him to Rome, the other thanking him for a geographical treatise, which no longer exists. Flavius Martinus had killed himself. We can read about Alypius's time as leader in a book called Ammianus (part XXIII, 1, 3).

Alypius worked for an emperor named Constantius II. He was probably chosen to make sure that no one from the western part of the empire was working in Britain. This was because people did not trust each other then. There were also fights and people were being put down. A man named Paulus Catena did cruel things, which showed how bad this time was. Alypius might have had to stop a fight started by a man named Carausius II, who tried to take power.

Later, Alypius was asked to build the Temple in Jerusalem again. This was part of a plan by an emperor named Julian. Julian wanted to undo the spread of Christianity in the Roman Empire. He wanted to bring back old religions, like pagan and Jewish ways. Emperor Julian wrote two letters (number 29 and 30) to Alypius. In one letter, he asked Alypius to come to Rome. In the other, he thanked Alypius for a book he wrote about maps and the Earth. This book is now lost.
simplified_secondary_school:
Alypius of Antioch was a geographer and a high-ranking Roman official, known as a vicarius, in Roman Britain, probably during the late 350s AD. He took over the position after the previous vicarius, Flavius Martinus, committed suicide. Alypius's time in office is recorded in the historical work of Ammianus (XXIII 1, 3).

He came from Antioch and served under Emperor Constantius II. Alypius was likely appointed to ensure that no officials with ties to the western Roman Empire were serving in Britain. This was a period marked by deep mistrust, rebellion, and harsh suppression, symbolized by the brutal actions of an imperial official named Paulus Catena. Alypius may also have had to deal with a rebellion led by a usurper named Carausius II.

Later, Alypius was commissioned to rebuild the Temple in Jerusalem. This project was part of Emperor Julian's systematic effort to reverse the spread of Christianity in the Roman Empire by restoring pagan and, in this case, Jewish religious practices. Among Emperor Julian's letters are two (numbers 29 and 30) addressed to Alypius: one inviting him to Rome, and the other thanking him for a geographical book he had written, which no longer exists today.

original_text: a method for simulation of elastoplastic solids in multibody systems with nonsmooth and multidomain dynamics is developed . the solid is discretised into pseudo - particles using the meshfree moving least squares method . the particles carry strain and stress tensor variables that are mapped to deformation constraints and constraint forces . the discretised solid model thus fit a unified framework for nonsmooth multidomain dynamics for realtime simulations including strong coupling of rigid multibodies with complex kinematic constraints such as articulation joints , unilateral contacts with dry friction , drivelines and hydraulics . the nonsmooth formulation allow for impulses , due to impacts for instance , to propagate instantly between the rigid multibody and the solid . plasticity is introduced through an associative perfectly plastic modified drucker - prager model . the elastic and plastic dynamics is verified for simple test systems and the capability of simulating tracked terrain vehicles driving on a deformable terrain is demonstrated .
simplified_kindergarten:
We made a way to show how things move. These things can squish and stretch and stay squished or stretched. Many parts move together. They can bump and touch in many places.

We break the big thing into tiny dots. We use a special way to do this. These tiny dots know how much they are squished or pulled. This helps us know how they bend and push each other.

This way helps us show how things move. It works for when things bump and touch. We can see it happen right away. It works with hard things that move together. Like robot arms that bend. Or when things slide and rub. It also works for car engines and water pumps.

Our way lets us show sudden pushes. Like when things crash. The push moves very fast between the hard parts and the squishy parts.

We also show how things can stay bent. We use a special math rule for this.

We checked how well it works for simple things that stretch and bend. And we showed it can make pretend cars with tracks drive on soft ground.
simplified_primary_school: We made a new way for computers to show how squishy and stretchy things move when they are part of many moving pieces, even when things bump or change quickly. We break the squishy thing into many tiny pretend dots using a special computer trick. These tiny dots hold information about how much they are stretching or squishing. This helps the computer know how they should bend and what forces are acting on them. This model of tiny dots fits into a big computer system. This system can show many moving parts, even stiff ones, moving together in real-time. It can show tricky movements like hinges, things rubbing, parts that make cars go, and parts that use liquid power. Because of how we set it up, when things hit each other, the push or bump travels right away between the stiff parts and the squishy part. We made it so the squishy thing can stay squished using a special math rule. We checked that the stretching and squishing parts work well with simple tests. We also showed that it can pretend to have big vehicles with tracks driving on ground that squishes and changes shape.
simplified_secondary_school:
A new method has been developed to simulate materials that can deform both temporarily and permanently (elastoplastic solids) within systems made of many connected parts (multibody systems). This method handles situations where forces change suddenly (nonsmooth dynamics) and where different types of physics interact (multidomain dynamics).

The solid material is broken down into many small, imaginary "pseudo-particles" using a technique called the meshfree moving least squares method. Each of these particles holds information about its deformation (strain) and internal forces (stress). This information is then used to define how the particles can deform and the forces that control their movement.

This particle-based solid model fits into a single system designed for complex, real-time simulations that involve sudden changes and different types of physics. It allows for strong connections between rigid parts, even with complex movement rules like those found in articulation joints, one-way contacts with friction, drivelines, and hydraulic systems. Because of how this method handles sudden changes, forces from impacts (impulses) can instantly travel between the rigid parts and the deformable solid.

The permanent deformation (plasticity) of the material is modeled using a specific mathematical approach called the associative perfectly plastic modified Drucker-Prager model. The way the material deforms both temporarily (elastically) and permanently (plastically) has been tested and confirmed using simple examples. The method's ability to simulate vehicles with tracks, like tanks, driving on ground that can deform, has also been demonstrated.

simplified_primary_school:
Year 3 BC was a normal year. In the Julian calendar, it started on a Wednesday or Thursday. In the Proleptic Julian calendar, it started on a Tuesday.

Back then, people called it the Year of Lentulus and Messalla. Sometimes, they also called it year 751 Ab urbe condita. We call it 3 BC now. People started using '3 BC' a long, long time ago, in the early Middle Ages. That's when the Anno Domini calendar, which we use today, became popular in Europe for naming years.

In the Roman Empire, a king named Maroboduus brought together different groups of people. These groups were called the Marcomanni, Hermunduri, Lombards, Semnoni, and Vandals. They were Germanic tribes. This happened in a place that is now called Bohemia.

A Roman leader named Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus led the Roman army in Germania. He crossed the Elbe River. He also built long bridges over wet, marshy land between the Rhine and Ems rivers.

Two important people were born this year. On December 24, Servius Sulpicius Galba was born. He later became a Roman emperor. He died in AD 69. Seneca the Younger was also born. He was a Roman leader and a thinker. He died in AD 65.

A powerful Chinese empress named Fu died around this time. She was part of the Han Dynasty.
simplified_secondary_school:
The year 3 BC was a common year. According to the Julian calendar, it began on a Wednesday or Thursday (sources differ). In the Proleptic Julian calendar, it began on a Tuesday. During that period, people often referred to it as the Year of the Consulship of Lentulus and Messalla. Less commonly, it was known as year 751 *Ab urbe condita* (from the founding of the city of Rome). The name '3 BC' for this year came into use during the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini (AD) calendar system became the main way to name years in Europe.

**Events**

In the Roman Empire, several key events occurred. King Maroboduus of the Marcomanni organized a group of Germanic tribes, including the Hermunduri, Lombards, Semnoni, and Vandals, in the region that would later be called Bohemia. Meanwhile, the Roman general Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus led the Roman army in Germania, crossing the Elbe River. He also constructed the *pontes longi* ("long bridges") across the marshlands located between the Rhine and Ems rivers.

**Births**

*   December 24 – Servius Sulpicius Galba, who would later become a Roman emperor (died AD 69).
*   Seneca the Younger, a prominent Roman statesman and philosopher (died AD 65).

**Deaths**

*   Fu, a Chinese grand empress of the Han Dynasty (approximate date).

original_text: we present a theoretical analysis of dilute gas bose - einstein condensates with dipolar atomic interactions under rotation in elliptical traps . working in the thomas - fermi limit , we employ the classical hydrodynamic equations to first derive the rotating condensate solutions and then consider their response to perturbations . we thereby map out the regimes of stability and instability for rotating dipolar bose - einstein condensates and in the latter case , discuss the possibility of vortex lattice formation . we employ our results to propose several novel routes to induce vortex lattice formation in a dipolar condensate .

It is very thin.

These rules help us see how the spinning gas acts.
Then we see what happens when we poke it.

We find out when the spinning gas stays still.
We find out when it wiggles.
When it wiggles, we see if it makes little swirls.

We use what we learned.
We show new ways to make the little swirls in the gas.
simplified_primary_school: We looked at a special kind of gas cloud called a Bose-Einstein condensate. This cloud was spinning in a squashed circle, and its tiny parts acted like little magnets. We used a simple rule and math about how liquids flow. This helped us figure out how the spinning cloud would act. Then, we saw what happened when we gently poked it. This helped us find out when the spinning cloud would stay steady or get wobbly. When it got wobbly, we thought about if it could make a pattern of tiny swirls. We used what we found out to suggest new ways to make the pattern of tiny swirls appear in the cloud.
simplified_secondary_school: We theoretically analyzed dilute gas Bose-Einstein condensates. These condensates have dipolar atomic interactions and are rotating within elliptical traps. Using the Thomas-Fermi limit, we applied classical hydrodynamic equations. First, we determined the solutions for these rotating condensates, and then we examined how they respond to disturbances. This allowed us to identify the conditions under which these rotating dipolar Bose-Einstein condensates are stable or unstable. For the unstable cases, we discussed the possibility of a vortex lattice forming. Based on our results, we propose several new ways to create vortex lattices in a dipolar condensate.

original_text: Year 699 (DCXCIX) was a common year starting on Wednesday (link will display the full calendar) of the Julian calendar. The denomination 699 for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the prevalent method in Europe for naming years. Events By place Umayyad Caliphate Umayyad troops invade Armenia, and secure the submission of Prince Smbat VI Bagratuni. The South Caucasus becomes a viceroyalty called al-Arminiya, and is divided into four regions: Caucasian Albania, Caucasian Iberia, the area around the Aras River, and Taron (modern Turkey). Asia June 26 – En no Ozunu, Japanese ascetic, is banished to Izu Ōshima (a volcanic island in the Izu Islands), and accused of confusing the mind of the people with magic. He will be later regarded as the founder of a folk religion called Shugendō. Births Abū Hanīfa, Arab imam and scholar (d. 767) Wang Wei, Chinese poet (d. 759) Deaths Niitabe, Japanese princess Ōe, Japanese princess Seaxburh of Ely, queen of Kent Werburgh, Anglo-Saxon princess Yuge, Japanese prince References Sources

Umayyad soldiers went into Armenia. They made Prince Smbat give up. The South Caucasus land became a big new place. It was called al-Arminiya. It was split into four parts. One part was Caucasian Albania. One part was Caucasian Iberia. One part was near the Aras River. One part was Taron. Taron is in Turkey now.

On June 26, a man named En no Ozunu lived in Japan. He was a holy man. People sent him away to an island. The island was called Izu Ōshima. It was a volcano island. People said he used magic. They said he made people confused. Later, people said he started a special religion. It was called Shugendō.

Some people were born. Abū Hanīfa was born. He was a smart man from Arabia. Wang Wei was born. He was a poet from China.

Soldiers from a place called Umayyad went into Armenia. They made Prince Smbat VI Bagratuni give up and follow their rules. A land called South Caucasus became a new area ruled by the Umayyad people. They called it al-Arminiya. This new area was split into four parts: Caucasian Albania, Caucasian Iberia, the land near the Aras River, and Taron (which is part of Turkey today).

On June 26th, a Japanese man named En no Ozunu was sent away to an island called Izu Ōshima. It was a volcano island. People said he used magic to confuse others. Later, people would say he started a special folk religion called Shugendō.

People born this year were: Abū Hanīfa, an Arab religious leader and smart person, who died in the year 767. Wang Wei, a Chinese poet, who died in the year 759.

People who died this year were: Niitabe, a Japanese princess. Ōe, another Japanese princess. Seaxburh of Ely, a queen from Kent. Werburgh, an Anglo-Saxon princess. Yuge, a Japanese prince.

The year 699 (DCXCIX) was a common year in the Julian calendar, beginning on a Wednesday. This method of numbering years, using the "Anno Domini" calendar era, became the most common way to name years in Europe during the early medieval period.

**Events**

*   **Umayyad Caliphate:** Umayyad troops invaded Armenia and successfully secured the submission of Prince Smbat VI Bagratuni. The South Caucasus region then became a viceroyalty called al-Arminiya. This new territory was divided into four regions: Caucasian Albania, Caucasian Iberia, the area around the Aras River, and Taron (which is in modern-day Turkey).
*   **Asia:** On June 26, En no Ozunu, a Japanese ascetic, was banished to Izu Ōshima, a volcanic island in the Izu Islands. He was accused of confusing people with magic. En no Ozunu is later recognized as the founder of a folk religion called Shugendō.

**Births**

*   Abū Hanīfa, an Arab imam and scholar (died 767).
*   Wang Wei, a Chinese poet (died 759).

**Deaths**

*   Niitabe, a Japanese princess.
*   Ōe, a Japanese princess.
*   Seaxburh of Ely, queen of Kent.
*   Werburgh, an Anglo-Saxon princess.
*   Yuge, a Japanese prince.

original_text: The armónico, also known as trilina, is a guitar-like instrument from Cuba. It is a hybrid between the Spanish guitar and the Cuban tres. The armónico has seven strings: five single strings, plus one pair of strings tuned in unison. The armónico was invented by Cuban trova musician Compay Segundo, who loved the instrument as it allowed him to exploit the possibilities of both the guitar and the tres. Counting from the lowest-pitched, the first three strings, E, A and D, are tuned an octave higher than the equivalent strings on a guitar with standard tuning. The fourth string, G, is doubled as well as also being an octave higher than on a guitar. The remaining two, B and E, are at the same pitch as a standard guitar. Therefore, the bottom E string is only one octave lower than the top E string on the armónico, instead of two octaves lower on the guitar. Gallery See also Trova Son montuno References Guitar family instruments Cuban musical instruments

It is a mix of two other music things. One is a Spanish guitar. The other is a Cuban tres.

Scientists have found evidence of a recent burst of star formation very close to the active center of the galaxy NGC 1097, specifically within 9 parsecs (pc). Signs of this starburst include specific patterns in ultraviolet (UV) light: absorption lines and continuous emission coming from young stars. These patterns were observed using the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) by focusing on a very small area (a small-aperture spectrum).

This starburst is a few million years old. It has a mass of 10^5 solar masses (M☉) and an observed brightness (luminosity) of 1.5 x 10^7 solar luminosities (L☉). It is also obscured, or hidden, by about 6 magnitudes of dust.

This discovery is important for two main reasons:
1. The starburst is very close to the galaxy's active nucleus, suggesting a possible connection between them.

These strings would make special signs in how the universe looks.

Looking for these special signs by seeing the universe in new ways helps us learn more about tiny particles when there was a lot of energy. This is another way to look for new ideas about how the universe works. It is different from looking when there is less energy, like at the LHC machine.

In theories that include strings, a network of them would have inevitably formed during a phase transition in the early universe and would still exist today. These strings would leave unique and identifiable marks on the universe. Looking for these signs using new ways of observing the universe helps us narrow down the possibilities for particle physics theories that describe very high energies. This research complements searches for new physics at lower energy levels, such as those conducted at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). Specifically, this discussion will focus on the signs of cosmic strings visible in cosmic microwave background polarization maps and in 21 cm redshift surveys.

original_text: Islamabad, Pakistan (CNN) -- A suspected U.S. drone strike killed 16 people and wounded six Thursday in one of Pakistan's semi-autonomous tribal regions, two officials said. The attack, which included four missiles, targeted a compound on the border of Orakzai Agency and North Waziristan, a local government official and a military official said. The compound belonged to Maulvi Shakirullah, a militant affiliated with the Haqqani network, the officials said. The network is widely viewed to be fueling the insurgency in Afghanistan . Pakistan's Geo TV, a CNN affiliate, also reported that the drone "fired four missiles at a madrasa," or seminary, run by Shakirullah. A previous drone strike in the Orakzai agency in 2009 killed 11 suspected militants from a faction of the Pakistani Taliban. The United States carries out drone strikes in Pakistan aimed at dismantling terror operations, but generally does not comment on them. U.S. activists in Pakistan to protest drone strikes . 5 killed in first drone strike since protest march . Read more: Why U.S. will live to regret drone strikes .

The word "industry" can mean different things.

In economics, "industry" means a type of work that helps a country make money.
It can also mean making things. This is a special kind of work, usually done in big factories with machines.
It can mean all the businesses that make things in a country. This includes making things in factories, and also making parts for other things, or finished products.
It can also mean the ways people live and make things in a place where many things are made in factories.
It can mean when a place changes to make many things in factories.
Industry classification is a way to sort different kinds of businesses and the work they do.

There are also places named Industry, like towns in Alabama, California, Illinois, Kansas, Maine, Missouri, New York, Pennsylvania, and Texas. There is also Industry station, Industry Bar in New York City, and Industry-Rock Falls Township in Phelps County, Nebraska.

In TV, "The Industry" was another name for a funny Canadian show called "Made in Canada". There is also a British TV show called "Industry" on BBC Two and HBO.

In music, "Industry" is the name of an American band from the 1980s, and an Irish band from 2009-2010. It is also the name of music albums by Dom & Roland, and Richard and Danny Thompson. Jon McLaughlin also made an EP called "Industry".

In archaeology, "industry" is a way to group old stone tools.

original_text: we investigate the effects of the addition of pre - main sequence evolution to star cluster simulations . we allowed stars to follow pre - main sequence tracks that begin at the deuterium burning birthline and end at the zero age main sequence . we compared our simulations to ones in which the stars began their lives at the zero age main sequence , and also investigated the effects of particular choices for initial binary orbital parameters . we find that the inclusion of the pre - main sequence phase results in a slightly higher core concentration , lower binary fraction , and fewer hard binary systems . in general , the global properties of star clusters remain almost unchanged , but the properties of the binary star population in the cluster can be dramatically modified by the correct treatment of the pre - main sequence stage .

We look at what happens when baby stars grow in our star games.
We let stars go on their baby star path. They start when they are born. They stop when they shine bright.
We looked at our star games. We looked at other games too. In those games, stars started shining bright right away.
We also looked at how two stars that are together start their dance.
We saw that when baby stars grow, it makes the middle of the star group a little bit tighter. It makes fewer pairs of stars. It makes fewer pairs of stars that are very close.
Most of the time, the big star groups stay almost the same. But how the pairs of stars in the group are can change a lot. This happens if we look at how baby stars grow the right way.

We looked at what happens when we add the early life of stars to our computer models of star groups.
In our models, we let stars go through their early life. This early life starts when they are just forming and ends when they become grown-up stars.
We compared these models to others where stars started as grown-up stars right away. We also checked how different starting distances and speeds for pairs of stars changed things.

We found that including this early life for stars made the middle of the star groups a little bit more crowded. It also meant there were fewer pairs of stars, and fewer pairs of stars that were very close together.
Overall, the big picture of the star groups stayed mostly the same. But how the pairs of stars behaved in the group changed a lot when we correctly included their early life.
simplified_secondary_school:
We studied what happens when we include the early life (pre-main sequence evolution) of stars in our computer models of star clusters. In these models, stars followed their pre-main sequence paths, which start when they begin burning deuterium (the deuterium burning birthline) and finish when they reach the zero age main sequence (ZAMS). We compared these results to models where stars were assumed to start their lives directly on the ZAMS. We also looked at how different starting conditions for binary star orbits affected the results.

We found that including the pre-main sequence phase leads to a slightly more concentrated core in the star cluster, fewer binary stars overall, and fewer "hard" binary systems (tightly bound pairs). Overall, the general characteristics of the star clusters don't change much. However, the characteristics of the binary stars within the cluster can change a lot if we accurately model their pre-main sequence stage.

original_text: w28 is one of the archetype supernova remnants ( snrs ) interacting with molecular clouds . h.e.s.s . observation found four tev sources which are coincident with the molecular clouds ( mcs ) around w28 , but _ fermi _ lat detected no prominent gev counterparts for two of them . an accumulative diffusion model is established in this letter and the energetic protons colliding the nearby mcs are considered to be an accumulation of the diffusive protons escaping from the shock front throughout the history of the snr expansion . we have fitted the @xmath0-ray spectra of the four sources and naturally explained the gev spectral break of the northeastern source ( source n ) and the nonsignificant gev emission of the southern sources a and c. the distances of sources a and c from the snr centre are found to be much larger than those of sources n and b , which may be the basic reason for the faint gev @xmath0-rays of the two former sources . W28 is a big old star explosion. It touches big gas clouds.
H.E.S.S. saw four bright spots. These spots are near the gas clouds around W28. But Fermi did not see bright spots for two of them.
We made a new idea. Tiny fast bits hit the gas clouds. These bits came from the star explosion. They moved out slowly as the star explosion grew.
We looked at the light from the four spots. We could tell why one spot (spot N) looked a certain way. And why two other spots (spot A and spot C) were not very bright.
Spots A and C are far from the star explosion. Spots N and B are closer. This might be why spots A and C are not very bright.
simplified_primary_school:
W28 is a special place in space. It is what's left after a very big star exploded. This leftover is called a supernova remnant. It is touching big clouds of gas.

Scientists used a special telescope called H.E.S.S. They saw four bright spots of very high energy. These spots were in the same places as the gas clouds around W28. But another special telescope called Fermi did not see these same bright spots clearly in a different kind of energy for two of them.

In this paper, we have a new idea. We think tiny, fast-moving particles called protons hit the gas clouds. These particles built up over a long time. They slowly spread out from the edge of the supernova remnant (the shock front) as it grew bigger.

We looked at the energy patterns of the four bright spots. We could explain why the energy pattern of the spot in the northeast (source N) changed suddenly. We also explained why the spots in the south (sources A and C) did not show much energy.

We found that spots A and C are much farther away from the middle of the supernova remnant than spots N and B. This might be why spots A and C did not show much energy.
simplified_secondary_school:
W28 is a classic example of a supernova remnant (SNR) that interacts with molecular clouds. Observations by H.E.S.S. found four sources emitting very high-energy gamma rays (TeV sources) that are located in the same areas as the molecular clouds (MCs) surrounding W28. However, the Fermi-LAT telescope did not detect strong corresponding emissions at lower gamma-ray energies (GeV counterparts) for two of these sources.

This paper introduces an "accumulative diffusion model." In this model, the energetic protons that collide with the nearby molecular clouds are thought to be an accumulation of protons that have diffused after escaping from the SNR's shock front over the entire period of its expansion.

We have analyzed the gamma-ray energy distributions (spectra) of the four sources. This model successfully explained why the northeastern source (Source N) shows a sudden drop in its GeV gamma-ray emission (a "spectral break"), as well as the very weak GeV emission from the southern sources A and C. It was found that sources A and C are much farther from the center of the SNR than sources N and B. This greater distance might be the main reason for the faint GeV gamma-ray emissions observed from sources A and C.

original_text: Los Angeles, California (CNN) -- A young boy fell to his death from a luxury suite at Los Angeles' Staples Center on Sunday night, according to the Los Angeles Police Department. The 2-year-old boy fell from the suite around 9:15 p.m. (12:15 a.m. ET), police said in a statement. The boy was transported to the USC Medical Center, where he later died, Sgt. Frank Alvelais told CNN. The toddler fell a few minutes after the conclusion of a NBA game between the Los Angles Lakers and the Golden State Warriors, CNN affiliate KTLA reported. After the game, the boy's family and others in his group were taking pictures while still in the luxury suite section of the arena, police said. The third deck of the Staples Center -- where the luxury suite is located -- has a roughly 50-foot drop, according to KTLA. The boy was identified by Los Angeles County Assistant Chief Coroner Ed Winter as Lucas Anthony Tang. An autopsy is pending, Winter said. Witness said the child was still moving his legs and arms as he was being put on a stretcher, KTLA reported. Detectives said they are investigating the circumstances surrounding the boy's death.

original_text: David Sherry (born 1974) is an artist. Sherry was born in Northern Ireland before studying at the Glasgow School of Art. He is best known for his performance art. Stitching is a video of him stitching pieces of wood to his feet while he offers verbal instructions on how to do this. Other works include Carrying a bucket of water about for a week and Avoiding eye contact for one seven-day period, both of which consist of the activities in their titles. Sherry has said he is trying to "expose the systematic processes of day to day life". In 2003, Sherry was shortlisted for the Beck's Futures prize. In 2006 Sherry exhibited at WestGermany Gallery, Berlin. The exhibition was entitled 'Solid'. In 2012 Flawedcore Records released Sherry's album of spoken word and music "I Love Those Paintings". This release triggered a curated group exhibition by Mother's Tankstation, an art gallery in Dublin, Ireland. He lives and works in Glasgow. References 1974 births Living people Alumni of the Glasgow School of Art Artists from Northern Ireland Scottish artists Performance artists from Northern Ireland Scottish performance artists

David Sherry, born in 1974, is an artist. He was born in Northern Ireland and later studied at the Glasgow School of Art. Sherry is best known for his performance art.

One of his works, a video titled *Stitching*, shows him sewing pieces of wood to his feet while he gives spoken instructions on how to do it. Other works include *Carrying a bucket of water about for a week* and *Avoiding eye contact for one seven-day period*. Both of these pieces involve performing the activities described in their titles. Sherry has explained that he aims to "expose the systematic processes of day to day life."

In 2003, Sherry was shortlisted for the Beck's Futures prize. He exhibited his work in 2006 at the WestGermany Gallery in Berlin, in an exhibition called 'Solid'. In 2012, Flawedcore Records released "I Love Those Paintings," an album by Sherry featuring spoken word and music. This release then led to a curated group exhibition organized by Mother's Tankstation, an art gallery in Dublin, Ireland. David Sherry lives and works in Glasgow.

original_text: (CNN) -- Authorities have recovered 54 bodies after a ferry crammed with people capsized in southern Bangladesh, police said Sunday. Among the victims were 22 children and 15 women, said Nazrul Islam, the police chief of Bhola district where the accident occurred Friday. Thirty more passengers are believed missing and presumed dead, he said. "Hopefully, in few hours, we should be able to confirm the exact number of missing (people)," Islam said. The boat had a capacity of 1,500 but was overcrowded with about 2,000 people who were traveling from the capital, Dhaka, to their homes in Bhola for the Muslim festival of Eid al-Adha. The boat toppled as passengers weighted down one side to disembark, Islam said. Police and firefighters rushed to aid passengers, many of whom were trapped in the lower deck. CNN's Harmeet Shah Singh contributed to this report .

Police and firefighters came fast to help. Many people were stuck inside the boat.

The ferry was designed to hold 1,500 people but was dangerously overcrowded with about 2,000 passengers. They were traveling from Dhaka, the capital, to their homes in Bhola for the Muslim festival of Eid al-Adha. The boat tipped over when passengers moved to one side, trying to get off.

Police and firefighters quickly arrived to help. Many passengers were trapped on the lower deck of the boat.

original_text: Martin Agricola (6 January 1486 – 10 June 1556) was a German composer of Renaissance music and a music theorist. Biography Agricola was born in Schwiebus in Lebusz. From 1524 until his death he lived at Magdeburg, where he occupied the post of teacher or cantor in the Protestant school. The senator and music-printer Georg Rhau, of Wittenberg, was a close friend of Agricola, whose theoretical works, providing valuable material concerning the change from the old to the new system of notation, he published. Among Agricola's other theoretical works is Musica instrumentalis deudsch (1528 and 1545), a study of musical instruments, and one of the most important works in early organology; and one of the earliest books on the rudiments of music. Agricola was also the first to harmonize in four parts Martin Luther's chorale, Ein feste Burg. Notes References Attribution Further reading Classical Composers Database 1486 births 1556 deaths Renaissance composers Classical composers of church music German classical composers German male classical composers German music theorists Silesian-German people People from Świebodzin
