It was one of the first and most important books about how instruments work. He also wrote one of the first books that taught the basic rules of music.

Martin was also the first person to write music for four different voices for a song by Martin Luther. The song was called "Ein feste Burg."
simplified_secondary_school:
Martin Agricola (born January 6, 1486 – died June 10, 1556) was a German composer of Renaissance music and a music theorist.

He was born in Schwiebus, in the Lebusz region. From 1524 until his death, he lived in Magdeburg, where he worked as a teacher or cantor (a church choir director) at a Protestant school.

Georg Rhau, a senator and music printer from Wittenberg, was a close friend of Agricola. Rhau published some of Agricola's theoretical writings, which are important because they explain the shift from older music notation to the newer system.

Another significant theoretical work by Agricola is *Musica instrumentalis deudsch* (published in 1528 and 145). This book is a study of musical instruments and is considered one of the most important early works in organology (the study of musical instruments). It is also one of the earliest books to cover the basic principles of music.

Agricola was also the first to arrange Martin Luther's hymn, "Ein feste Burg" (A Mighty Fortress Is Our God), for four vocal parts.

For making real pictures, the picture they want is the best answer. This is true when there is no noise.

Phase retrieval is about finding a hidden message or picture. You only have some special clues about it. A new idea called 'compressed sensing' helps. It uses the fact that the hidden picture is often simple. This means we don't need as many clues to find it. This new way is called compressive phase retrieval, or CPR.

In this work, we make special math problems for CPR. These problems use the idea that the picture is simple. We also show step-by-step ways to solve these problems. For real pictures (like photos) that don't have negative parts, we show that the hidden picture is the best answer to our special math problem. This is true when the clues are perfect.

Computer tests show that our new way is quick, correct, and works well even if the clues have small mistakes.
simplified_secondary_school:
Phase retrieval is a classical, difficult problem that involves reconstructing an original signal using only its Fourier magnitude measurements. Inspired by a technique called compressed sensing, recent research in phase retrieval has started to use "signal sparsity"—the idea that a signal has very few important components—to reduce the number of measurements required. This approach is known as compressive phase retrieval (CPR).

In this paper, specific mathematical minimization problems are designed for CPR to take advantage of signal sparsity, and alternating direction algorithms are introduced to solve these problems. For reconstructing real-valued, non-negative images, it is shown that the actual image is the perfect solution to these formulated minimization problems when there is no noise in the measurements. Computer simulations demonstrate that this proposed method is fast, accurate, and robust, meaning it performs well even with noisy measurements.

It is becoming very important to design molecules and materials in a smart way. Strong computers and clever computer programs help a lot. A science called quantum chemistry is now very important for designing things. Normally, quantum chemistry tells us what a molecule will do if we know its shape. But inverse quantum chemistry is different. It tries to find the right shape for a molecule if we know what we want it to do. In this paper, we look at the ways people have tried to do inverse quantum chemistry. We talk about what works well and what doesn't.

This paper is titled "Inverse Quantum Chemistry: Ideas and Plans for Designing Smart Molecules." It was written by Thomas Weymuth and Markus Reiher. They work at ETH Zurich, in a physical chemistry lab at Vladimir-Prelog-Weg 2, 8093 Zurich, Switzerland. The paper was written on March 9, 2014. It was printed in a science magazine called 'International Journal of Quantum Chemistry', volume 114, in 2014, on pages 823 to 837.
simplified_secondary_school:
The rational design of molecules and materials is becoming increasingly important. With the help of powerful computer systems and advanced algorithms, quantum chemistry plays a crucial role in this design process. Traditional quantum chemistry predicts the properties of a molecular structure that is already known. However, the goal of inverse quantum chemistry is to discover a structure that has one or more desired properties. This review examines the inverse quantum chemical methods proposed so far, discussing their advantages and disadvantages.

This paper, titled 'Inverse Quantum Chemistry: Concepts and Strategies for Rational Compound Design,' was written by Thomas Weymuth and Markus Reiher. They are from ETH Zurich, Laboratory for Physical Chemistry, Vladimir-Prelog-Weg 2, 8093 Zurich, Switzerland. The paper was dated March 9, 2014, and published in the International Journal of Quantum Chemistry, volume 114, in 2014, on pages 823-837.

original_text: Kogarkoite is a sodium sulfate fluoride mineral with formula Na3(SO4)F. It has a pale blue color, a specific gravity of about 2.67 and a hardness of 3.5. The crystal is monoclinic and is a type of naturally occurring antiperovskite. Kogarkoite is named after the Russian petrologist Lia Nikolaevna Kogarko (born 1936) who discovered the mineral. Discovery and occurrence Kogarkoite was first described in 1973 for an occurrence on Alluaiv Mountain, Lovozero Massif, Kola Peninsula, Russia. On Alluaiv it occurs in pegmatitic veins in nepheline syenite. It occurs with sodalite in syenite xenoliths in an alkali intrusive complex at Mont Saint-Hilaire, Canada. In Hortense Hot Spring, Chaffee County, Colorado, it occurs as a sublimate. It occurs at Lake Natron near Ol Doinyo Lengai, Tanzania and Suswa Volcano, Lake Magadi, Kenya. See also List of minerals List of minerals named after people References Sodium minerals Sulfate minerals Fluorine minerals Monoclinic minerals Minerals in space group 11

A Russian scientist named Lia Nikolaevna Kogarko found this mineral. The mineral is named after her. She was born in 1936.

People first wrote about Kogarkoite in 1973. They found it on Alluaiv Mountain in Russia. This mountain is in a place called Lovozero Massif, on the Kola Peninsula. On Alluaiv Mountain, it is found in special rock veins inside another rock called nepheline syenite.

It is also found with another mineral called sodalite. This happens in Canada, at a place called Mont Saint-Hilaire. There, it is inside small pieces of syenite rock that are part of a bigger rock.

In Colorado, USA, at Hortense Hot Spring, it appears as a powder that comes from hot steam.

You can also find it in Tanzania, near Lake Natron and a volcano called Ol Doinyo Lengai. And in Kenya, near Suswa Volcano and Lake Magadi.
simplified_secondary_school:

Its crystals are monoclinic, and it is also a naturally occurring type of antiperovskite. The mineral is named after the Russian petrologist Lia Nikolaevna Kogarko (born 1936), who discovered it.

Kogarkoite was first identified and described in 1973, with its initial discovery on Alluaiv Mountain, part of the Lovozero Massif on the Kola Peninsula, Russia. On Alluaiv Mountain, it is found in pegmatitic veins within nepheline syenite rock. In Canada, at Mont Saint-Hilaire, it occurs alongside sodalite in syenite xenoliths within an alkali intrusive complex. It also forms as a sublimate at Hortense Hot Spring in Chaffee County, Colorado. Further occurrences include Lake Natron near Ol Doinyo Lengai, Tanzania, and Suswa Volcano near Lake Magadi, Kenya.

original_text: Year 252 BC was a year of the pre-Julian Roman calendar. At the time it was known as the Year of the Consulship of Cotta and Geminus (or, less frequently, year 502 Ab urbe condita). The denomination 252 BC for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the prevalent method in Europe for naming years. Events By place Greece Abantidas, the tyrant of Sicyon, is murdered by his enemies and is succeeded by his father, Paseas. Vietnam The Thuc Dynasty replaces the Hong Bang Dynasty in Au Lac (a kingdom in central Vietnam). Births Deaths Abantidas, tyrant of Sicyon (assassinated) References

Things happened in this year.
In Greece:
Abantidas was a bad ruler in Sicyon. His enemies killed him. His father, Paseas, became the new ruler.

In Vietnam:

No new births were written about for this year.
Abantidas died. He was the bad ruler from Sicyon. His enemies killed him.

Sometimes, they also called it year 502, counting from when the city of Rome was built. The name "252 BC" for this year started to be used much later, when people in Europe began using the calendar we mostly use today to name years.

In a place called Sicyon in Greece, a mean ruler named Abantidas was killed by his enemies. His father, Paseas, then became the new ruler.

In a country called Au Lac in central Vietnam, a new family of rulers, the Thuc family, took over from the old Hong Bang family of rulers.
simplified_secondary_school:
The year 252 BC was part of the Roman calendar system that existed before the Julian calendar. In its own time, Romans referred to it as the "Year of the Consulship of Cotta and Geminus," or sometimes as "year 502 Ab urbe condita" (meaning "from the founding of the city"). The designation "252 BC" for this year became common much later, during the early Middle Ages, when the Anno Domini (AD) system became the main way to name years in Europe.

Key Events:
In Greece: Abantidas, the ruler (tyrant) of Sicyon, was killed by his enemies. His father, Paseas, took his place.
In Vietnam: The Thuc Dynasty took over from the Hong Bang Dynasty in Au Lac, a kingdom located in central Vietnam.

Deaths:
Abantidas, the tyrant of Sicyon, who was assassinated.

original_text: In ancient Roman religion, Vica Pota was a goddess whose shrine (aedes) was located at the foot of the Velian Hill, on the site of the domus of Publius Valerius Publicola. This location would place the temple on the same side of the Velia as the Forum and perhaps not far from the Regia. Cicero explains her name as deriving from vincendi atque potiundi, "conquering and gaining mastery." In the Apocolocyntosis, Vica Pota is the mother of Diespiter; although usually identified with Jupiter, Diespiter is here treated as a separate deity, and in the view of Arthur Bernard Cook should perhaps be regarded as the chthonic Dispater. The festival of Vica Pota was January 5. Asconius identifies her with Victoria, but she is probably an earlier Roman or Italic form of victory goddess that predated Victoria and the influence of Greek Nike; Vica Pota was thus the older equivalent of Victoria but probably not a personification of victory as such. In a conjecture not widely accepted, Ludwig Preller thought that Vica Pota might be identified with the Etruscan divine figure Lasa Vecu. In ancient Roman religion, Vica Pota was a goddess. Her shrine (aedes) was located at the bottom of the Velian Hill, on the site where Publius Valerius Publicola's house (domus) once stood. This means her temple was on the same side of the Velian Hill as the Roman Forum, and possibly close to the Regia. The Roman writer Cicero explained that her name came from the Latin phrase *vincendi atque potiundi*, meaning 'conquering and gaining mastery'.

In a work called the *Apocolocyntosis*, Vica Pota is described as the mother of Diespiter. While Diespiter is usually seen as another name for Jupiter, in this text he is treated as a distinct god. Scholar Arthur Bernard Cook suggested that Diespiter in this context might be the underworld god Dispater. Her festival was celebrated on January 5.

The ancient writer Asconius linked Vica Pota with Victoria, the Roman goddess of victory. However, Vica Pota was likely an earlier Roman or Italic goddess of victory who existed before Victoria became prominent and before the influence of the Greek goddess Nike. So, Vica Pota was an older version of Victoria, but probably not a direct personification (a human-like representation) of victory itself. She is sometimes also linked to Vacuna, another goddess who was occasionally identified with victory. In a theory that is not widely accepted, scholar Ludwig Preller suggested that Vica Pota might be the same as the Etruscan divine figure Lasa Vecu.

original_text: AD 46 (XLVI) was a common year starting on Saturday of the Julian calendar. At the time, it was known as the Year of the Consulship of Asiaticus and Silanus (or, less frequently, year 799 Ab urbe condita). The denomination AD 46 for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the prevalent method in Europe for naming years. Events By place Roman Empire The settlement at Celje gets municipal rights, and is named municipium Claudia Celeia. Dobruja is annexed into Roman Moesia. A census shows that there are more than 6,000,000 Roman citizens. After the death of its king, Thracia becomes a Roman province. Rome and its northeast border are reunited by the Danube Road. According to Orosius, there is a serious famine in Syria Central Asia A drought and an invasion of locusts hit the Mongolian steppes, causing a famine and a revolt at Xiongnu. Births Plutarch, Greek historian and biographer (approximate date) Deaths Marcus Vinicius, Roman consul and governor (b. c. 5 BC) Rhoemetalces III, Roman client king of Thrace (murdered) Servius Asinius Celer, Roman politician (executed) References 0046 als:40er#46

Back then, people called it the year of leaders Asiaticus and Silanus.
People started to call it Year 46 a long, long time ago. This is how people in Europe started to name years.

A town called Celje got special rules. It was named Claudia Celeia.
A place called Dobruja joined Roman Moesia.
A count showed that more than six million people were Roman citizens.
Its king died. Thracia became a Roman land.
Rome and its north-east edge joined again. This was because of the Danube Road.

A person named Orosius said there was not enough food in Syria.
No rain came. Many bugs came. These things happened in Mongolia. So there was not enough food. People fought in Xiongnu.

Plutarch was born. He was a writer from Greece.

Marcus Vinicius died. He was a Roman leader.
Rhoemetalces the Third died. He was a king in Thrace. Someone killed him.
Servius Asinius Celer died. He was a Roman leader. Someone killed him.
simplified_primary_school:
The year AD 46 started on a Saturday. It was a normal year in the Julian calendar.
Back then, people called it the year when Asiaticus and Silanus were important leaders (consuls). Sometimes, they also called it year 799 since the city of Rome was built.
We have called this year AD 46 since a long, long time ago. That was when the AD way of naming years became the main way in Europe.

In the Roman Empire, the town of Celje got special rights and was named Claudia Celeia. Dobruja became part of Roman Moesia. A count of people showed there were more than 6 million Roman citizens. After its king died, Thracia became a Roman province. Rome and its border in the northeast were joined again by the Danube Road. A writer named Orosius said there was a very bad hunger in Syria.

In Central Asia, there was no rain and many locusts (bugs that eat plants) came to the Mongolian grasslands. This caused a big hunger and a fight in Xiongnu.

Around this time, a Greek writer named Plutarch was born. He told stories about history and people's lives.

People who died this year were: Marcus Vinicius, a Roman leader and ruler (born around 5 BC). Rhoemetalces the Third, a king of Thrace who worked with Rome (he was killed). Servius Asinius Celer, a Roman leader (he was killed).
simplified_secondary_school:
The year AD 46 was a common year in the Julian calendar. During this period, people often referred to it as the Year of the Consulship of Asiaticus and Silanus. Less commonly, it was called year 799 Ab urbe condita (meaning 'from the founding of the city' of Rome). The name 'AD 46' for this year became common much later, during the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar system became the main way to name years in Europe.

In the Roman Empire during AD 46, several important events occurred. The settlement of Celje was granted municipal rights, becoming known as municipium Claudia Celeia (a town with its own local government). The region of Dobruja was annexed, or taken over, by the Roman province of Moesia. A census revealed that there were over 6 million Roman citizens. After its king died, Thracia became a Roman province. The Danube Road helped to reconnect Rome with its northeastern border regions. According to the historian Orosius, a severe famine struck Syria.

In Central Asia, the Mongolian steppes suffered from a severe drought and a locust invasion. This led to a famine and a revolt among the Xiongnu people.

Notable births in AD 46 include Plutarch, a Greek historian and biographer (this is an approximate date).

Among those who died in AD 46 were: Marcus Vinicius, a Roman consul and governor (born around 5 BC); Rhoemetalces III, the Roman client king of Thrace, who was murdered; and Servius Asinius Celer, a Roman politician who was executed.

original_text: (CNN) -- A Taiwanese fishing vessel has not been heard from since reporting that pirates were attacking it in the Indian Ocean several days ago, authorities said Thursday. The ship has a crew of 26 from Taiwan, China and Vietnam, the European Union Naval Force Somalia said in a statement. On Christmas morning, the vessel FV Shiuh Fu reported being under pirate attack about 120 miles off the northeast tip of Madagascar, the statement said, but "no further communication with the vessel has been possible" since then. Authorities said they do not have information about the condition of the ship or its crew. Pirates are now holding 26 vessels and 613 hostages off the coast of Somalia, where many pirates are based, the European Union Naval Force Somalia said.

They are from Taiwan, China, and Vietnam.

This was far from Madagascar.
We have not heard from the boat since then.

We do not know if the boat or the people are okay.

They have 613 people.
This is near Somalia.
Many bad people live there.

There are 26 people on the boat. They are from Taiwan, China, and Vietnam. A group called the European Union Naval Force Somalia said this in a message.

On Christmas morning, the boat called FV Shiuh Fu said pirates were attacking it. This was about 120 miles away from a place called Madagascar. The message said no one has been able to talk to the boat since then.

People in charge said they do not know how the boat or the people on it are doing.

The European Union Naval Force Somalia also said that pirates are now holding 26 other boats and 613 people. This is happening near the land of Somalia, where many pirates live.
simplified_secondary_school:
A Taiwanese fishing boat has not been heard from since it reported a pirate attack in the Indian Ocean several days ago, authorities said on Thursday.

The ship, named FV Shiuh Fu, has a crew of 26 people from Taiwan, China, and Vietnam.

According to the European Union Naval Force Somalia, the vessel reported the pirate attack on Christmas morning. This happened about 120 miles off the northeast tip of Madagascar.

Since that report, no further communication with the ship has been possible. Authorities do not have any information about the condition of the FV Shiuh Fu or its crew.

The European Union Naval Force Somalia also stated that pirates are currently holding 26 other vessels and 613 hostages off the coast of Somalia, where many pirates are based.

original_text: The Prorocentrales are a small order of dinoflagellates. They are distinguished by having their two flagella inserted apically, rather than ventrally as in other groups. One flagellum extends forward and the other circles its base, and there are no flagellar grooves. This arrangement is called desmokont, in contrast to the dinokont arrangement found in other groups. Accordingly, the Prorocentrales may be called desmoflagellates, and in some classifications were treated as a separate class Desmophyceae. All members have chloroplasts and a theca, which is composed of two large plates joined by a sagittal suture. This structure is shared with the Dinophysiales, and they are probably sister groups. This does not show up on rRNA trees, which also show two separate groups of Prorocentrum, but they leave their relationships mostly unresolved. References Dinophyceae Dinoflagellate orders
simplified_kindergarten:
Prorocentrales are a small kind of dinoflagellates.
They are special. They have two tails.
The tails are on top. Other kinds have tails on the bottom.
One tail goes straight. The other tail goes around its bottom.
They have no tail lines.
This way is called desmokont. Other kinds have a dinokont way.
So, Prorocentrales can be called desmoflagellates.
Some people say they are a different group called Desmophyceae.
All of them have green parts. They have a shell.
The shell is made of two big pieces. The pieces are stuck together in the middle.
Dinophysiales have this kind of shell too. They are like family.
Science pictures do not show this.
The pictures show two kinds of Prorocentrum.
But it is hard to tell how they are all related.
simplified_primary_school:
Prorocentrales are a small group of tiny living things called dinoflagellates.

They are special because their two tiny tails, called flagella, grow from the top of their bodies. Other dinoflagellates have their tails grow from the middle. One tail points straight ahead. The other tail goes around the bottom of the first tail. They do not have special lines or grooves for their tails.

This way their tails are set up is called 'desmokont'. Other dinoflagellates have a different way, called 'dinokont'. Because of this, Prorocentrales can also be called 'desmoflagellates'. Some scientists used to put them in their own special group called 'Desmophyceae'.

All of them have green parts called chloroplasts, which help them make food like plants. They also have a hard outer shell, called a theca. This shell is made of two big pieces that are joined together down the middle.

Another group, called Dinophysiales, has the same kind of shell. This means Prorocentrales and Dinophysiales are probably like brothers and sisters. But when scientists look at their DNA, it doesn't clearly show they are like brothers and sisters. The DNA also shows two different kinds of Prorocentrum, but it doesn't fully explain how they are all related.
simplified_secondary_school:
The Prorocentrales are a small order of dinoflagellates. They are unique because their two flagella are attached at the top (apically), instead of underneath (ventrally) as in other dinoflagellate groups. One flagellum points forward, and the other wraps around its base. Importantly, they do not have flagellar grooves. This specific setup is called a desmokont arrangement, which is different from the dinokont arrangement found in other groups. For this reason, Prorocentrales are sometimes called desmoflagellates, and some classification systems have treated them as a separate class, Desmophyceae.

All members of this order have chloroplasts and a protective outer shell called a theca. This theca is made of two large plates joined by a seam down the middle, known as a sagittal suture. This structure is also found in the Dinophysiales, and these two groups are probably closely related (sister groups). However, genetic analysis using rRNA trees does not clearly show this relationship between Prorocentrales and Dinophysiales. These trees also indicate two separate groups within Prorocentrum, but they leave their relationships mostly unresolved.

original_text: transfer - ionization in fast collisions between a bare ion and an atom , in which one of the atomic electrons is captured by the ion whereas another one is emitted , crucially depends on dynamic electron - electron correlations . we show that in collisions with a highly charged ion a strong field of the ion has a very profound effect on the correlated channels of transfer - ionization . in particular , this field weakens electron emission into the direction opposite to the motion of the ion and strongly suppresses the emission perpendicular to this motion . instead , electron emission is redirected into those parts of the momentum space which are very weakly populated in fast collisions with low charged ions .
simplified_kindergarten:
Tiny things bump fast.
A special tiny thing hits another tiny thing.
One small part goes to the special tiny thing.
Another small part flies out.
How these small parts move together is very important for this to happen.

We found something.
If the special tiny thing has a lot of power, its strong pull changes how the small parts move together.
This pull makes fewer small parts fly out backwards.
It makes almost no small parts fly out sideways.

Instead, the small parts fly out to new places.
Not many small parts usually go to these places.
This happens when the special tiny thing has little power.
simplified_primary_school:
When an ion, which is a tiny part of an atom with no electrons, quickly bumps into another atom, one tiny electron from that atom is taken by the ion. At the same time, another tiny electron flies out. This process is called transfer-ionization, and how these tiny electrons affect each other is very important for it to happen.

We found out that when a very powerful ion hits an atom, the strong power around the ion greatly changes how the electrons work together during transfer-ionization. Specifically, this strong power makes it harder for electrons to fly out backwards from where the ion is going. It also stops many electrons from flying out sideways. Instead, the electrons fly out in new directions and with new speeds. These are directions and speeds where electrons usually don't go when ions with less power hit atoms quickly.
simplified_secondary_school: When a bare ion collides quickly with an atom, a process called transfer-ionization happens: the ion captures one of the atom's electrons, and another electron is released. How this process unfolds critically depends on the dynamic electron-electron correlations. We found that in collisions with a highly charged ion, the ion's strong electrical field has a very profound effect on these correlated transfer-ionization pathways. Specifically, this field weakens electron emission in the direction opposite to the ion's motion and strongly suppresses emission perpendicular to its motion. Instead, electron emission is redirected into parts of momentum space that are very weakly populated in fast collisions with low charged ions.

original_text: (CNN) -- Armed forces in the Democratic Republic of Congo beat back dozens of gunmen who stormed three key locations in the capital Monday. Armed men attacked the country's airport, state TV headquarters and army headquarters in Kinshasa. At least 34 assailants were killed, according to an Interior Ministry statement. The statement called on "victims of this terrorist attack ... to know that the assailants had no other purpose than to create panic and terror on the eve of celebrations so important to our culture." In response, it said that security had been beefed up in the capital. African Union Commission Chairwoman Nkosazana Dlamini-Zuma condemned the attacks, stressing the AU's commitment to support Congolese efforts to consolidate peace, security and stability.

The bad men went to three places in the main city.
This was on Monday.
The bad men attacked the airport.
They attacked the TV station.
They attacked the army's main house.
This was in Kinshasa city.
34 bad men died.
The government said this.
The government told people who were hurt.
They said the bad men just wanted to make people scared.
This was before big parties for their country.
So the government sent more police and soldiers to the main city.
A lady named Nkosazana said the attacks were very bad.
She said her group will help Congo.
They want Congo to be safe and have peace.
simplified_primary_school:
Soldiers in a country called Congo stopped many men with guns. The men attacked three important places in the main city on Monday. The men with guns attacked the airport, the main TV station, and the main army building in Kinshasa. At least 34 of the attackers died. A government office said this.

The government office told people who were hurt that the attackers only wanted to make people scared. This happened right before important parties for their country. Because of this, more guards were put in place in the main city.

A leader named Nkosazana Dlamini-Zuma from the African Union said the attacks were very bad. She said the African Union would help Congo to have peace, safety, and a calm country.
simplified_secondary_school: On Monday, armed forces in the Democratic Republic of Congo fought off dozens of gunmen who attacked three important places in the capital city, Kinshasa. The gunmen targeted the country's airport, the state TV headquarters, and the army headquarters. At least 34 attackers were killed, according to a statement from the Interior Ministry. The ministry said these attacks were "terrorist" and aimed only to cause fear and panic just before important cultural celebrations. Because of the attacks, security was increased in the capital. Nkosazana Dlamini-Zuma, the Chairwoman of the African Union Commission, spoke out against the attacks. She emphasized that the AU is dedicated to helping Congo strengthen peace, security, and stability.

Rescuers pulled 45 miners out of mine, including two who later died from injuries sustained in the blast, the news agency reported. original_text: we present a theoretical study on the orbital magnetism in multilayer graphenes within the effective mass approximation . the hamiltonian and thus susceptibility can be decomposed into contributions from sub - systems equivalent to monolayer or bilayer graphene . the monolayer - type subband exists only in odd layers and exhibits a delta - function susceptibility at @xmath0 . the bilayer - type subband appearing in every layer number gives a singular structure in the vicinity of @xmath0 due to the trigonal warping as well as a logarithmic tail away from @xmath0 . the integral of the susceptibility over energy is approximately given only by the layer number .
simplified_kindergarten:
We look at magnets. They are in many layers of graphene. We use a special math idea to help us.
We can split up how it works and how it pulls magnets. We can look at parts like one layer of graphene. Or parts like two layers of graphene.
The part like one layer is only in the odd layers. It shows a very strong magnet pull at a special spot.
The part like two layers is in all the layers. It makes a special shape near the special spot. This is because of a special twist. It also gets smaller far from the special spot.

simplified_primary_school: We looked at how tiny magnets work in many layers of graphene. We used a simple idea about how heavy they are. The math we use, and how easily it becomes magnetic, can be broken down into parts. These parts are like one layer of graphene or two layers of graphene. The part that acts like one layer of graphene is only found in layers like 1, 3, 5, and so on. It becomes magnetic very strongly at a special point (xmath0). The part that acts like two layers of graphene is found in every layer. It makes it magnetic in a special way near xmath0 because of its special triangle shape. It also slowly becomes less magnetic as you move away from xmath0. If you add up all the magnetic strength, it is roughly just the number of layers.
simplified_secondary_school:
This study theoretically investigates the orbital magnetism in multilayer graphenes, using a method called the effective mass approximation. We found that the system's magnetic response, known as susceptibility, can be understood by breaking it down into contributions from simpler parts that act like either single-layer (monolayer) or double-layer (bilayer) graphene.

The parts that behave like monolayer graphene (called monolayer-type subbands) are found only in layers with odd numbers. These show a very sharp, spike-like magnetic response at a specific energy.

In contrast, the parts that behave like bilayer graphene (bilayer-type subbands) are present in every layer. These create a very strong, sharp feature in the magnetic response near that same specific energy, which is caused by something called trigonal warping. They also show a gradually decreasing 'tail' (a logarithmic tail) in the magnetic response further away from that energy.

Overall, if you sum up the magnetic response across all energies, its total value depends approximately only on the total number of layers.

This helps the computer avoid making choices it cannot undo, so it can always finish its work and get the right answer. Other ways that do this often need to copy a big part of the problem at the start. But pull-tabbing does not need to do that. We explain exactly what pull-tabbing is. We show which computer programs can use this new way. We add pull-tabbing to how these programs work. And we show that it works correctly.
simplified_secondary_school:
Pull-tabbing is a method for evaluating calculations in functional logic programming. It uses a recently developed technique called graph transformation. This method is designed to avoid making choices that, once made, cannot be changed and might prevent the program from finding all possible correct answers. Unlike other similar methods that share this ability, pull-tabbing doesn't need to copy large parts of the program's current state before making a choice. In this work, we precisely define the pull-tab transformation. We describe the types of programs it's designed for, show how to use it within their calculations, and prove that these new calculations are correct.

Also, special lines on the edges of these shapes are found using something called 'Bergman's logarithmic limit set'. original_text: (CNN) -- A trio of rare Snow Leopard cubs have made their public debut at a zoo in Switzerland this week. The as yet unnamed triplets, born in Basel Zoo eight weeks ago, were displayed on Wednesday with their proud parents. Mayhan, the cubs' mother, was paired with father Pator in 2009 as part of a Europe-wide zoo breeding program. The pregnancy was confirmed in January this year. The zoo said the cubs, who are still being suckled by their mother, are expected to remain in Basel for their first three years but will later travel as ambassadors for their endangered species. Snow leopards are usually found in the Himalayan ranges of central Asia at altitudes of up to 6,000 meters. The species has been pushed to the brink of extinction by farmers protecting their livestock and hunters who sell its fur and body parts, which are prized by some traditional Chinese medicine practitioners. There are now an estimated 3,500 to 7,000 snow leopards left living in the wild, according to the Snow Leopard Trust.

original_text: (CNN) -- Stargazers glimpsed a rare treat as a "blood moon" crossed the Earth's shadow Tuesday. The moon took on a devilish glow as it appeared in different phases from 2 to 4 a.m. ET. Hundreds of people from North and South America, where the blood moon was most visible, shared their images of the lunar spectacle with CNN iReport. "My dad and I have been planning to view this for many months, and it was truly a breathtaking experience," said high school student and astronomy enthusiast Ahan Malhotra from Miami. If rain or clouds obscured your lunar experience, don't fret. This episode is the first of four consecutive total lunar eclipses -- known as the tetrad -- that will occur in six-month intervals until September 2015. Unlike solar eclipses, lunar eclipses are safe to view with the naked eye and don't require special filters. Did you capture the blood moon in its best light? By using hourly data on air pressure at the Earth's surface from models created by the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) and the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) Climate Forecast System Reanalysis (CFSR), we discovered that changes in atmospheric pressure cause the Earth's inner core to move back and forth. This movement is called the Slichter mode, and it results in extremely small gravitational changes at the Earth's surface, less than a nanogal.

We performed these calculations using a method called 'normal-mode formalism,' which helps us understand how things vibrate. Our calculations were based on an Earth model that is spherical (round), self-gravitating (has its own gravity), anelastic (meaning it can deform and isn't perfectly rigid), and similar to the standard Preliminary Reference Earth Model (PREM).

To understand how the Earth responds to these pressure changes, we statistically analyzed the observed pressure field. We focused on the strength of the largest-scale, global patterns of pressure changes (known as 'degree-one spherical harmonic components') to see how they varied over time.

We also considered two ways the ocean might react to these pressure changes: the 'inverted barometer' effect, where the ocean surface moves up when pressure is low and down when pressure is high, and the 'non-inverted barometer' effect, where the ocean doesn't fully respond in this way.

However, when we compared our results to known background disturbances (noise levels) and the capabilities of extremely sensitive instruments called 'superconducting gravimeters,' we found that the Slichter mode's effect at the Earth's surface is too small to be detected by current technology. This means that actually observing the Slichter mode will be a very difficult challenge for instruments in the near future.

original_text: EXIT were a performance art group during the mid-1970s. EXIT members Penny Rimbaud and Gee Vaucher later founded anarchist punk rock band Crass, adopting many of EXIT's experimental/multi media techniques into Crass' presentation. The group performed as part of the 1972 ICES festival at the Roundhouse. Rimbaud acknowledges that EXIT in turn had been involved with the Fluxus Movement (of which Yoko Ono was a member). EXIT was a performance art group active in the mid-1970s. Two of its members, Penny Rimbaud and Gee Vaucher, later formed the anarchist punk rock band Crass. They adopted many of EXIT's experimental and multimedia techniques for Crass's performances.

The group performed at the 1972 ICES festival, which took place at the Roundhouse. Penny Rimbaud has stated that EXIT was also involved with the Fluxus Movement, a group that included artist Yoko Ono. He also mentioned that Crass was more influenced by avant-garde art than by any earlier rock and roll music.

EXIT's discography includes *The Mystic Trumpeter - Live at the Roundhouse 1972* and *The ICES Tapes* (Exit Stencil Music Cat No. EXMO2), which was released as a CD and book in 2013.

original_text: we outline a method for characterizing deviations from the properties of a standard model ( sm ) higgs boson . we apply it to current data in order to characterize up to which degree the sm higgs boson interpretation is consistent with experiment . we find that the sm higgs boson is consistent with the current data set at the @xmath0 confidence level , based on data of excess events reported by cms and atlas , which are interpreted to be related to the mass scale @xmath1 , and on published @xmath2 exclusion regions . we perform a global fit in terms of two parameters characterizing the deviation from the sm value in the gauge and fermion couplings of a higgs boson . we find two minima in the global fit and identify observables that can remove this degeneracy . an update for moriond 2012 data is included in the appendix , which finds that the sm higgs boson is now consistent with the current data set at only the @xmath3 confidence level ( which corresponds to @xmath4 tension compared to the best fit point ) .

This comes from special findings by CMS and ATLAS. These findings are about how heavy something is, at @xmath1. It also comes from other special areas called @xmath2.
We check everything together. We look at two things. These things show how much the Higgs particle is different from what we think. This is about how the Higgs particle connects to other things.

We show a way to see how a special particle, the Higgs boson, is different from what our main science idea (the Standard Model) says it should be like. We use this way with the information we have now. This helps us find out how much our idea about the Higgs boson (from the Standard Model) matches what we see in our tests.

We found that the Higgs boson (from the Standard Model) matches the information we have now very well, with a @xmath0 certainty. This is based on extra things that happened, which two big science groups (CMS and ATLAS) told us about. These extra things seem to be linked to a certain weight or size, called @xmath1. It is also based on areas where scientists have said the Higgs boson cannot be, based on their findings (@xmath2 exclusion regions).

We did a big calculation using two numbers. These numbers show how much the Higgs boson connects to other particles differently from what the Standard Model says. We found two best answers in our big calculation. We also found things we can measure that will help us pick only one best answer.

There is an update using new information from Moriond 2012 at the end of our paper. This update shows that the Higgs boson (from the Standard Model) now matches the information we have now with only a @xmath3 certainty. This means there is a @xmath4 difference from the very best answer we found before.
simplified_secondary_school:
We describe a method to identify how a Higgs boson might differ from what the Standard Model (SM) predicts. We use this method with current experimental data to see how well the idea of a Standard Model Higgs boson matches what we observe. We found that the Standard Model Higgs boson matches the current data with @xmath0 confidence. This conclusion is based on extra events reported by the CMS and ATLAS experiments, which are believed to be related to a mass of @xmath1, and also on previously published areas where the Higgs boson has been ruled out.

We analyzed all the data by adjusting two parameters. These parameters describe how much the Higgs boson's interactions (its "couplings") with gauge bosons and fermions differ from what the Standard Model predicts. Our analysis showed two possible best-fit solutions, and we identified measurements that could help distinguish between them.

An update using data from Moriond 2012, included in the appendix, shows that the Standard Model Higgs boson is now consistent with the current data at only the @xmath3 confidence level. This indicates a @xmath4 "tension" when compared to the ideal fit.

We looked at a special crystal called YBCO. It had tiny, long holes inside. These holes were made by shooting it with very fast lead particles. We measured how strongly this crystal acted like a magnet. We found that how strong it was changed depending on the angle we looked at it.

When the magnet's pull lined up with the tiny holes, the crystal suddenly twisted. We measured how big this sudden twist was. We only saw this twist when the crystal was a little bit cold (in a small temperature range, less than 2 degrees wide). It also happened when the magnet's pull was not too strong.

This measurement helps us guess how much energy tiny magnetic swirls get by sticking to these holes. But when the crystal got even colder, the power that made the swirls stick to the holes disappeared. We think this happened because the heat made the tiny parts inside wiggle and change how strong the sticking power was.
simplified_secondary_school: We measured an angle-dependent contribution to the stable magnetism of a YBaCuO single crystal. This crystal contained column-shaped defects, which were created by irradiating it with 5.8 GeV lead (Pb) ions. This contribution appears as a sudden jump in the stable torque signal when the magnetic field's direction crosses that of the defects. The size of this jump, observed in a narrow temperature interval of less than 2 Kelvin wide and for magnetic fields up to about twice the dose equivalent field, is used to estimate the energy gained by magnetic vortices getting trapped (pinned) on these defects. The effective pinning energy vanishes at temperatures below T_0. This vanishing is attributed to its renormalization by thermal fluctuations.

original_text: East Side, West Side: Tales of New York Sporting Life 1910–1960 () is a sports book written by Lawrence S. Ritter and published in 1998. It is a Total Sports production and was distributed by Andrews McNeel. The book centers, as its title suggests, on popular sports sites and figures of New York between 1910 and 1960. Among the personalities the book talks about in depth are Babe Ruth, Lou Gehrig, Joe Louis, Primo Carnera, Tony Canzoneri, Sugar Ray Robinson, Casey Stengel, Mickey Mantle, Joe DiMaggio, Lou Stillman, Jacob Ruppert and more. Sports covered in depth are baseball, basketball, boxing, hockey and horse racing, all of which were popular sports in New York during the 50 years covered by the book. 1992 non-fiction books Books about sports Sports in New York City

original_text: Beijing (CNN) -- A van carrying flammable liquid rammed into a bus in China early Saturday, sparking an explosion that left 43 people dead, state media reported. Both drivers were among the fatalities in the accident in Hunan Province, according to Xinhua news agency. Another person in the van was also killed. Six people were injured, four seriously, according to state-run CCTV. The fate of the other passengers is unclear. The bus had a capacity of 53 people, Xinhua said. The accident destroyed five cars. Authorities extinguished the fire, but a rescue effort is under way. CCTV also reported that the bus operator was blacklisted by local authorities for violating safety rules.

original_text: (CNN) -- A knife-wielding man attacked a woman in a supermarket, eventually cutting off her head and running away with it, government officials in Spain's Tenerife Island said Friday. The man entered a shopping center in the town of Arona, in the Cristianos area of Tenerife, and stole a knife from the supermarket before attacking the apparent stranger, a central government spokeswoman said. Shopping center security guards were alerted and chased the man, subduing him until police arrived. The man, whom national police in Tenerife identified as a Bulgarian, was apparently known in the shopping area, the government spokeswoman said. The British Foreign and Commonwealth office confirmed Friday that a British national had died in Tenerife, part of the Canary Islands. CNN's Al Goodman, Per Nyberg and Bharati Naik contributed to this report.

For zig-zag periods and two other kinds of periods, we find the exact answers. We don't count the parts that are just products.

Graphical functions are a type of complex mathematical function that always gives a single, definite value for each input. They originate from calculations called Feynman amplitudes, which physicists use to describe how particles interact.

We study the characteristics of these functions and use their connection to multiple polylogarithms (another type of mathematical function) to calculate specific values known as Feynman periods. For the "zig-zag" family and two other specific types of periods, we provide exact results, considering them up to products of simpler numbers. We have proven that these periods can be written as combinations of whole numbers multiplied by specific values of single-valued multiple polylogarithms, which are calculated at the number one.

For a larger group of "constructible graphs," we have developed a step-by-step method (an algorithm) that allows computers to calculate their periods using specialized math software. The theory of graphical functions has been used in other research to prove the "zig-zag conjecture."

We look at black holes. Some are small, some are very big. They have a disc that spins. They also shoot out a jet. We want to see how the disc and jet work together. We look at their light. This light is X-rays and radio waves.

We looked at 100 special black holes. We knew how big they were. We saw their radio light and X-ray light. We also looked at 8 other black holes. We looked at these 8 black holes 50 times. We saw their radio light and X-ray light at the same time.

With all these black holes, we looked at how things were linked. We looked at their radio light. We looked at their X-ray light. We looked at how big the black holes were.

We found that the radio light was linked to how big the black hole was. It was also linked to the X-ray light. This link was very strong.

This helps us understand black holes better. Before, we sorted black holes by how we saw them. Now, we can also sort them by how big they are. And by how much food they eat.

We study how the spinning gas around black holes makes powerful beams of energy. We look at this for both small black holes and very big black holes. We check the X-ray light and radio waves that come from them.

We collected information from 100 active galaxies. For these galaxies, we knew how big their black hole was, how much radio energy came from its center, and how much X-ray light it gave off. We also looked at 8 black holes in our own galaxy. For these 8, we had 50 times when we watched them with radio waves and X-ray light at the same time.

Using all this information, we looked for connections between how much radio light (L_R) and X-ray light (L_X) the black holes gave off, and how big the black hole was (M).

We found that the amount of radio light (L_R) was strongly linked to both how big the black hole was (M) and how much X-ray light it gave off (L_X).

We show how this finding can help us make a bigger idea about black holes. The old idea said that black holes look different because we see them from different angles. Our new idea also includes how big the black hole is and how fast it is eating gas.
simplified_secondary_school:
We investigate the link between the disc of material surrounding a black hole and the powerful jets of particles it produces. This study includes both stellar-mass black holes (smaller ones) and supermassive black holes (much larger ones). We examine the characteristics of the intense light they emit in hard X-ray and radio wavelengths.

To do this, we collected data from 100 active galactic nuclei (AGN), which are galaxies with very active supermassive black holes at their centers. For these AGN, we had measurements of their black hole mass, their central radio emission at 5 GHz, and their X-ray brightness (luminosity) in the 2-10 keV energy range. We also included 8 black holes found within our own galaxy, for which we had 50 sets of observations where both radio and X-ray data were taken at the same moment.

With this combined set of data, we then investigated the relationships (correlations) among three factors: the black hole's radio brightness (luminosity), its X-ray brightness (luminosity), and its mass. We found a very strong and clear connection: the radio luminosity is correlated with *both* the black hole's mass and its X-ray luminosity.

Finally, we demonstrate how these findings can expand the current "unification by orientation" model (which explains differences in black holes based on our viewing angle). Our work suggests that we should also include "unification by mass and accretion rate," meaning that a black hole's mass and how quickly it pulls in matter also play a role in how it appears.

original_text: Ahikam (Hebrew אחיקם, "My brother has risen") was one of the five whom, according to the Hebrew Bible, Josiah sent to consult the prophetess Huldah in connection with the discovery of the book of the law. Biblical accounts He was the son of Shaphan, the royal secretary, and the father of Gedaliah, governor of Judea after the destruction of Jerusalem by the Babylonians. On one occasion, in a move described by Jonathan Magonet as taking the prophet into 'protective custody', he protected Jeremiah against the fury of Jehoiakim (Jeremiah 26:24) It was in the chamber of another son (Gemariah) of Shaphan that Baruch read Jeremiah's scroll in the hearing of all the people. Gedaliah was a leader in Judea.
This was after the Babylonians broke Jerusalem.

simplified_primary_school:
Ahikam was a man whose name meant "My brother has risen." The Bible says that King Josiah sent Ahikam and four other people to talk to a prophetess named Huldah. They went to her because a special book, called "the book of the law," had been found.

Ahikam was the son of Shaphan, who was the king's secretary. Ahikam was also the father of Gedaliah. Gedaliah became the leader, or governor, of Judea after the city of Jerusalem was broken down by people called the Babylonians.

Ahikam kept the prophet Jeremiah safe from King Jehoiakim's anger (Jeremiah 26:24). A writer named Jonathan Magonet said Ahikam put Jeremiah in a 'safe place' to protect him.

Ahikam's brother was named Gemariah. In Gemariah's room, a man named Baruch read a special paper, or scroll, from Jeremiah. All the people listened to him read it.
simplified_secondary_school:
Ahikam (whose Hebrew name, אחיקם, means "My brother has risen") was one of five men whom King Josiah sent to consult the prophetess Huldah. This event, described in the Hebrew Bible, occurred after the discovery of the book of the law.

Ahikam was the son of Shaphan, who served as the royal secretary. He was also the father of Gedaliah, who became governor of Judea after the Babylonians destroyed Jerusalem.

On one occasion, Ahikam protected the prophet Jeremiah from the intense anger of King Jehoiakim, effectively taking Jeremiah into protective custody (Jeremiah 26:24).

It is also mentioned that Baruch read Jeremiah's scroll aloud for all the people to hear in the chamber of Gemariah, who was another son of Shaphan and therefore Ahikam's brother.

A location is a certain spot on a map or in the world.

But the word "location" can mean other things too.

In music and movies:
"Location" is the name of a small music album from 2005 by a band called The Grand Opening.
"Location" is also the name of a song by Khalid from 2016.
And "Location" is a song by Dave from 2019.
A "filming location" is where a movie or TV show is made.

The word "location" is also used for:
In sign language, it means where your hands are when you make signs.

simplified_secondary_school:
A location is a specific, fixed point on Earth.

Beyond this primary meaning, "location" can also refer to different things depending on the context:

*   **In arts and entertainment:**
    *   An EP (extended play record) from 2005 by The Grand Opening, titled "Location."
    *   Songs titled "Location" by Khalid (2016) and Dave (2019).
    *   A filming location, which is the place where a movie or TV show is produced.

*   **In other fields:**
    *   In sign language, it refers to the position of the hands during signing.
    *   In computing, an HTTP location is a specific field within an HTTP header.
    *   In business or logistics, it describes where a facility (like a factory or warehouse) is placed, especially in the "facility location problem."
    *   In statistics, it refers to how a data distribution shifts, often described by a "location parameter."

Related terms include:
*   A location parameter, which is a value in statistics that shows the central point or typical value of a probability distribution.
*   Other words that sound similar or have related meanings, which might be confused with "location," such as "locale," "locate," "locator," "locution," "allocation," "place," and "position."

original_text: Porgy is a novel written by the American author DuBose Heyward and published by the George H. Doran Company in 1925. The novel tells the story of Porgy, a crippled street beggar living in the black tenements of Charleston, South Carolina, in the 1920s. The character was based on Charlestonian Samuel Smalls. In some of the novel's passages, black characters speak in Gullah, a creole language that had developed among enslaved African Americans during the slavery years on the Sea Islands. The novel was adapted for a 1927 play of the same name by Heyward and his wife, playwright Dorothy Heyward. Even before completing the play, Heyward was in discussions with composer George Gershwin for an operatic version of his novel. This was produced in 1935 as Porgy and Bess (renamed to distinguish it from the play). References External links Full text of Porgy, plus criticism and commentary by Kendra Hamilton, hosted by the University of Virginia 1925 American novels American novels adapted into plays Novels set in South Carolina Porgy and Bess

A real man helped make Porgy's story. His name was Samuel Smalls. He lived in Charleston too.

Some people in the book speak Gullah. Gullah is a special way to talk. People made it up a long time ago. These people were from Africa. They lived on islands by the sea. They were not free then.

The book became a play in 1927. DuBose Heyward and his wife made the play. Her name was Dorothy Heyward.

Before the play was done, DuBose Heyward talked to George Gershwin. George Gershwin writes music. They wanted to make a singing play.

It came out in 1935. It was called Porgy and Bess. They gave it a new name. This was so people knew it was not the first play.

In some parts of the book, Black people in the story speak Gullah. Gullah is a special language. Black people who were forced to be slaves made up this language. They spoke it on the Sea Islands a long time ago when they were slaves.

Later, the book was made into a play in 1927. The play was also called Porgy. DuBose Heyward and his wife, Dorothy Heyward, wrote the play. Dorothy wrote plays too.

simplified_secondary_school:
*Porgy* is a novel written by American author DuBose Heyward and published in 1925 by the George H. Doran Company. It tells the story of Porgy, a crippled street beggar who lived in the black tenements of Charleston, South Carolina, during the 1920s. The character was based on Charlestonian Samuel Smalls.

original_text: (CNN) -- An armed group in strife-torn eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo has kidnapped eight international Red Cross staffers -- seven Congolese and a Swiss delegate -- the aid group said Tuesday. The eight International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) personnel, who had just completed an assessment of displaced people in the Fizi area, have been held since April 9 by Mai Mai Yakutumba, a militia operating in South Kivu province. "We demand that the group holding our personnel release them as quickly as possible," said Franz Rauchenstein, head of the ICRC delegation in the Congo. "We are in contact with our colleagues, and their families have been briefed on the situation." Violent ethnic clashes in the South Kivu, related to the 1994 genocide in neighboring Rwanda, have forced thousands of civilians from their homes in recent months, the ICRC said. "It is in order to protect and assist armed-conflict victims that we have been carrying out our activities in the area," Rauchenstein said. "We continue to insist that the strictly neutral, impartial and humanitarian nature of our work be recognized, and that our colleagues be able to return to their loved ones soon."

Franz Rauchenstein said, "We want our helpers to be free very fast." He is in charge of the Red Cross in Congo. He also said, "We can talk to our helpers. Their families know what is happening."

The Red Cross said there is bad fighting in South Kivu. Many people had to leave their homes. This happened in the last few months. This fighting is like bad fighting that happened in Rwanda a long time ago.

Rauchenstein said, "We work here to help people. We help people who are in danger from fighting." He also said, "We just help people. We do not pick sides. We want our helpers to go home to their families soon."

original_text: Former head teacher James Stewart has been arrested over £40,000 of expenses claims . The former head of a failing school has been arrested over £40,000 of expenses claims – including a wine club subscription and Virgin Media package for his home. James Stewart, who made £120,000 a year as the academy's principal, was quizzed by police after a Government watchdog said he received £39,026 of 'irregular' payments. It said the sum included £25,544 spent on 'alcohol, hospitality, shopping, gift cards, home appliances/furnishings'. Of this, £3,593.62 was 'directly attributable' to alcohol, including the wine club subscriptions. Investigators said the 'potentially irregular expenditure' also included £2,934.84 in direct debits to mobile phone companies and to Virgin Media for a home package. Mr Stewart, 69, quit as head of Sawtry Community College in Huntingdon, Cambridgeshire, in the summer after a critical Ofsted report which put the school in special measures. He did not respond last night to a request for comment. original_text: Grootegast (; ) is a village and former municipality in the northeastern Netherlands. It is the sister city of Kingston, Tasmania. The municipality was merged into the municipality of Westerkwartier on 1 January 2019. Geography The former municipality contained the population centres: Doezum, Enumatil, Faan, Grootegast, Kornhorn, Lutjegast, Niekerk, Oldekerk, Opende, Sebaldeburen. In some of these villages they still speak the West Frisian language. The village of Grootegast Grootegast was the main village of the former municipality. The name of the village refers to a 'gast' or 'gaast', a higher, sandy ridge in an otherwise swampy area. Groot is Dutch for 'large'. Grootegast is the birthplace of the theologian Cornelius Van Til. Lutjegast Lutjegast is a village in the former municipality of Grootegast. In the Groningen dialect of Low German 'Lutje' means small or little. original_text: (CNN) -- Police in Malaysia are holding 11 people on suspicion of involvement in a militant group responsible for planning acts of terror, police spokeswoman Asmawati Ahmad said Sunday. There is no indication that the group, arrested last week, has any connection to the missing Malaysia Airlines Flight 370, she said. The detainees are also suspected of having links to terrorists in other countries. Police plan to detain other suspects as a preemptive measure, Asmawati said. The neighboring country has closed and closely monitored the border with that region of Malaysia in a crackdown on terrorism, according to the CIA World Factbook. original_text: Gwenllian (or Gwenllïan) (Welsh, a combination of gwen "fair, blessed, white" and llian "flaxen") was the name of several ladies who lived in medieval Wales. The two best known have, for different reasons, become symbols of Welsh patriotism and/or independence. It is also the name of a song by prominent Welsh songwriter Meic Stevens and Anglesey rock band Calfari. Gwenllian ferch Gruffydd (1097–1136) Her patriotic revolt and subsequent death in battle at Kidwelly Castle contributed to the Great Revolt of 1136 Gwenllian ferch Llywelyn (1282–1337) The only child of Llywelyn ap Gruffudd, the last native Prince of Wales Welsh feminine given names

Gwenllian is also the name of a song. A famous Welsh songwriter named Meic Stevens made it. A band called Calfari from Anglesey also made a song with this name.

One Gwenllian was Gwenllian ferch Gruffydd. She lived a long time ago, from 1097 to 1136. She led a fight because she loved Wales. She died in a battle at Kidwelly Castle. Her fight helped start a big fight called the Great Revolt in 1136.

Another Gwenllian was Gwenllian ferch Llywelyn. She lived from 1282 to 1337. She was the only child of Llywelyn ap Gruffudd. He was the last Welsh Prince of Wales.

Gwenllian (sometimes spelled Gwenllïan) is a Welsh name. It combines 'gwen' (meaning 'fair, blessed, or white') and 'llian' (meaning 'flaxen'). This name was given to several women in medieval Wales. It is also the name of a song by prominent Welsh songwriter Meic Stevens and the Anglesey rock band Calfari.

Two of these historical figures are particularly well-known, and for different reasons, they have become symbols of Welsh patriotism and independence.

One notable figure was Gwenllian ferch Gruffydd (1097–1136). Her patriotic rebellion and subsequent death in battle at Kidwelly Castle helped spark the Great Revolt of 1136.

Another was Gwenllian ferch Llywelyn (1282–1337). She was the only child of Llywelyn ap Gruffudd, who was the last native Prince of Wales.

original_text: (CNN) -- A 25-foot dead whale washed ashore on New York's Jones Beach Island on Thursday morning, a New York State Parks official said. George Gorman, director of recreational services for New York State Parks, told CNN that officials believe it's a humpback whale. Jones Beach Island is a barrier island bordering the south of Long Island and a popular recreational spot for New Yorkers. The New York State Marine Mammal and Sea Turtle Stranding Program -- a branch of the Riverhead Foundation for Marine Research and Preservation that rescues and rehabilitates marine animals -- was called to examine the whale and remove it from the beach. Riverhead officials estimate that the animal weighs 20 to 25 tons. The group is moving the animal out of the path of the incoming tide with a tractor. An necropsy will be performed Friday and then officials will determine where to bury the remains.

original_text: Achall, daughter of Cairbre Nia Fer, king of Tara, and his wife Fedelm Noíchrothach, is a minor character from the Ulster Cycle of Irish mythology. original_text: fast radio bursts ( frbs ) are millisecond - duration radio signals occurring at cosmological distances . however the physical model of frbs is mystery , many models have been proposed . here we study the frequency distributions of peak flux , fluence , duration and waiting time for the repeating frb 121102 . the cumulative distributions of peak flux , fluence and duration show power - law forms . the waiting time distribution also shows power - law distribution , and is consistent with a non - stationary poisson process . these distributions are similar as those of soft gamma repeaters ( sgrs ) . we also use the statistical results to test the proposed models for frbs . these distributions are consistent with the predictions from avalanche models of slowly driven nonlinear dissipative systems .

FRBs are fast radio bursts. They are like radio signals. They last a very short time. They come from very far away.
We do not know what makes FRBs. Many people have ideas about them.
We studied one FRB. It is called FRB 121102. It happens many times. We looked at its strength. We looked at how long it lasted. We looked at its energy. We looked at how long we waited for it.
Its strength, energy, and how long it lasted followed a power law.
How long we waited for it also followed a power law. This was like a special kind of random happening. It did not always happen the same way.
These patterns were like the patterns of soft gamma repeaters. We call them SGRs.
We used what we found to check the ideas about FRBs.
These patterns matched ideas about avalanches. These ideas are for special systems. These systems are pushed slowly. They are not simple. They lose energy.
simplified_primary_school:
Fast radio bursts, or FRBs, are quick radio signals. They last only a tiny bit of time, like a blink. They come from very, very far away in space. But we don't know exactly how FRBs work. Many ideas have been suggested to explain them.

We looked at a special FRB called 121102. This FRB repeats, meaning it sends out signals many times. We studied how bright its signals get, how much energy they have, how long they last, and how long we wait for the next signal.

When we looked at the brightness, energy, and length of these signals, they followed a special pattern called a 'power-law'. The time we waited between signals also followed this 'power-law' pattern. This tells us that the signals don't happen at a perfectly steady time, but more like random bursts where the timing can change.

These patterns are like the patterns we see from other space objects called 'soft gamma repeaters' (SGRs).

We also used what we found to check the different ideas people have about how FRBs work. These patterns match what we would expect from ideas called 'avalanche models'. These models are about things where energy slowly builds up and then suddenly bursts out, like a tiny avalanche.
simplified_secondary_school:
Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs) are radio signals that last only a few milliseconds and come from extremely far away in the universe. However, we don't fully understand what causes FRBs, and many different scientific models have been suggested to explain them.

In this study, we examined how often different characteristics occur for a repeating FRB called FRB 121102. We looked at its peak brightness (peak flux), total energy (fluence), how long each burst lasted (duration), and the time between bursts (waiting time).

When we analyzed the peak brightness, total energy, and duration, we found they all follow a "power-law" pattern. This means there are many faint, low-energy, and short bursts, but fewer bright, high-energy, and long ones. The time between bursts also follows a power-law pattern, which is consistent with a "non-stationary Poisson process." This means bursts happen randomly, but the average rate at which they occur can change over time.

These patterns are similar to those observed in another type of cosmic event called Soft Gamma Repeaters (SGRs). We also used these statistical findings to test the various models proposed to explain FRBs. The patterns we found are consistent with predictions from "avalanche models." These models describe systems where energy slowly builds up and then is suddenly released in bursts, similar to how an avalanche occurs in a system that loses some energy over time.

It can also mean something that is always true, no matter what. Like saying "all red apples are apples."

In special thinking rules (called logic), it can be a way to change how you write down true ideas.

Other words that are a bit like tautology are "redundancy" (which means having extra things) and "tautological" (which describes something that is a tautology).

A "tautonym" is a special scientific name for an animal or plant where both parts of its name are exactly the same, like "Gorilla gorilla."

*   In language, a tautology is a redundant statement, often found in literature and rhetoric, where information is unnecessarily repeated.
*   In formal logic, a tautology is a statement that is true under every possible interpretation.
*   It can also be a rule of inference in logic, which is a rule for replacing logical expressions with equivalent ones.

Other related terms include pleonasm, redundancy, and tautological.

Separately, a "tautonym" is a scientific name for a species where both parts of the name have the same spelling.

This paper explains how a perfect liquid moves over time. It uses special math rules called Euler equations. The paper shows that how the liquid changes in a space is a special kind of math rule called a 'Poisson map'. This 'Poisson map' works with a special math idea about things that keep their size.

This is important and not easy. This is because, when we look at the liquid in one way (called the Eulerian way), how it changes over time might not stay in a special math group called the 'Sobolev class'. This means it might not always be 'smooth' or 'well-behaved' in that way.

This means it preserves a special mathematical structure called the Lie-Poisson bracket, which is linked to transformations that move the fluid while keeping its volume unchanged.

This finding is interesting and not obvious. The challenge is that, when we describe the fluid's movement from a fixed viewpoint (called the Eulerian representation), the 'time maps' don't necessarily keep the fluid's properties within a specific mathematical category of 'smoothness' (known as the Sobolev class).

The way this problem is solved is by using the fact that the fluid's movement *is* smooth when described from the perspective of individual fluid particles (called the Lagrangian representation). Then, a specific mathematical technique called a 'Lie-Poisson reduction procedure' is carefully applied.

original_text: The shang () is a Tibetan ritual upturned flat handbell employed by Bonpos and shamans. Description Shang range in size from approximately three to 20 inches in diameter. They are traditionally believed to originate in Zhangzhung and are symbolically similar to the dril bu or ghanta. Shang are traditionally consecrated and made of sophisticated metallic alloy. This theory will be used to describe the transition from quark matter to nuclear matter, a process important in heavy ion collisions and in the early universe. This difficulty arises from several factors: quarks are confined (meaning they cannot exist alone), bound states and correlations appear, special relativity plays a role, and complex nonlinear phenomena cause changes in the vacuum structure of quantum chromodynamics. original_text: the casimir effect in a dispersive and absorbing multilayered system is considered adopting the ( net ) vacuum - field pressure point of view to the casimir force . using the properties of the macroscopic field operators appropriate for absorbing systems and a convenient compact form of the green function for a multilayer , a straightforward and transparent derivation of the casimir force in a lossless layer of an otherwise absorbing multilayer is presented . the resulting expression in terms of the reflection coefficients of the surrounding stacks of layers is of the same form as that obtained by zhou and spruch for a purely dispersive multilayer using the ( surface ) mode summation method [ phys . rev . a * 52 * , 297 ( 1995 ) ] . owing to the recursion relations which the generalized fresnel coefficients satisfy , this result can be applied to more complex systems with planar symmetry . this is illustrated by calculating the casimir force on a dielectric ( metallic ) slab in a planar cavity with realistic mirrors . also , a relationship between the casimir force and energy in two different layers is established .

We look at the Casimir effect. It is in many layers. These layers can soak up light and spread light out. We think about the push from empty space.

We used special tools and special math. We showed how to find the Casimir force. We found it for one layer. This layer does not soak up light. Other layers around it do soak up light.

Our answer uses how light bounces off the layers around it. This answer looks like what Zhou and Spruch found. They looked at layers that only spread light out. They used a different way to find their answer.

Because of special math rules for how light bounces, our answer can be used for other things. These things are more complex and are flat.

We showed this by finding the Casimir force on a flat piece. This piece can be like metal. It is inside a flat box with real mirrors.

We also found how the Casimir force and energy are linked in two different layers.
simplified_primary_school:
We look at the Casimir effect in a system with many layers that can spread light and soak it up. We do this by thinking about the push from empty space that creates the Casimir force.

We use special rules for how fields work in systems that soak up light. We also use a simple way to write down a special math tool for many layers. This helps us easily find the Casimir force in one layer that doesn't soak up light, even though the other layers do.

The answer we get uses how much light bounces off the layers around it. This answer looks just like the one found by Zhou and Spruch. They found their answer for a system with many layers that only spread light, using a different math method.

Because of special math rules that describe how light bounces and goes through layers, our answer can be used for more complicated systems that are flat. We show this by figuring out the Casimir force on a flat piece of material, like plastic or metal. This piece is inside a flat box with mirrors that are like real ones.

We also find a connection between the Casimir force and the energy in two different layers.
simplified_secondary_school:
This research examines the Casimir effect in a system made of many layers of materials that can absorb light and change its speed. We calculate the Casimir force by considering the pressure from the "vacuum fields" – the tiny energy fluctuations in empty space.

The study uses specific mathematical tools, called macroscopic field operators, which are suitable for absorbing materials. It also uses a simplified mathematical function, known as the Green function, for systems with multiple layers. This allows for a clear and direct way to calculate the Casimir force within a layer that doesn't absorb light, even if the surrounding layers do.

The final formula, which is expressed using the reflection coefficients (how much light bounces off) of the surrounding layers, matches the form of an equation previously found by Zhou and Spruch. They had used a different technique called the mode summation method for a system that only affected light's speed but didn't absorb it.

Because of certain mathematical relationships (called recursion relations) that the generalized Fresnel coefficients (which describe how light reflects and transmits at interfaces) follow, this finding can be applied to more complex systems that have flat, parallel layers. To demonstrate this, the researchers calculated the Casimir force acting on a flat slab of material (either an insulator or a metal) placed inside a flat cavity with mirrors that behave like real-world mirrors. Additionally, the study establishes a connection between the Casimir force and the energy present in two different layers of the system.

original_text: we use three samples ( 3crr , 6ce and 6c * ) to investigate the radio luminosity function ( rlf ) for the ` most powerful ' low - frequency selected radio sources . we find that the data are well fitted by a model with a constant co - moving space density at high redshift as well as by one with a declining co - moving space density above some particular redshift . this behaviour is very similar to that inferred for steep - spectrum radio quasars by willott et al(1998 ) in line with the expectations of unified schemes . we conclude that there is as yet no evidence for a ` redshift cut - off ' in the co - moving space densities of powerful classical double radio sources , and rule out a cut - off at @xmath0 .
