We saw that our facts fit two ideas. One idea says there are always the same number of radio things far away. The other idea says there are fewer radio things far away, past a certain point.

This is like what Willott and friends saw in 1998. They looked at other bright radio things. This fits what we thought would happen.

We think there is no end to how far away the strong radio things are. We know there is no end at a special number.
simplified_primary_school: We looked at three groups of radio sources (called 3crr, 6ce, and 6c*). We wanted to learn how bright the strongest radio sources are that we found using low radio waves. Our information fit two ideas. One idea said that the number of these radio sources stayed the same far away in space and time. The other idea said that the number of these radio sources went down after a certain point far away in space and time. This is much like what other scientists (Willott and friends in 1998) found for other radio sources called steep-spectrum radio quasars. This matches what we thought would happen. So, we think there is no proof yet that strong, classic double radio sources suddenly stop appearing far away in space and time. We know they do not stop appearing at a specific point.
simplified_secondary_school: We used three specific groups of radio sources (3CRR, 6CE, and 6C*) to study their 'radio luminosity function' (RLF). These were the 'most powerful' sources, selected because they emit strongly at low radio frequencies. We found that our data fit well with two different models. One model proposes a constant 'co-moving space density' (the number of these sources in a given volume of expanding space) at high redshifts (early in the universe). The other model suggests this density started to decline beyond a particular redshift. This behavior is very similar to what Willott et al. (1998) concluded for 'steep-spectrum radio quasars'. This supports the predictions of 'unified schemes', which are theories that explain different types of radio sources as the same object viewed from different angles. We conclude that there is currently no evidence for a 'redshift cut-off' (a point where these sources suddenly stop appearing) in the 'co-moving space densities' of powerful 'classical double radio sources'. We specifically rule out a cut-off at @xmath0.

original_text: (CNN) -- Jane Goodall left her home in London for the wilds of Africa when she was just 26-years-old driven by the love of and the fascination for animals. Jane Goodall has spent a lifetime studying primates. Despite no formal training, her enthusiasm to study primates led to the key discovery that chimpanzees have abilities and personalities similar to humans. Five decades on, and Dr. Goodall is now a tireless campaigner for conservation and environmental causes, especially the need to protect natural habitat. With Goodall's global Roots and Shoots movement, her work has moved from the jungle to the classroom and much of that effort has been focused on educating the next generation. Jane Goodall will be on CNN African Voices on Saturday, October 24, 12.30 and 19.30 GMT, and Sunday, October 25, 18.00 GMT .

original_text: we study six - dimensional ( 6d ) su(6 ) supersymmetric models where the doublet - triplet splitting , quark - lepton mass relations and gaugino - mediated supersymmetry breaking are taken into account . we find that effective 4d gauge coupling constants have highly nontrivial behavior between two compactification scales . it is shown that realistic patterns of yukawa coupling constants are obtained for valid values of parameters and that hierarchical numbers are generated via suppression by extra - dimensional effects . hip-2008 - 09/th + * gauge and yukawa couplings in 6d supersymmetric su(6 ) models * nobuhiro uekusa _ department of physics , university of helsinki + and helsinki institute of physics , + p.o . box 64 , fin-00014 helsinki , finland _ + _ e - mail _ : nobuhiro.uekusa@helsinki.fi

We look at special models. These models have six parts. They are called SU(6) super models. We think about some things in them. We think about 'doublet-triplet splitting'. We think about how quarks and leptons weigh. We also think about how 'gaugino-mediated supersymmetry breaking' happens.

We found that special 4D numbers act in a very tricky way. This happens between two special scales.

It shows that special numbers called 'Yukawa coupling constants' work in real ways. This is when we use the right numbers. It also shows that numbers in a special order are made. This is because things from other parts make them smaller.
simplified_primary_school: We study special models that have six dimensions. In these models, we look at how doublet and triplet particles split, how heavy quarks and leptons are related, and how supersymmetry breaks with help from gauginos. We found that the strength of forces in our normal four dimensions changes in a very tricky way between two special sizes. We show that we can get real-world patterns for Yukawa couplings if we use the right numbers for our model. Also, numbers are made in a special order, from big to small, because the extra dimensions make them smaller.
simplified_secondary_school: This study examines six-dimensional (6D) SU(6) supersymmetric models, considering factors like doublet-triplet splitting, quark-lepton mass relations, and gaugino-mediated supersymmetry breaking. We found that the effective four-dimensional (4D) gauge coupling constants behave in a very complex way between two compactification scales. It was demonstrated that realistic patterns of Yukawa coupling constants can be achieved with appropriate parameter values, and that hierarchical numbers are generated through suppression caused by extra-dimensional effects.

We do this by looking at many numbers about where oxygen is found in the flat part of our galaxy, using special stars called cepheids. This special distance is at @xmath1 kpc. It changes depending on how fast gas falls into our galaxy's flat part. We might be wrong by about @xmath2 kpc. At the same time, we found out how much oxygen is made in the flat part of our galaxy. We also think that the flat part of our galaxy was made very quickly, in only @xmath3 gyr. This idea helps us understand why not much gas falls into our galaxy from outside.
simplified_secondary_school: We propose a new method to determine the rotation speed of the galactic spiral density waves and, consequently, the corotation radius (@xmath0) in our galaxy. This is done by statistically analyzing how oxygen is distributed radially across the galactic disk, using data from Cepheid stars. We found the corotation resonance to be located at @xmath1 kpc. This position depends on the rate at which gas falls onto the galactic disk, and our measurement has a statistical error of @xmath2 kpc. At the same time, we also determined the constant rate of oxygen synthesis within the galactic disk. We also argue that the galactic disk formed over a very short time-scale, specifically @xmath3 billion years (Gyr). This scenario helps to solve the problem of the observed lack of intergalactic gas infall.

original_text: At the 1896 Summer Olympics, three fencing events were contested at the Zappeion. They were prepared and organized by the Sub-Committee for Fencing. The épée event for men was cancelled. All fencing was done to three touches. Events were held on 7 April and 9 April 1896. 15 athletes from four nations competed; 8 fencers from 3 nations won one medal each. Medal summary These medals are retroactively assigned by the International Olympic Committee; at the time, winners were given a silver medal. original_text: Montevideo, Uruguay (CNN) -- A court sentenced former Uruguayan President Juan Maria Bordaberry to 30 years in prison on Wednesday for the coup that consolidated his power in 1973 and for human rights violations. Judge Mariana Mota convicted Bordaberry of violating the constitution, nine counts of "forced disappearance" and two counts of political homicide. Bordaberry, 81, had already been under house arrest since 2007 for the killings of two opposition legislators in 1976. Bordaberry assumed office in 1972 following an election that has since been questioned. In 1973, he backed a military coup that kept him in power, and dissolved congress and suspended the country's constitution. The military pushed him out in favor of another leader in 1976. Journalist Dario Klein contributed to this report for CNN .

He became the leader in 1972 after an election. Some people now say that election was not fair. In 1973, he helped the army take power by force. This helped him stay the leader. He closed down the group of people who make laws and stopped the country's main rules from working. But in 1976, the army made him leave and put another leader in charge.

Bordaberry, who is 81, had already been under house arrest since 2007. This was for the killings of two opposition politicians in 1976.

This coup allowed him to remain in power, dissolved the country's congress, and suspended its constitution. The military eventually removed him from power in 1976, replacing him with another leader.

original_text: Hong Kong (CNN) -- China has removed 162,629 "phantom staff" from government payrolls, as Beijing presses on with a campaign against official corruption and misuse of public money, state media reported. Hebei province in central China was the worst offender, with 55,793 officials found to be getting paid even though they never worked, followed by Sichuan and Henan, state news agency Xinhua reported on Monday. No "phantom employees" were found in Shanghai and Tibet, the report added. President Xi Jinping has made rooting out corruption a top priority since taking power amid widespread suspicion that government officials use their position for personal gain. The People's Daily also said that 114,418 official vehicles had been scrapped out of a total of 119,846. China has promised to phase out the use of government vehicles by the end of 2015, except for those used for emergencies and law enforcement. The news reports did not shed light on how employees could receive a paycheck without showing up for work, but Hong Kong-based commentator Frank Ching said it was not unheard of for senior Chinese officials to employ friends and family members.

Hebei province in central China had the most cases, with 55,793 officials found to be receiving pay without actually working. Sichuan and Henan provinces followed. State news agency Xinhua reported this on Monday, adding that no ghost employees were found in Shanghai and Tibet.

Since becoming president, Xi Jinping has made fighting corruption a top priority. This is because there was widespread suspicion that government officials were using their positions for personal gain.

The People's Daily also reported that 114,418 official vehicles have been taken out of service, out of a total of 119,846. China has promised to gradually stop using government vehicles by the end of 2015, except for those needed for emergencies and law enforcement.

The news reports did not explain how employees could get paid without actually working. However, Frank Ching, a commentator based in Hong Kong, noted that it was common for senior Chinese officials to hire friends and family members.

original_text: we present the reduced operator approximation : a simple , physically transparent and computationally efficient method of modelling open quantum systems . it employs the heisenberg picture of the quantum dynamics , which allows us to focus on the system degrees of freedom in a natural and easy way . we describe different variants of the method , low- and high - order ( including the interaction operators ) , defining them for either general quantum harmonic oscillators baths or specialising them for independent baths with lorentzian spectral densities . the method captures the decoherence of the system interacting with the bath , while conserving the total energy . the wide range of the method applicability is presented on the example of systems coupled to different baths with different strengths , and compared with the exact pseudomode and the popular quantum state diffusion approach . our results suggest that quantum coherence effects persist in open quantum systems for much longer times than previously thought .
simplified_kindergarten:
This is a new way. It is easy. It helps us learn about tiny things. These tiny things are open.

It uses a special way to watch tiny things move. This helps us see what the tiny thing is doing.

We show different kinds of this way. Some are small. Some are big. They look at how things touch each other. This way works for many kinds of baths. A bath is like the world around a tiny thing.

This way helps us see when a tiny thing gets messy. This happens when it touches its bath. But all the energy stays the same.

We show this way works for many things. It works for tiny things that touch different baths. Some baths touch hard. Some baths touch soft. We checked it with other ways.

We think the special order of tiny open things stays for much, much longer than we thought.
simplified_primary_school:
We are showing you a method called the reduced operator approximation. It is a simple way to understand tiny quantum systems that are open. It is easy to see how it works and computers can use it quickly. This method uses a special way to look at how tiny quantum things move. It helps us easily focus on the important parts of the system.

We explain different versions of this method. Some are simple, and some are more detailed. We show how to use them for systems connected to different kinds of "baths" (like big energy pools). These baths can be general or have a special energy spread.

This method helps us see when the tiny system loses its special quantum properties because it is connected to the bath. But it always keeps the total energy the same.

We show how useful this method is by using it for systems connected to different baths in different ways. We also compare it to other methods, like the exact pseudomode and the quantum state diffusion approach.

What we found makes us think that the special quantum properties in these tiny open systems last much longer than people thought before.
simplified_secondary_school:
We introduce the "reduced operator approximation," a simple, clear, and efficient method for modeling open quantum systems (these are quantum systems that interact with their environment). This method uses the Heisenberg picture of quantum dynamics, which makes it easy and natural to focus on the important properties of the system itself.

We describe different versions of this method, including both simpler ("low-order") and more complex ("high-order") approaches. The high-order versions also consider "interaction operators," which describe how the system and its environment interact. These versions can be applied to general "quantum harmonic oscillator baths" (a type of environment) or specifically to separate environments with "Lorentzian spectral densities" (a specific way to describe the environment's properties).

This method accurately shows how the system loses its quantum properties (called "decoherence") when it interacts with its environment, while still making sure that the total energy of the system and environment together stays the same.

To show how widely this method can be used, we give examples of systems connected to various environments with different interaction strengths. We also compare our method's results with those from the "exact pseudomode" method and the well-known "quantum state diffusion" approach. Our findings suggest that quantum coherence (the ability of a quantum system to be in multiple states at once) lasts much longer in open quantum systems than scientists previously believed.

original_text: In telecommunication, an artificial transmission line is a two-port electrical network that has the characteristic impedance, transmission time delay, phase shift, or other parameter(s) of a real transmission line. It can be used to simulate a real transmission line in one or more of these respects. Early artificial lines were used in telephony research and took the form of a cascade of lattice phase equalisers to provide the necessary delay. The lattice phase circuit was invented by Otto Zobel in the 1920s. References Telecommunications equipment

We can use it to pretend it is a real line.

They were made to make things slow.
A man named Otto Zobel made this special part.
He made it a long, long time ago.

In phone and message systems, a fake wire is an electric thing with two ends. It works like a real wire. It has the same special parts, like how much it slows down a message or how it changes the message. People use it to act like a real wire in one or more of these ways.

The first fake wires were used to study phone systems. They were made of many special parts linked together, called lattice phase equalisers. These parts helped to slow down messages, which was important. A man named Otto Zobel made this special part, the lattice phase circuit, a long time ago in the 1920s.
simplified_secondary_school:
In telecommunications, an artificial transmission line is an electrical circuit with two connection points. It is designed to have the same electrical properties as a real transmission line, such as its characteristic impedance, the time it takes for a signal to travel through it (transmission time delay), or how much the signal's wave shifts (phase shift). This allows it to imitate a real transmission line for one or more of these characteristics.

Early artificial lines were used in telephony research. They were built by connecting many "lattice phase equalisers" one after another (in a cascade) to create the necessary signal delay. Otto Zobel invented the lattice phase circuit in the 1920s.

To solve these problems well and fast, we look at a special way called QMR. We use it for these special math problems. We also suggest a new way to do it, called the weighted quasi-minimal residual way.

We are studying how to solve a specific type of mathematical problem called "complex symmetric shifted linear systems." These problems often come up in large computer simulations that model the structure of electrons, and there is a strong need to solve them quickly.

To solve these systems efficiently, we adapt a specific version of the QMR (Quasi-Minimal Residual) method, which is typically used for non-Hermitian shifted linear systems. We then introduce a new approach for this method, called the "weighted quasi-minimal residual approach."

We have developed a specific computational algorithm, named "shifted QMR_sym(@xmath0)." This algorithm works by carefully choosing a "weight" that makes it particularly efficient. We present practical examples to demonstrate how well the "shifted QMR_sym(@xmath0)" method performs.

"Contrast" can mean many different things.

simplified_secondary_school:
The word "Contrast" has several different meanings, depending on the topic.

In the field of **Science**, contrast can refer to:
*   **Contrast (vision)**, which is the difference in color and brightness between different parts of an image.
*   **Contrast (statistics)**, a way to compare averages. This can be a combination of averages where the numbers used to combine them add up to zero, or simply the difference between two average values.
*   **Behavioral contrast**, a phenomenon studied in psychology, specifically in behavior analysis.
*   A **contrast agent**, a substance used in medicine to help doctors see structures or fluids inside the body more clearly. This is often shortened to just "contrast."

In **Technology**:
*   **Contrast ratio** measures how well a display system can show the difference between its brightest white and darkest black.
*   **Display contrast** refers to the overall quality of contrast on electronic visual displays.

In **Language**:
*   **Contrast (linguistics)** refers to how different sounds or features create distinctions between words.
*   **Contrast (literary)** describes the differences between two or more characters, ideas, or things in a story or text.

In **Arts and entertainment**:
*   **Music**:
    *   **The Contrast (band)** is an English pop band formed in 1999.
    *   **Contrast (music)** refers to the differences between various parts of a musical piece or between different instrumental sounds.
*   **Albums** titled "Contrast" include:
    *   An album by Conor Maynard (2012).
    *   An album by Matt Fax (2017).
    *   An EP (Extended Play) by The Features (2006).
    *   An album by Emi Maria (2010).
    *   An album by Klinik (1992).
    *   An album by Signal Aout 42 (1990).
    *   An EP by Toru Kitajima (2014).
*   **Other media**:
    *   **Contrast (video game)** is a puzzle-based platform game released in 2013.
    *   **The Contrast (play)** is a play written by Royall Tyler in 1787.

original_text: harmonic emission from cluster nanoplasmas subject to short intense infrared laser pulses is studied . in a previous publication [ m. kundu _ et al . _ , , 033201 ( 2007 ) ] we reported particle - in - cell simulation results showing resonant enhancements of low - order harmonics when the mie plasma frequency of the ionizing and expanding cluster resonates with the respective harmonic frequency . simultaneously we found that high - order harmonics were barely present in the spectrum , even at high intensities . the current paper is focused on the analytical modeling of the process . we show that dynamical stochasticity owing to nonlinear resonance inhibits the emission of high order harmonics .
simplified_kindergarten:
We look at tiny balls of light. We shine a bright light on them. Then, light comes out.

Before, we used a computer. It showed us something. Some light came out very strong. This happened when the tiny balls of light shook just right.

But other kinds of light did not come out. Not even when we shined a very bright light.

Now, we use math to see why this happens.

We found out why. The tiny balls of light get mixed up. This stops some light from coming out.
simplified_primary_school:
We are looking at special light that comes from tiny hot gas balls. These gas balls are hit with quick, strong laser light.

In an old paper, we showed what our computer models found. Some of the special light got much stronger. This happened when the tiny hot gas balls wiggled at the same speed as that special light. But at the same time, we saw that the other kind of special light was hardly there. This was true even when the laser was very strong.

This new paper is about using math to explain how all this works. We found that things getting messy and mixed up stops the other kind of special light from coming out. This happens because of a tricky kind of matching.
simplified_secondary_school:
This research examines the 'harmonic emission' (a type of light) that comes from tiny groups of atoms, called 'cluster nanoplasmas,' when they are hit by short, powerful infrared laser pulses.

In a previous study (Kundu et al., 2007), our computer simulations (called 'particle-in-cell simulations') showed that 'low-order harmonics' (light at lower multiples of the laser's original frequency) become much stronger. This 'resonant enhancement' occurs when the 'Mie plasma frequency' (a specific vibration frequency of the cluster as it turns into a plasma and expands) matches the frequency of the harmonic being produced. At the same time, we observed that 'high-order harmonics' (light at much higher multiples of the laser frequency) were almost completely absent from the light produced, even when the laser was very powerful.

This current paper focuses on creating a mathematical model (an 'analytical model') to explain this process. We show that a chaotic and unpredictable behavior, known as 'dynamical stochasticity,' which is caused by a complex interaction called 'nonlinear resonance,' prevents the emission of high-order harmonics.

We looked at how things moved up and down. We call these Shubnikov-de Haas movements. They were in special tiny boxes. The boxes were made of HgTe / HgCdTe. They had 2 to 3 tiny parts inside.

We saw up to 12 special spots. These spots were where the movements met. We saw them when we used magnets. The magnets were strong, from 0.9 to 6 T.

We found out about a special energy. It is called zero magnetic field spin-orbit splitting energy. We found a lot of this energy. We saw it from the special spots. We also saw it from where other lines crossed. These lines are called Landau levels.

These energy amounts were big. They were bigger than how much Landau levels spread out when it is warm. These amounts matched well with other spin-orbit splitting energies. These were called Rashba spin-orbit splitting energies. A special math idea called the Kane model helped us figure them out.

The movements we saw also matched well with what a computer showed us. The computer used the same math idea.
simplified_primary_school:

It will take pictures of 300 million faraway star groups called galaxies, noting where they are and what they look like, even those that are very, very far away. This work will be finished over 525 nights, which will take 5 years, starting in 2012. About one terabyte of raw information will be made every night. This includes pictures for science and pictures to make sure the camera is working right. The DES team has a special system to sort, check, and save all this information. The Dark Energy Survey (DES) is a project that aims to build, install, and operate a new 74-CCD camera at the Blanco telescope. Its goal is to study what causes cosmic acceleration. Observations will take place over 525 nights during a five-year period, starting in 2012. About 1 terabyte (TB) of raw data, including images for scientific research and for calibrating the instruments, will be produced every night.

The system's basic functions involve specific scientific computer programs and a 'data challenge' process. This data challenge process helps to simultaneously improve the data management system's algorithms (the step-by-step instructions it follows) and the analysis codes used by the science teams.

This means the black hole spins very fast in the opposite direction to the incoming gas. This specific arrangement of gravity and magnetism is highly energetic but also unstable.

Another compound, tartar emetic, is produced when potassium tartrate is heated with antimony trioxide. Tartar emetic is harmful, causing intense nausea, extreme weakness (prostration), and vomiting by irritating the lining of the stomach and intestines (gastrointestinal mucosa).

original_text: moving target defense ( mtd ) can enhance the resilience of cyber systems against attacks . although there have been many mtd techniques , there is no systematic understanding and _ quantitative _ characterization of the power of mtd . in this paper , we propose to use a cyber epidemic dynamics approach to characterize the power of mtd . we define and investigate two complementary measures that are applicable when the defender aims to deploy mtd to achieve a certain security goal . one measure emphasizes the _ maximum _ portion of time during which the system can afford to stay in an undesired configuration ( or posture ) , without considering the cost of deploying mtd . the other measure emphasizes the _ minimum _ cost of deploying mtd , while accommodating that the system has to stay in an undesired configuration ( or posture ) for a given portion of time . our analytic studies lead to algorithms for optimally deploying mtd .
simplified_kindergarten:
MTD helps computers stay safe. It makes them strong against bad guys.
There are many ways to use MTD. But we do not know how strong MTD is. We do not have numbers for it.
In this book, we use a sickness idea. It shows how strong MTD is.
We look at two ways to measure MTD. These ways help when someone uses MTD to keep things safe.
One way looks at how long a computer can be a little bit unsafe. We do not think about MTD's cost for this. It is about the most time.
The other way looks at the least money MTD costs. This is even if the computer is a little bit unsafe for some time.
What we learned helps us make rules. These rules show the best way to use MTD.
simplified_primary_school: MTD helps make computer systems stronger against attacks. Even though there are many ways to use MTD, we don't fully understand how strong it is, especially with numbers. In this work, we suggest using a way that looks at how computer problems spread, like a sickness, to figure out how strong MTD is. We explain and look at two different ways to measure things. These ways are useful when the protector wants to use MTD to reach a certain safety goal. One way focuses on the longest time the computer system can be in a bad state, without thinking about how much it costs to use MTD. The other way focuses on the smallest cost to use MTD, even if the computer system has to be in a bad state for a certain amount of time. Our careful studies help us find special steps or rules for using MTD in the best way.
simplified_secondary_school: Moving target defense (MTD) can enhance the resilience of cyber systems against attacks. Although many MTD techniques exist, there is no clear, organized understanding or a way to measure the power of MTD using numbers. In this paper, we suggest using a cyber epidemic dynamics approach to measure MTD's power. We define and study two complementary measures that are useful when a defender aims to use MTD to achieve a specific security goal. One measure focuses on the maximum amount of time a system can be in an undesired configuration (or posture), without considering the cost of using MTD. The other measure focuses on the minimum cost of using MTD, even if the system has to be in an undesired configuration (or posture) for a specific amount of time. Our analytic studies lead to algorithms for optimally using MTD.

original_text: Bendidee is a national park in the Goondiwindi Region, Queensland, Australia. Geography The park is north east of Goondiwindi on the Darling Downs, 264 km west of Brisbane. The park is part of the Brigalow Belt South bioregion and lies within the water catchment areas of the Macintyre and Weir rivers. The park was established to conserve an area of remaining brigalow–belah plant communities. Bendidee State Forest is adjacent to the national park to its north. The elevation of the terrain is 245 meters. Fauna A total of five rare or threatened species have been identified within the park. 30 amphibians and reptiles as well as more than 89 invertebrates have been found at Bendidee, including the endangered Bull oak jewel butterfly. The most interesting inhabitants of the park are the legless lizard (Paradelma orientalis) and the small Dwyer's Hooded Snake (Parasuta dwyeri). Amenities The park provides for walking, bird watching and nature appreciation activities. original_text: In open systems, a virtual terminal (VT) is an application service that: Allows host terminals on a multi-user network to interact with other hosts regardless of terminal type and characteristics, Allows remote log-on by local area network managers for the purpose of management, Allows users to access information from another host processor for transaction processing, Serves as a backup facility. PuTTY is an example of a virtual terminal. ITU-T defines a virtual terminal protocol based on the OSI application layer protocols. However, the virtual terminal protocol is not widely used on the Internet. See also Pseudo terminal for the software interface that provides access to virtual terminals Secure Shell Telnet Terminal emulator for an application program that provides access to virtual terminals Virtual console for an analogous concept that provides several local consoles Sources Computer terminals ITU-T recommendations
simplified_kindergarten:

They can fix computers.

PuTTY is one virtual terminal.
A group called ITU-T made rules for virtual terminals.

simplified_primary_school:
A virtual terminal (VT) is a special computer program. It lets many computers connected together talk to other computers, no matter what kind they are. It lets people who look after computer networks sign in from far away to manage them. It lets people get information from another main computer to do tasks. It also works as a backup.

PuTTY is one example of a virtual terminal.

A group called ITU-T has rules for how virtual terminals should work. These rules are based on other computer rules. But these virtual terminal rules are not used much on the internet.
simplified_secondary_school:
A virtual terminal (VT) is a computer service that allows different computer systems and users to interact smoothly, especially in multi-user networks.

It performs several key functions:
*   It enables terminals on a network to connect with other computers (hosts), even if they are different types or have different features.
*   Network managers can use it to log in remotely and manage the system.
*   Users can access information from other computers to complete tasks, such as processing transactions.
*   It can also serve as a backup system.

PuTTY is a well-known example of a virtual terminal.

The ITU-T organization has defined a specific virtual terminal protocol, which is based on the OSI application layer protocols. However, this particular protocol is not widely used on the Internet today.

Related concepts include:
*   A **pseudo terminal**, which is a software interface used to access virtual terminals.
*   **Secure Shell (SSH)** and **Telnet**, which are protocols often used to connect to virtual terminals.
*   A **terminal emulator**, which is an application program that provides access to virtual terminals.
*   A **virtual console**, which is a similar concept that offers several local consoles.

original_text: Schwarzwald-Baar is a Landkreis (district) in the south of Baden-Württemberg, Germany. Neighboring districts are (from north clockwise) Ortenaukreis, Rottweil, Tuttlingen, Constance, the Swiss canton of Schaffhausen, and the districts Waldshut, Breisgau-Hochschwarzwald and Emmendingen. History The district was created in 1973, when the two districts Donaueschingen and Villingen were merged. Geography The district got its name from the two predominant landscapes in the district. The Black Forest (Schwarzwald), and the Baar, the foothills between the Black Forest and the Swabian Alb. Both the Danube River and the Neckar River have their origins in the Schwarzwald and Baar district. Coat of arms The coat of arms is modeled after the two coat of arms of the cities of Villingen and Schwenningen. Villingen had a horizontally divided blue-and-white arms, while Schwenningen had a vertically divided blue-and-white arms. Combining both divisions gives the four fields. Schwarzwald-Baar is a district (Landkreis) located in the south of Baden-Württemberg, Germany. It shares borders with several other districts: Ortenaukreis, Rottweil, Tuttlingen, Constance, Waldshut, Breisgau-Hochschwarzwald, and Emmendingen. It also borders the Swiss canton of Schaffhausen.

The district was formed in 1973 when the two districts of Donaueschingen and Villingen merged.

Its name comes from the two main landscapes within its borders: the Black Forest (Schwarzwald) and the Baar. The Baar is an area of foothills situated between the Black Forest and the Swabian Alb. Both the Danube River and the Neckar River begin their journeys in the Schwarzwald-Baar district.

The district's coat of arms is based on the coats of arms of the cities of Villingen and Schwenningen. Villingen's original coat of arms was divided horizontally in blue and white, while Schwenningen's was divided vertically in blue and white. Combining these two division styles created the four fields seen in the current coat of arms. The eagle in the top-left section comes from Villingen's coat of arms, representing the Zähringen family who ruled Villingen in the 13th century.

We present results from an instrument that combines two measurement techniques: surface acoustic wave (SAW) propagation and surface plasmon resonance (SPR). Our goal is to use these two independent methods to understand the physical properties of protein layers, especially how much water they contain. SAW measures changes in adsorbed mass, while SPR detects changes in the surface's electrical properties.

We show how sensitive our instrument is to mass by using copper electrodeposition for calibration. For the Love mode device we used, its sensitivity in liquid is 150 fg/mm². We also demonstrate how this instrument can be used to monitor biological processes.

Finally, we present the determined thickness of protein layers and the ratio of protein to water content for the S-layer proteins we studied. We found the thickness to be 4.7 ± 0.7 nm and the protein to water content ratio to be 75 ± 15%.

original_text: The year 647 BC was a year of the pre-Julian Roman calendar. In the Roman Empire, it was known as year 107 Ab urbe condita . The denomination 647 BC for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the prevalent method in Europe for naming years. Events Middle East Elam refuses to extradite an Aramaean prince, giving the king Ashurbanipal of Assyria an excuse to invade the country. He sacks the city of Susa. The name '647 BC' for this year wasn't used until the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini (AD) calendar became the main way to name years across Europe.

In the Middle East during this year, the kingdom of Elam refused to hand over an Aramaean prince. This gave Ashurbanipal, the king of Assyria, an excuse to invade Elam. He then destroyed the city of Susa.

Also around this time, the artwork known as 'Ashurbanipal and his Queen in the Garden' was created. It originated from the palace at Nineveh (located in modern-day Kuyunjik, Iraq) and is now displayed at the British Museum in London.

original_text: Mare Anguis (Latin anguis, the "serpent sea") is a lunar mare located on the near side of the Moon, about 150 kilometers in diameter. Located within the Crisium basin, Mare Anguis is a part of the Nectarian System, meaning that it was formed during the Nectarian time period. Like most mare, the surface of Mare Anguis is dark, indicating that it has been filled with volcanic basalt. It forms part of a concentric ring to the northeast of the Crisium rim, and it lies at an elevation 800 m above Mare Crisium. Channels lead down from Mare Anguis to Mare Chrisium, with some possible indications of lava flow. The small, 7–km diameter crater Eimmart A lies at the east edge of Mare Anguis. It is a proposed source for the Antarctic meteorite ALHA81005. The impact appears to have excavated mare basalt, which probably accounts for the olivine/low-Ca pyroxene mixture found in the spectrum. original_text: Tickell's thrush (Turdus unicolor) is a passerine bird in the thrush family Turdidae. It is common in open forest in the Himalayas, and migrates seasonally into peninsular India, Nepal and rarely to Bangladesh. The name commemorates the British ornithologist Samuel Tickell who collected in India and Burma. Characteristics Males of the Tickell's thrush have uniform blue-grey upperparts, a whitish belly and vent. Adults have yellow beak and legs while it may be darker in juveniles. There is a yellow eye-ring which is thinner and fainter than the Indian black bird which is usually bigger in size. Females and young birds have browner upperparts. Distribution and habitat The Tickell's thrush is omnivorous, eating a wide range of insects, earthworms and berries. It nests in bushes. It does not form flocks, but loose groups of two to five. Populations move further south in India and Nepal in winter. References Tickell's thrush Birds of Pakistan Birds of North India Birds of Nepal Birds of Bhutan Tickell's thrush

The bird is named after Samuel Tickell. He was a bird scientist from Britain who found birds in India and Burma.

Boy Tickell's thrushes are blue-grey on their backs and heads. They have a white tummy and bottom. Grown-up birds have yellow beaks and legs. Young birds can have darker beaks and legs. It has a yellow ring around its eye. This ring is not as thick or bright as the one on an Indian black bird. The Indian black bird is usually bigger. Girl birds and young birds are browner on their backs and heads.

Tickell's thrush eats many different things. It likes insects, earthworms, and berries. It builds its nest in bushes. It does not fly in big groups. Instead, you might see it in small groups of two to five birds. These birds move more to the south in India and Nepal when it is winter.
simplified_secondary_school:
Tickell's thrush (Turdus unicolor) is a passerine, or perching bird, belonging to the thrush family, Turdidae. It is commonly found in open forests of the Himalayas and migrates seasonally to peninsular India, Nepal, and occasionally to Bangladesh. The bird is named after British ornithologist Samuel Tickell, who collected specimens in India and Burma.

Male Tickell's thrushes have a consistent blue-grey color on their upper body and a whitish belly and vent (the area under the tail). Adult birds have a yellow beak and legs, though these parts can be darker in younger birds. They have a yellow ring around their eyes, which is thinner and less noticeable than the eye-ring of the Indian blackbird, a species that is typically larger. Females and young birds have browner upper bodies instead of blue-grey.

Tickell's thrushes are omnivores, meaning they eat both plants and animals. Their diet includes various insects, earthworms, and berries. They build their nests in bushes. These birds do not gather in large flocks but are usually seen in small, loose groups of two to five individuals. During winter, populations of Tickell's thrush migrate further south within India and Nepal.

The modern name, 373 BC, became widely used in Europe during the early medieval period, once the Anno Domini calendar system became the standard for naming years.

In the Persian Empire, King Artaxerxes II launched an invasion of Egypt, aiming to bring the country back under Persian rule. However, King Nectanebo I managed to gather his forces and successfully repelled the Persian invasion.

Meanwhile, in Greece, Iphicrates led an Athenian expedition that successfully lifted a Spartan siege on Corcyra. The ancient Greek city of Helike was completely destroyed by a massive earthquake and tsunami. Additionally, an earthquake also destroyed the Temple of Apollo in Delphi.

original_text: Taliban suicide bombers attacked Afghanistan's regional spy headquarters in Ghazni city early Thursday, leaving 21 people dead and more than 150 civilians wounded, a government official said. Nineteen of the dead were the suicide attackers. The remaining two were members of the National Directorate of Security personnel. The NDS is the nation's spy agency. A Taliban spokesman claimed responsibility for the attack. The assault began when one of the suicide attackers detonated an explosives-filled truck at the front gate of the spy agency, said Shafiq Nang, a spokesman for the governor of Ghazni province. Twelve of them stormed the NDS. Six others hit the Rapid Reaction Police headquarters in the city. For the next hour, Afghan security forces battled the Taliban attackers until all of them were killed, he said. original_text: (CNN) -- A blitz of attacks across Afghanistan led to the deaths of 52 armed Taliban members in the past day, the Ministry of Interior said Wednesday. original_text: we propose a new parametrization of the distribution of the initial eccentricity in a nucleus - nucleus collision at a fixed centrality , which we name the elliptic power distribution . it is a two - parameter distribution , where one of the parameters corresponds to the intrinsic eccentricity , while the other parameter controls the magnitude of eccentricity fluctuations . unlike the previously used bessel - gaussian distribution , which becomes worse for more peripheral collisions , the new elliptic power distribution fits several monte carlo models of the initial state for all centralities .

It uses two numbers. One number shows how round or flat it is. The other number shows how much it can change.

There was an old way. It did not work well when balls hit on the side. Our new way works well. It works for all kinds of hits. It works with many computer pictures.

It uses two special numbers. One number shows how squashed it naturally is. The other number shows how much the squashing can change. There was an old way called the 'Bessel-Gaussian distribution'. But that old way didn't work well when the atoms just barely touched. Our new 'elliptic power distribution' works well for many computer tests of how things start, no matter how the atoms hit.

We call this the "elliptic power distribution." This distribution uses two parameters: one represents the basic oval shape, and the other controls how much that shape can vary. The previously used "Bessel-Gaussian distribution" became less accurate for more glancing collisions. In contrast, our new "elliptic power distribution" accurately fits several computer models of the initial collision state for all types of centralities.

Charles Napier, 65, was held when officers swooped at his mother’s home in Sherborne, Dorset. Arrest: Police have held Charles Napier (right) the half-brother of senior MP John Whittingdale, (left) who is the . chair of the influential Commons culture, media and sport committee . His half-brother, John Whittingdale MP, is chairman of the Commons culture, media and sport select committee. Napier was questioned as part of Operation Fairbank, a Scotland Yard inquiry into claims that a VIP paedophile ring operated in London more than three decades ago with links to Peter Righton, a notorious paedophile who is now dead. Former ministers, MPs, senior police officers and men with links to the royal household are alleged to have indecently assaulted boys in care homes. Sorry we are unable to accept comments for legal reasons.

A special action works on a system.
It makes a special ball get smaller.
It makes it smaller in many ways.
It has a special arrow that shows how.

We look at the shapes of these arrows.

simplified_primary_school:
Imagine a special kind of squeeze that acts on a tiny quantum system. This squeeze makes a special shape, called a 'Bloch ball', smaller in different ways. We can show how it squeezes with a special arrow called a 'compression vector'.

We looked closely at the shapes these compression vectors make. We proved that an idea from two people, Dixit and Sudarshan, was right. Their idea was: when the tiny system has @xmath1 special parts called 'qubits', and we look at it in a special way (called the Pauli basis), then all the different compression vectors together make a simple shape, like a triangle or a pyramid.

We then looked at this even more. We checked what shapes the compression vectors make when we look at the system in other special ways. We found out exactly when all the compression vectors will make that simple shape.
simplified_secondary_school:
A type of quantum process, called a generalized depolarizing channel, acts on an @xmath0-dimensional quantum system. This process compresses a shape known as the "Bloch ball" in specific directions. Each such channel is associated with a "compression vector" that describes this squeezing.

We investigated the geometric properties (shapes and arrangements) of these compression vectors. We successfully proved a conjecture proposed by Dixit and Sudarshan. Their conjecture stated that when the quantum system consists of @xmath1 qubits and is described using the Pauli basis (a particular mathematical framework), then the collection of all possible compression vectors forms a geometric shape called a simplex.

We then expanded on this finding by examining the geometry of these vectors when using other mathematical bases. Specifically, we identified the precise conditions under which the set of all compression vectors forms a simplex.

original_text: Alta Ribagorça () is one of the comarques of Catalonia, Spain. Its capital is Pont de Suert. The highest peak is the Comaloformo (3030 metres above sea level) in the massif of Bessiberri. Northeast of the region is the western part of the Aigüestortes i Estany de Sant Maurici National Park. It is connected with the Aran valley thorough the Vielha tunnel. The Catalan Romanesque Churches of the Vall de Boí are UNESCO World Heritage Sites.(Nov. 30, 2000). . In the North-East of the area there is the Aigüestortes i Estany de Sant Maurici National Park, which attracts an important number of visitors. The local Ribagorçan dialect is a variant of Catalan, which has some transitional traits to Aragonese. Location: Farthest east point: 0° 58' 27,80" East longitude. Farthest west point: 0° 41' 30,24" East longitude. Farthest north point: 42° 37' 58,88" North latitude. Farthest south point: 42° 18' 0,07" North latitude. Highest point: Comaloformo (3030 m) in the Bessiberri Range. Municipalities References External links Official site of the Consell Comarcal of Alta Ribagorça Information on Alta Ribagorça from the Generalitat de Catalunya Information on Alta Ribagorça from the official site of Lleida

Alta Ribagorça is a place in Catalonia, Spain. Its main town is called Pont de Suert.

The tallest mountain there is Comaloformo. It is 3030 meters high and is part of the Bessiberri mountains.

In the northeast, there is a big park called Aigüestortes i Estany de Sant Maurici National Park. This is the western part of the park. Many people like to visit this park.

You can get to the Aran valley from Alta Ribagorça by going through the Vielha tunnel.

The old churches in the Vall de Boí are very special. UNESCO said they are important places for the whole world to keep safe. This happened on November 30, 2000.

Alta Ribagorça has a specific location on a map. Its easternmost point is 0° 58' 27,80" East. Its westernmost point is 0° 41' 30,24" East. Its northernmost point is 42° 37' 58,88" North. Its southernmost point is 42° 18' 0,07" North.
simplified_secondary_school:
Alta Ribagorça is one of the comarques, or regions, of Catalonia, Spain, with Pont de Suert as its capital. The highest point in the area is Comaloformo, a peak reaching 3030 meters above sea level within the Bessiberri massif.

The western part of the Aigüestortes i Estany de Sant Maurici National Park is located in the northeast of Alta Ribagorça, drawing a significant number of visitors. The region is also connected to the Aran valley by the Vielha tunnel.

Culturally, the Catalan Romanesque Churches of the Vall de Boí are recognized as UNESCO World Heritage Sites, a designation received on November 30, 2000. The local Ribagorçan dialect is a variant of Catalan that shows some characteristics transitioning towards Aragonese.

Alta Ribagorça's geographical location spans: 0° 58' 27.80" East longitude at its farthest east, 0° 41' 30.24" East longitude at its farthest west, 42° 37' 58.88" North latitude at its farthest north, and 42° 18' 0.07" North latitude at its farthest south.

original_text: Spectrometry may refer to: Optical spectrometry, a technique for measuring the distribution of light across the optical spectrum, from the ultraviolet spectral region to the visible and infrared Ion-mobility spectrometry, an analytical technique used to separate and identify ionized molecules in the gas phase based on their ion mobility in a carrier buffer gas Mass spectrometry, an analytical technique that measures the mass-to-charge ratio of charged particles Rutherford backscattering spectrometry, an analytical technique used to determine the structure and composition of materials by measuring the back-scattering of a beam of high energy ions impinging on a sample Neutron triple-axis spectrometry, a technique used in inelastic neutron scattering See also Spectrometer Spectrophotometry Spectroscopy
simplified_kindergarten:
One kind looks at light. It sees how light spreads. It sees colors. It sees light we cannot see, too.

Another kind looks at tiny bits. These bits have a charge. It makes them move in air. It sees how fast they go. This helps to find them.

Another kind looks at tiny bits. These bits have a charge. It sees how heavy they are. It sees their charge too.

Another kind shoots tiny fast bits. It shoots them at things. It sees how the bits bounce back. This tells us what things are made of.

Another kind looks at tiny neutron bits. It sees how they bounce. It sees how they change when they bounce.
simplified_primary_school:
Spectrometry means different ways to look at things, like:

Optical spectrometry is a way to measure how much light there is in different colors. This includes light we can't see, like ultraviolet, light we can see, and light that feels warm, like infrared.

Ion-mobility spectrometry is a way to sort and find tiny charged bits when they are floating in the air. It works by seeing how fast these bits move through a special gas.

Mass spectrometry is a way that measures how heavy tiny charged bits are compared to their electric charge.

Rutherford backscattering spectrometry is a way to find out what things are made of and how they are put together. It works by measuring how a strong stream of tiny charged bits bounces back when it hits a small piece of something.

Neutron triple-axis spectrometry is a way used when tiny particles called neutrons bounce off things and change their energy.

You might also hear about Spectrometer, Spectrophotometry, and Spectroscopy.
simplified_secondary_school: Spectrometry refers to various techniques. Optical spectrometry measures how light is spread across the optical spectrum, from ultraviolet to visible and infrared light. Ion-mobility spectrometry is an analytical technique that separates and identifies charged molecules (ions) in a gas by measuring how fast these ions move through a carrier buffer gas. Mass spectrometry is an analytical technique that measures the ratio of mass to electric charge for charged particles. Rutherford backscattering spectrometry is an analytical technique that determines the structure and composition of materials by measuring how high-energy ions bounce back after hitting a sample. Neutron triple-axis spectrometry is a technique used in the study of inelastic neutron scattering. Related terms include Spectrometer, Spectrophotometry, and Spectroscopy.

original_text: (CNN) -- Egyptian presidential candidate Abdel Moneim Aboul Fotouh was carjacked and beaten late Thursday as he was returning from a conference, his campaign manager said. Aboul Fotouh was being driven on a highway when a car carrying masked gunmen intercepted him, said Ali Behnasawy, the campaign manager. The suspects pulled the driver out of the car and beat him with their guns. Aboul Fotouh tried to intervene and he was also beaten, Behnasawy said. The gunmen then stole the car and fled. Aboul Fotouh was taken to a hospital and was in an intensive care unit because of a head injury, the campaign manager said. Aboul Fotouh, a former member of the Muslim Brotherhood, announced his candidacy last year in the presidential election scheduled for June.

They drove away fast.
Aboul Fotouh went to the hospital.
He was very sick.
His head was hurt.
His helper told us all this.
Aboul Fotouh was in a group before.
It was called Muslim Brotherhood.
Last year, he said he wants to be president.
People will vote for president in June.

original_text: By . Simon Jones . Sunderland have revived their interest in Brighton's Will Buckley and are offering £2.5million for the winger. The 24-year-old, who also attracted interest from Crystal Palace in January, played 30 times in the Championship last season, scoring three goals. Buckley is also being eyed up by West Bromwich Albion, as Alan Irvine looks to rival Gus Poyet for the signature of the highly-rated Englishman. In demand: Will Buckley has attracted interest from Crystal Palace previously - now Sunderland want him . Buckley moved to Brighton for £1million from Watford three years ago, and came off the bench in the club’s 1-0 defeat to Sheffield Wednesday on Saturday. Meanwhile, Brighton boss Sami Hyypia is hoping to return to his former club Liverpool to sign their Jack Robinson on loan. On target: West Brom are also interested in getting hold of the Brighton man but Sunderland are at the front .

original_text: we explore the effects of different boundary conditions and coupling schemes on the response of a particle detector undergoing uniform acceleration in optical cavities . we analyze the thermalization properties of the accelerated detector via non - perturbative calculations . we prove non - perturbatively that if the switching process is smooth enough , the detector thermalizes to the unruh temperature regardless of the boundary conditions and the form of the coupling considered .
simplified_kindergarten:
We look at a small thing. It finds tiny bits. It speeds up. It is in light boxes. We see what it does. We see how it acts with different setups. We see how it connects.

We look at how hot the fast thing gets. We use a special way to count.

We show with our special way to count that if it starts gently, the thing gets to a special warm level. This happens no matter how things are around it. This happens no matter how it connects.
simplified_primary_school:
We look at how a little machine acts. This machine finds tiny bits and is speeding up inside special light boxes. We study how it acts when the edges of the boxes are set up in different ways, and when its parts are joined together in different ways.

We study how the speeding-up machine gets warm. We do this by using very careful math.

We found out for sure with our careful math that if the machine is turned on smoothly enough, it gets warm to a special heat level called the Unruh temperature. This happens no matter how the edges of the boxes are set up, and no matter how the parts are joined together.
simplified_secondary_school: We investigate how a particle detector, accelerating constantly inside optical cavities (special light-filled spaces), responds to different conditions at its edges and various ways it connects to its environment. We analyze how this accelerating detector reaches a stable temperature, or 'thermalizes,' using advanced calculations that avoid simplifying approximations. Through these precise calculations, we prove that if the detector is activated or deactivated smoothly enough, it will always reach a specific temperature called the Unruh temperature. This happens no matter what the conditions are at the boundaries or how the detector is connected.

original_text: we study the quantum deformation of the barrett - crane lorentzian spin foam model which is conjectured to be the discretization of lorentzian plebanski model with positive cosmological constant and includes therefore as a particular sector quantum gravity in de - sitter space . this spin foam model is constructed using harmonic analysis on the quantum lorentz group . the evaluation of simple spin networks are shown to be non commutative integrals over the quantum hyperboloid defined as a pile of fuzzy spheres . we show that the introduction of the cosmological constant , removes all the infrared divergences : for any fixed triangulation , the integration over the area variables is finite for a large class of normalization of the amplitude of the edges and of the faces .
simplified_kindergarten:
We look at a special model. It is called Barrett-Crane spin foam. People think this model is like a drawing of another model. That model is Plebanski. It has a special number. This model also shows how gravity works. It works in a special place called de Sitter space.

We make this spin foam model with a special math tool. It uses something called quantum Lorentz group.

When we look at simple spin networks, we see special math sums. These sums are on a shape called quantum hyperboloid. This shape is like many fuzzy balls stacked up.

We see that adding the special number makes all the bad problems go away. If we draw it with triangles, the math sums for the areas are not too big. This works for many ways to measure the sides and faces.
simplified_primary_school:
We are studying a special math model. It is called the 'quantum deformation of the Lorentzian Barrett-Crane spin foam model'. People think this model is a simpler version of another model, the 'Lorentzian Plebanski model'. This other model has a 'positive cosmological constant'. Our special model helps us understand how gravity works at a very tiny level in a special place called 'de Sitter space'.

This spin foam model is built using a math tool called 'harmonic analysis'. This is done on something called the 'quantum Lorentz group'.

When we look at simple 'spin networks', we find they are special math sums. These sums are called 'non-commutative integrals'. They happen over something called the 'quantum hyperboloid'. This 'quantum hyperboloid' is like a stack of 'fuzzy spheres'.

We found that when we add the 'cosmological constant', it fixes a big problem. It gets rid of all the 'infrared divergences'. This means that no matter how we break down the space into small parts (called a 'triangulation'), the math sums for the 'area variables' always give a clear, final answer. This works for many ways we measure the strength of the 'edges' and 'faces' in our model.
simplified_secondary_school:
Our research focuses on a quantum version of the Barrett-Crane Lorentzian spin foam model. Scientists believe this model is a discrete (step-by-step) representation of the Lorentzian Plebanski model, which includes a positive cosmological constant. This means it describes quantum gravity specifically within a de Sitter universe. This spin foam model is built using a mathematical method called harmonic analysis, applied to the quantum Lorentz group.

When we calculate simple spin networks, the results are special types of integrals (non-commutative integrals) over a quantum hyperboloid. This quantum hyperboloid can be imagined as many 'fuzzy spheres' stacked together, where 'fuzzy' means the spheres don't have perfectly defined points.

We demonstrate that adding the cosmological constant solves a major problem: it removes all 'infrared divergences.' These divergences are issues where calculations would otherwise give infinite results. This means that for any specific way of dividing space into smaller pieces (called a triangulation), calculations involving area variables always result in a finite, definite number. This holds true for many different ways of setting the strength (amplitude) of the connections (edges) and surfaces (faces) in our model.

original_text: Open circuit may refer to: Open-circuit scuba, a type of SCUBA-diving equipment where the user breathes from the set and then exhales to the surroundings without recycling the exhaled air Open-circuit test, a method used in electrical engineering to determine the impedance in the excitation branch of a real transformer Open-circuit voltage, the difference of electrical potential between two terminals of a device when there is no external load connected An electrical circuit is an "open circuit" if it lacks a complete path between the terminals of its power source See also Closed-circuit (disambiguation) Short circuit (disambiguation) Open system (disambiguation)
simplified_kindergarten:
Open scuba is a diving gear. A person breathes air from it. They breathe out into the water. The air they breathe out does not go back into the gear.

An open test is a way to check power things. It helps find how hard it is for power to flow in a part of a big power box.

Open voltage is how much push power has. It is between two ends of a thing. This is when nothing else is hooked up to it.

A power path is "open" if it does not have a full way for power to go from its start to its end.

Look also at: Closed path. Short path. Open way.
simplified_primary_school:
The words "open circuit" can mean different things.

One is "open-circuit scuba." This is a type of diving gear. A diver breathes air from a tank. Then, they breathe out into the water. The air they breathe out is not used again.

Another is an "open-circuit test." This is a way people who work with electricity check a special part inside a machine called a transformer. They want to find out how much it resists electricity.

A third is "open-circuit voltage." This is how much electricity is waiting between two points on a device. This happens when nothing else is plugged into it.

In electricity, a path for power is called a circuit. A circuit is "open" if the electricity cannot flow all the way around. It's like a broken path from where the power starts.

You can also learn about "closed circuits," "short circuits," and "open systems."
simplified_secondary_school:
An electrical circuit is called an "open circuit" when there isn't a complete path for electricity to flow between the power source's terminals.

The phrase "open-circuit" is also used in several contexts, including:

*   **Open-circuit scuba:** This is a type of SCUBA diving equipment where the diver breathes air from the tank and then exhales it directly into the surrounding water, rather than recycling the exhaled air.
*   **Open-circuit test:** In electrical engineering, this is a method used to find the impedance in the excitation branch of a real transformer.
*   **Open-circuit voltage:** This is the difference in electrical potential between two terminals of a device when no external load is connected.

See also: Closed-circuit, Short circuit, Open system.

When it is cold, Ar(H) likes a shape called cubic C15. This shape is like MgCu. It does not like the hexagonal C14 shape. That shape is like MgZn. People thought it liked the hexagonal shape before.

original_text: (CNN) -- Jimmy Ellis, who belted out the dance anthem "Disco Inferno" in the 1970s for the Trammps, died Thursday in South Carolina, the funeral home handling arrangements said. He was 74. Ellis' rich baritone powered the funky Grammy-winning tune from the "Saturday Night Fever" album. It is only second to "The Bodyguard" as the best-selling soundtrack album of all time with sales of 15 million. Despite the success associated with the hit movie, Ellis and the band couldn't translate it into mainstream appeal, scoring just three R&B Top 10 hits from 1972 to 1978. But the group still commanded respect in the music community. "But the Trammps' prowess can't be measured by chart popularity. Ellis' booming, joyous vocals brilliantly championed the celebratory fervor and atmosphere that made disco both loved and hated among music fans," the group's biography on allmusic.com says. The Trammps began in Philadelphia in 1972. Ellis' memorial service is scheduled for March 16 at Central Church of God in Charlotte, North Carolina. People we've lost in 2012: The lives they lived .

The "Saturday Night Fever" album sold 15 million copies. It is the second best-selling album from a movie ever. Only "The Bodyguard" album sold more.

Even though the movie was very popular, Jimmy Ellis and The Trammps did not have many other big hit songs. They only had three songs that made it into the top 10 on the R&B music charts between 1972 and 1978. But other musicians still thought they were very good.

A story about the group says that how good The Trammps were cannot be measured by how popular their songs were. It says Jimmy Ellis' loud and happy singing showed the fun and party feeling of disco music. Disco music was loved by some people and not liked by others.

A special service to remember Jimmy Ellis will be held on March 16. It will be at the Central Church of God in Charlotte, North Carolina.

Jimmy Ellis, the lead singer of the band The Trammps, known for the 1970s dance hit "Disco Inferno," passed away on Thursday in South Carolina at the age of 74. This information was confirmed by the funeral home handling his arrangements.

His powerful, deep voice was central to "Disco Inferno," a funky, Grammy-winning song featured on the "Saturday Night Fever" soundtrack album. This soundtrack sold 15 million copies, making it the second best-selling soundtrack of all time, only behind "The Bodyguard."

Even with the huge success of the "Saturday Night Fever" movie and soundtrack, Ellis and The Trammps struggled to achieve widespread mainstream popularity. Between 1972 and 1978, they only had three songs reach the Top 10 on the R&B charts.

Despite their limited chart success, the band earned significant respect within the music industry. According to their biography on allmusic.com, The Trammps' true skill wasn't just about chart hits. Instead, Ellis's strong, joyful singing perfectly captured the exciting and festive spirit of disco, a genre that was both adored and disliked by music fans.

The Trammps formed in Philadelphia in 1972. A memorial service for Ellis is planned for March 16 at Central Church of God in Charlotte, North Carolina.

original_text: In telecommunication, insertion gain is the gain resulting from the insertion of a device in a transmission line, expressed as the ratio of the signal power delivered to that part of the line following the device to the signal power delivered to that same part before insertion. If the resulting number is less than unity, an "insertion loss" is indicated. Insertion gain is usually expressed in dB. References Telecommunications engineering

Power goes on the wire.
You put a small box in the wire.
We look at the power after the box.
We look at the power before the box.
If the power is bigger after the box, it is called insertion gain.
This gain tells us how much bigger the power got.
If the power is smaller after the box, it is called insertion loss.
We often say this gain in "dB".
simplified_primary_school: When we send messages, 'insertion gain' is how much a signal gets stronger when you put a special part into a wire. You find it by comparing how strong the signal is *after* the part is in, to how strong it was *before* the part was in, at the same spot in the wire. If the number you get is smaller than one, it means the signal actually got weaker. This is called 'insertion loss'. We usually measure insertion gain using something called 'dB'.
simplified_secondary_school: In telecommunications, insertion gain describes the change in signal strength that occurs when a device is placed into a transmission line. It is calculated as the ratio of the signal power at a specific point *after* the device has been inserted, compared to the signal power at that *same point* *before* the device was inserted. If this calculated ratio is less than one, it means there is an "insertion loss" instead of a gain. Insertion gain is usually expressed in decibels (dB).

original_text: the study of eclipsing binaries is our primary source of measured properties of normal stars , achieved through analysis of light and radial velocity curves of eclipsing systems . the study of oscillations and pulsations is increasingly vital for determining the properties of single stars , and investigating the physical phenomena active in their interiors . combining the two methods holds the promise of establishing stringent tests of stellar evolutionary theory , and of calibrating model - dependent asteroseismology with empirically measured stellar properties . i review recent advances and outline future work .
simplified_kindergarten:
Some stars hide each other. We watch them. We see their light. We see how they move. This helps us learn about stars.

Stars also jiggle and pulse. This helps us learn about stars that are alone. It tells us what happens inside them. This is very important.

If we use both ways, we can check our ideas about how stars grow. We can make our star models better. We use what we see to help us.

I will talk about new things we learned. I will talk about what we will do next.
simplified_primary_school: Looking at two stars that go around each other, where one sometimes hides the other's light, is the main way we learn about regular stars. We do this by watching how their light changes and how fast they move. Learning about how stars wobble and pulse is becoming very important. It helps us find out things about stars that are by themselves, and to see what is happening deep inside them. Putting these two ways of learning together could help us really check how stars change over time. It could also help us make sure our ideas about how stars wobble are right, by using what we actually see about stars. I will talk about new things we have learned and what we need to do next.
simplified_secondary_school: Our main way to learn about the properties of normal stars is by studying eclipsing binaries. These are star systems where two stars orbit each other and periodically block each other's light. We analyze how their brightness changes (light curves) and how their speed towards or away from us changes (radial velocity curves). The study of how stars oscillate and pulsate (vibrate) is becoming increasingly important. It helps us determine the properties of single stars and investigate the physical processes happening deep inside them. Combining these two methods offers a powerful way to strictly test our theories about how stars evolve. It also allows us to make asteroseismology – the study of a star's interior using its vibrations, which relies on theoretical models – more accurate by comparing it with actual, measured stellar properties.

simplified_primary_school:
A resource is something we can use that helps us. Sometimes there is not much of it, but sometimes there is a lot.

Resource can also mean these things:
Natural resources are things we get from nature. They help people get what they need and want.
Water resources are places where we get water. This water is helpful or could be helpful.
In biology, a resource is something living things need to stay alive, grow, and have babies.
In business, a resource is something like a product, a job someone does, or other helpful things. These are used to make things and do jobs for people. This includes people. Their skills, energy, talents, what they can do, and what they know are used to make things.
For planning projects, a resource is a helpful thing used when planning what to do.
In computers, a resource is something real or something in the computer that there is not much of. For example, computer memory, how fast it works, and how fast the internet is.

A "resource fork" is information that goes with a Mac computer file.
A "resource" in Windows is information inside special computer files called EXE and DLL files.

simplified_secondary_school:
A resource is a source or supply from which benefit is produced. These are typically, but not necessarily, of limited availability.

The term "resource" can also refer to:

*   **Natural resources:** Anything obtained from the environment to satisfy human needs and wants.
*   **Water resources:** Sources of water that are useful or potentially useful.
*   **In biology:** Substances or objects a biological organism needs for normal maintenance, growth, and reproduction.
*   **In economics:** A commodity, service, or other asset used to produce goods and services. This includes **Human resources (HR)**, which are the skills, energy, talents, abilities, and knowledge people use for production.
*   **In project management:** Economic resources used when planning tasks.
*   **In computing:** Physical or virtual items that have limited availability, such as memory, processing capacity, and network speed. Specific uses of the term in computing include:
    *   **Computational resource:** A resource used for solving a computational problem.
    *   **Web resource:** Anything identified by a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI) that can be found at a specific location.
    *   **Resource fork:** Data associated with a Mac OS file.
    *   **Resource (Windows):** Data embedded within EXE and DLL files.
    *   **Resource (Java):** Data used by an application.
*   **Resource (band):** A former German electronic dance group.

*   Johannes Müller (1864–1949) was a theologian.

original_text: Dachau () is a Landkreis (district) in Bavaria, Germany. It is bounded by (from the south and clockwise) the districts of Fürstenfeldbruck, Aichach-Friedberg, Pfaffenhofen, Freising and Munich, and by the city of Munich. History The district was established in 1952. There were slight changes to the territory in the administrative reform of 1972. Geography The district extends from the northwestern suburbs of Munich to the so-called Dachauer Land. It is a hilly countryside, which is now densely populated. The growing Munich metropolitan area is advancing more and more into the district's territory. Coat of arms The arms include a red zigzag line on white background, which was the heraldic figure of the Wittelsbach family during the 12th and 13th century. Although it was not used anymore from the 14th century on, it is now part of the arms of Dachau in order to symbolise, that Dachau is an old part of the Bavarian state. In the upper part of the arms the blue and white checkered pattern of Bavaria is displayed. Towns and municipalities References External links (German) Districts of Bavaria

The district started in 1952. Its land changed a little in 1972.

The Dachau district was created in 1952. Its borders were slightly adjusted during an administrative reform in 1972.

The district stretches from the northwestern suburbs of Munich into the area known as Dachauer Land. It is a hilly region that has become densely populated. The expanding metropolitan area of Munich is increasingly extending into the district.

The district's coat of arms features a red zigzag line on a white background. This design was the symbol of the Wittelsbach family in the 12th and 13th centuries. Even though the family stopped using it after the 13th century, it is now included in Dachau's coat of arms to show that Dachau has long been a part of the Bavarian state. The upper section of the coat of arms also displays Bavaria's traditional blue and white checkered pattern.

simplified_kindergarten:
We look at how some things push each other from far away. It is a special push.
When this push changes, things stop lining up easily. It gets much colder before they line up.
This happens because the far-away pushes make things very mixed up.
A liquid that is almost stuck stays around. It is in a big, special state.
