simplified_primary_school: We look at how some things push each other away, even from far away. This pushing is like a special kind of electricity. We found that if this pushing gets weaker in a certain way, the temperature where things usually line up neatly gets much, much lower. This happens because the far-away pushing makes it very hard for things to get organized. Then, a special kind of liquid that almost stops moving can still exist in a wide area. In this area, everything changes in a strange way when the temperature changes. But, right before things line up, they act the same way as when things only push each other from very close by.
simplified_secondary_school:
This research investigates mathematical models that describe systems with long-range repulsive forces, similar to a generalized version of the Coulomb interaction between charged particles.

We found that when a specific parameter, the 'interaction exponent,' is reduced within a certain range, it significantly lowers the 'charge ordering temperature'—the temperature at which charges typically arrange into an ordered pattern. This effect occurs regardless of the number of spatial dimensions and is due to strong 'geometric frustration' caused by these long-range interactions. Geometric frustration means the particles struggle to settle into stable, ordered positions because of conflicting forces acting over long distances.

Under these conditions, a state resembling a 'nearly frozen Coulomb liquid' (a liquid of charged particles that is almost stuck in place) persists within a wide 'pseudogap phase'. This pseudogap phase is unique because all its measurable properties show an unusual and distinct way of changing with temperature.

However, the main way the system behaves at the critical point, very close to the charge-ordering temperature, remains identical to what is observed in models where forces only act over short distances.

original_text: Year 466 BC was a year of the pre-Julian Roman calendar. At the time, it was known as the Year of the Consulship of Priscus and Albinus (or, less frequently, year 288 Ab urbe condita). The denomination 466 BC for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the prevalent method in Europe for naming years. Events By place Greece Cimon carries the war against Persia into Asia Minor and wins the Battle of the Eurymedon in Pamphylia. This is a decisive defeat of the Persians as Cimon's land and sea forces capture the Persian camp and destroy or capture the entire Persian fleet of 200 triremes (manned by Phoenicians). Many new allies of Athens are now recruited, such as the trading city of Phaselis on the Lycian-Pamphylian border. Italy The Tyrant, Thrasybulus, is driven out by the citizens of Syracuse, Sicily. The city moves to a democratic system of government. The Greek colony of Taras, in Magna Graecia, is defeated by the native population of Apulia. As a result, the Tarentine monarchy falls, with the installation of a democracy. Births Deaths King Xerxes I of Persia. References

In Italy, a bad ruler named Thrasybulus was sent away. The people of Syracuse sent him away. The city got a new way to rule. The people could choose their leaders.

A Greek city called Taras lost a fight. People from Apulia beat them. So, the king of Taras lost his power. The city got a new way to rule. The people could choose their leaders.

King Xerxes of Persia died.

People started using this name much later, in the Middle Ages, when they began using the calendar we use today to name years in Europe.

What happened:

This was a very big loss for the Persians. Cimon's soldiers on land and his ships at sea took over the Persian camp. They also broke or took all 200 Persian ships, which were called triremes and had sailors from Phoenicia. Many new friends joined Athens. One was a trading city called Phaselis, which was near Lycia and Pamphylia.

In Italy:
In Syracuse, a city in Sicily, the people made their bad ruler, Thrasybulus, leave. The city then started to let its people choose their leaders. This is called a democracy.
A Greek town called Taras, in a place called Magna Graecia, was beaten by the people who already lived in Apulia. Because of this, the kings who ruled Taras lost their power. The city then started to let its people choose their leaders.

simplified_secondary_school:
The year 466 BC was part of the pre-Julian Roman calendar system. At the time, Romans referred to it as the "Year of the Consulship of Priscus and Albinus," named after their two leading officials, or sometimes as "year 288 Ab urbe condita," meaning 288 years since the founding of Rome. The designation "466 BC" for this year began to be used in the early medieval period, once the Anno Domini calendar system became the main way to name years across Europe.

**Events**

**Greece**
The Athenian general Cimon took the war against Persia into Asia Minor, achieving a decisive victory at the Battle of the Eurymedon in Pamphylia. This battle was a major defeat for the Persians: Cimon's land and sea forces captured the Persian camp and either destroyed or seized their entire fleet of 200 triremes, which were manned by Phoenicians. As a result, Athens gained many new allies, including the important trading city of Phaselis, located on the border between Lycia and Pamphylia.

**Italy**
In Syracuse, Sicily, the citizens expelled their ruler, the Tyrant Thrasybulus. The city then transitioned to a democratic system of government. Elsewhere in Magna Graecia (the Greek areas of southern Italy), the Greek colony of Taras suffered a defeat at the hands of the native population of Apulia. This defeat led to the downfall of the Tarentine monarchy and the establishment of a democracy in its place.

**Deaths**
King Xerxes I of Persia died this year.

We used some special math ideas to show something new.

Imagine you have any kind of shape or space, and you slice it up into many layers or lines. We found a way to change these slices, either smoothly or with straight lines. After we change them, all the smallest repeating parts of these slices will be like flat surfaces or lines, always one size smaller than the space they are in.

For example, imagine a special 3D ball shape. We can make a smooth moving pattern on it. In this pattern, all the places where things end up will be like flat surfaces.

We also found that if you slice up a 3D shape (or bigger) into just lines, you can change these lines using only straight parts. After this change, none of the lines will form a complete loop. But all the smallest repeating parts of the pattern will still be lines.

These new findings show that an old idea, called the Seifert idea, is not always true. The Seifert idea said that any moving pattern on a 3D ball shape, if it doesn't have any special 'stop' points, must always have a path that goes in a circle and repeats itself.
simplified_secondary_school:
Using mathematical techniques called the 'theory of plugs' and a 'self-insertion construction' (developed by the second author), we proved that any foliation (a way of dividing a geometric space, or manifold, into layers) can be modified. This modification can be very smooth (real analytic) or made of flat pieces (piecewise-linear). The result is that all the 'minimal sets' (the smallest regions where the system's long-term behavior settles) will have a dimension one less than the space they are in (codimension 1).

Specifically, this means that the 3-sphere (a 3-dimensional sphere) can have a very smooth dynamical system where all 'limit sets' (the regions where trajectories eventually settle) are 2-dimensional.

We also showed that a 1-dimensional foliation (where the layers are like lines) on a manifold with at least 3 dimensions can be changed using flat pieces. After this change, there will be no closed layers, but all the minimal sets will still be 1-dimensional.

These findings provide new examples that contradict the Seifert conjecture. This conjecture stated that any dynamical system on the 3-sphere with no 'singular points' (places where the system stops or is undefined) must always have a 'periodic trajectory' (a path that repeats itself).

original_text: (CNN) -- A retired union president was indicted by a federal grand jury Thursday and is accused of using his position to steal hundreds of thousands of dollars. The charges against Terence J. Bonner, former president of the National Border Patrol Union, include wire fraud, said United States Attorney Laura E. Duffy. Bonner, who is from California, is accused of taking payments for union activities and using it for himself in a scheme to "defraud some 14,000 dues-paying union members," Duffy said. "These false claims included periods of time when Bonner was actually visiting his mistress in Chicago or family members, as well as trips to attend non-Union activities, such as hockey games and other sporting events," the indictment says. Bonner is also accused of filing for reimbursement for travel expenses for personal trips, Duffy said. "Siphoning hundreds of thousands of dollars from hard-working fellow Border Patrol agents, many of whom put their lives on the line every day to protect this country, is a particularly troubling form of corruption that must be addressed," Duffy said. Bonner was arrested and scheduled to be in court for his first appearance on Monday, August 20.

What's more, unlike all other directional methods, a theory was developed for "compactly supported shearlet frames" that also achieve this optimal sparsity.

For two years, people looked for the plane but could not find it. Then, a French group that looks into plane accidents asked the writers of this paper for help. They wanted the writers to figure out the most likely places where the plane's parts might be. The writers had to use all the information they had about where the plane crashed and where people had already looked.

The writers used a special math method to help plan the search. This method helped them find the most likely spot where the plane's parts would be. This distribution considered all available information about the crash site and previous search efforts. We used a Bayesian method, originally developed for search planning, to produce the posterior probability distribution for the target's location.

original_text: (CNN) -- IKEA has recalled eight of its children's wall-mounted lamps after one toddler died and another was injured, the Consumer Product Safety Commission said Wednesday. In each case, the lamp cord was pulled into the crib by the infant, creating the strangulation hazard. Both incidents took place in Europe. There are eight different models involved in the recall: a blue star, a yellow moon, a pink flower, a white flower, a red heart, green bug, a blue seashell and an orange seahorse. Customers who have one of the lamps should immediately check that the lamp and cord are out of reach of children and contact IKEA customer service to receive a repair kit free of charge, the retailer said. Customers can call IKEA toll-free at (888) 966-4532, or online at www.ikea-usa.com. There's a recall link at the top of the page for more information. CNN's Scott Thompson contributed to this report.

original_text: Hagfors Municipality (Hagfors kommun) is a municipality in Värmland County in west central Sweden. Its seat is located in the city of Hagfors. The present municipality was created in 1974, when the City of Hagfors (itself instituted as a municipal entity in 1950 as one of the last cities in Sweden) was amalgamated with three rural municipalities. Being sparsely populated, Hagfors Municipality has natural areas that offer good fishing, canoeing and wild life. Because it is sparsely populated, Hagfors Municipality has natural areas that offer good opportunities for fishing, canoeing, and observing wildlife. The municipality includes several localities: Bergsäng, Edebäck, Ekshärad, Geijersholm, Hagfors (the seat), Mjönäs, Råda, Sunnemo, and Uddeholm.

original_text: (CNN) -- A French Red Cross official abducted by militants in Yemen has been freed, according to a statement from the International Committee of the Red Cross. Benjamin Malbrancke had been kidnapped the evening of April 21 about 30 kilometers outside the western municipality of Hodeida, while traveling in a Red Cross vehicle. "We are relieved and extremely happy to have our colleague back with us, in good health. His return marks the end of many long, hard days, full of anxiety for him and his family, as well as for the team here in Yemen and for his friends," said Eric Marclay, who heads the ICRC operations in Yemen. "We would like to express our deep gratitude to all those who have lent us their support during these long weeks, so that Benjamin can now look forward to soon rejoining his family." Malbrancke had been traveling with two Yemeni drivers, who were also taken but then released unharmed. Kidnappings are common in Yemen, which has been beset by political turbulence for more than a year. Protests led to the departure of President Ali Abdullah Saleh from office, and the government has battled Islamic militants.

original_text: Mākutu in the Māori language of New Zealand means "witchcraft", "sorcery", "to bewitch"; and also a "spell or incantation". It may also be described as a belief in malignant occult powers possessed by certain people. An October 2007 mākutu-lifting in the Lower Hutt suburb of Wainuiomata led to the death by drowning of a woman and the hospitalisation of a teen, allegedly due to attempts to remove such a curse. See also Tohunga References Māori mythology Asian witchcraft Māori religion

In October 2007, in a place called Wainuiomata in New Zealand, some people tried to take away mākutu. A woman died by drowning, and a teenager had to go to the hospital. People said this happened because they were trying to remove a magic curse.

In October 2007, an event known as a 'mākutu-lifting' took place in Wainuiomata, a suburb of Lower Hutt. This attempt to remove a curse allegedly resulted in a woman's death by drowning and a teenager being hospitalized.

original_text: (CNN) -- Tropical Storm Haikui made landfall Wednesday morning in Zhejiang province, where hundreds of thousands of people had been evacuated from its path. "The rain is the bigger impact going forward," said CNNI Meteorologist Taylor Ward, who noted that Haikui had diminished from typhoon status before hitting land about 225 kilometers (140 miles) south of Shanghai. "We have already had up to 8 inches in some locations." Ward said another 6 to 10 inches of rain were expected to fall, with "maybe isolated amounts greater." Haikui was moving northwest at 20 kph (12 mph) but was expected to slow over the coming two days, he said. Chinese officials had evacuated 374,000 people from Shanghai and 250,000 from Zhejiang, according to the state-run China Daily. Haikui, which had been a typhoon on Tuesday, is the third tropical storm to hit China's east coast in less than a week.

original_text: (CNN) -- The captain of an Italian cruise ship foiled an attack by pirates off the coast of Somalia on Saturday by ordering his security crew to fire back, Italy's ANSA news agency reported. Six armed pirates in a speedboat attacked the ship, which had about 1,500 people onboard, ANSA said. They fled after the security crew fired back. The cruise ship Melody, which is owned by an Italian company, was on its way back to Italy after a cruise, ANSA said. No one was injured in the attack about 180 miles north of the Seychelles. Piracy is rampant off the coast of Somalia despite the presence of foreign navies. The area hardest hit off the coasts of Somalia and Kenya, including the Gulf of Aden, is more than 1.1 million square miles -- four times the size of the state of Texas.

A labor court has issued an injunction, ordering the unions to maintain staffing at 80 percent of normal levels, or they will face fines of up to £13,400.

simplified_primary_school:
Ansgar Elde was an artist from Sweden. He was born on November 27, 1933, and he died on December 4, 2000. He was well-known for making art with clay, called ceramics.

Ansgar was part of a group called the Situationist International, or SI. On March 15, 1962, Ansgar and another person named Jørgen Nash were asked to leave the SI group. Both Ansgar and Jørgen were important leaders in the group. People said they were secretly working with some art collectors.

But really, Ansgar and Jørgen were helping other artists. These artists were Ervin Eisch, Lothar Fischer, Dieter Kunzelmann, Renee Nele, Heimrad Prem, Gretel Stadler, Helmut Sturm, and Hand-Peter Zimmer. These eight artists had been asked to leave the group earlier, on February 10, 1962.

After that, Ansgar, Jørgen, and some other people started a new group. They called it the Second Situationist International.

Ansgar also worked with another artist named Asger Jorn. They made a very big art piece out of clay. They made it in a place called Albisola in Italy. Then, they moved it by train to Aarhus in Denmark.

In the early 1990s, an artist named Heike Arndt met Ansgar. He asked her to come to Savona, Italy, and she did. There, she made her own art.
simplified_secondary_school: Ansgar Elde (born November 27, 1933, died December 4, 2000) was a Swedish artist known for his ceramic work. Elde was a member of the Situationist International (SI). On March 15, 1962, he and Jørgen Nash were removed from the SI. They had both served on the central committee and were accused of secretly working with art collectors. However, they were actually defending eight other artists—Ervin Eisch, Lothar Fischer, Dieter Kunzelmann, Renee Nele, Heimrad Prem, Gretel Stadler, Helmut Sturm, and Hand-Peter Zimmer—who had been removed from the group earlier, on February 10, 1962. Afterward, Elde, Jørgen Nash, and others began working on the Second Situationist International. Elde also worked with artist Asger Jorn on a large ceramic piece. This artwork was created in Albisola, Italy, and then transported by train to Aarhus, Denmark. In the early 1990s, Heike Arndt met Elde and accepted his invitation to Savona, Italy, where she further developed her own artistic work.

simplified_kindergarten:
When a tiny anti-neutrino hits something. Many people want to learn about this. It helps with big science tests. These tests watch how tiny things called neutrinos move.

We figure out how these things hit and bounce. We use a smart math way to look at it. We also think about tiny extra changes.

In our smart math way, tiny extra changes that happen very close become special numbers. We can find these numbers from what we know about how neutrons break apart.

In our smart math way for tiny things, the tiny extra changes are as big as how much the big parts move back. This also has a tiny push from something called weak magnetism. We also figured out how much the big parts move back when they get hit.

This is important. Our smart math way helps us find even more tiny changes. It tells us how sure we are about our answers.

There is a special reaction called @xmath0. It involves tiny particles called low-energy anti-neutrinos. This reaction is very important right now. It helps with special tests that are happening to see how tiny particles called neutrinos change very accurately.

We figure out how likely this reaction is to happen. We use a special way of thinking about physics called 'EFT'. This way helps us understand things without needing one specific idea. We also look at tiny extra changes called 'radiative corrections' that are of a certain size, @xmath1.

In EFT, the tiny extra changes that happen very close by are put into special numbers. We call these 'low-energy constants'. We can find out what these numbers are by looking at information from how neutrons break apart.

In our special way of using EFT for low-energy things, the tiny extra changes of size @xmath1 are thought to be about the same size as other changes. These other changes are called 'nucleon recoil corrections'. They also include something called 'weak magnetism'. We also figured out these 'recoil corrections'.

We want to say that EFT helps us figure out even more tiny changes in a clear, step-by-step way. This helps us guess how sure we are about our answers.

This work was done by U. Raha, F. Myhrer, and K. Kubodera. They are from the Department of Physics and Astronomy at the University of South Carolina in Columbia, SC 29208.
simplified_secondary_school:
We are studying a specific low-energy antineutrino reaction, @xmath0, which is very important for current, highly accurate experiments investigating how neutrinos change their type (neutrino oscillation). We calculated the differential cross section (a measure of how likely the reaction is to occur) using a method called effective field theory (EFT). EFT is a way to describe physics at low energies without relying on a specific detailed model. Our calculations include small adjustments called "radiative corrections" of a specific magnitude, @xmath1.

In EFT, complex short-distance quantum effects (radiative corrections) are represented by simpler "low-energy constants." The values of these constants can, in theory, be found from existing data on neutron beta-decay. In our low-energy EFT, we treat these @xmath1 radiative corrections as being as important as "nucleon recoil corrections." These recoil corrections describe the movement of the nucleus after the reaction and include a specific effect called "weak magnetism." We have also calculated these recoil corrections.

It's important to note that EFT allows us to systematically calculate even smaller, higher-order corrections. This helps us estimate the theoretical uncertainties in our final results, making them more reliable.

"Italian" can refer to:

*   **The people:**

*   **The language:**
    *   **Italian language:** A Romance language.
    *   **Regional Italian:** Different versions or dialects of the Italian language spoken in various regions.
    *   **Languages of Italy:** All the different languages and dialects spoken within Italy, not just standard Italian.

*   **Cultural aspects:**
    *   **Italian culture:** The traditions, arts, and way of life in Italy.
    *   **Italian cuisine:** The traditional foods of Italy.
    *   **Folklore of Italy:** The traditional stories, beliefs, and urban legends from Italy.
    *   **Mythology of Italy:** The ancient religions and beliefs of Italy.

    *   **Italian or Italian-A:** Older names for the Ping-Pong virus, a computer virus that no longer exists.

original_text: The Hector Hodler Library is one of the largest Esperanto libraries, with approximately 30,000 books in addition to periodicals, manuscripts, photos, music, and other collections. It occupies three rooms in the central office of the Universal Esperanto Association (UEA) in Rotterdam, Netherlands. The Swiss Esperanto Society opened the library in Switzerland in 1908. In 1912, the library came into the possession of Hector Hodler, the founder of UEA, and after Hodler's death in 1920 the library remained under the management of UEA in Switzerland. In 1947, the library was renamed the Hector Hodler Library, and when UEA headquarters relocated to Rotterdam, the library was moved there in 1960. Other major collections of Esperanto books are at the International Esperanto Museum, the Montagu C. Butler Library, the Center for Documentation and Study about the International Language in Switzerland, and the German Esperanto Library. See also List of libraries in the Netherlands References External links About the library Libraries in the Netherlands Esperanto libraries Buildings and structures in Rotterdam Education in South Holland Esperanto in the Netherlands

original_text: (CNN) -- University of Arkansas authorities say they see no "suspicious circumstances" surrounding the death of a 19-year-old football player who was was found dead in his room Sunday. Garrett Uekman, a sophomore tight end for the Razorbacks, was found unconscious and unresponsive in his room about 11:15 a.m., the university said in a written statement Sunday afternoon. Attempts to revive him were unsuccessful, and he was pronounced dead at a nearby hospital shortly after noon. A roommate last saw Uekman playing video games about an hour earlier, "and he appeared to be in good health," the university said. An autopsy will be performed. "The cause of Uekman's death is not known at this time, but there are no suspicious circumstances," the statement said. Uekman, of Little Rock, "was living his dream of going to the U of A and playing football for the Razorbacks," parents Danny and Michelle Uekman said in a statement released through the school. He appeared in nine games for the 10-1 Arkansas squad this season.

original_text: Year 130 BC was a year of the pre-Julian Roman calendar. At the time it was known as the Year of the Consulship of Lentulus/Pulcher and Perperna (or, less frequently, year 624 Ab urbe condita) and the Fifth Year of Yuanguang. The denomination 130 BC for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the prevalent method in Europe for naming years. original_text: Haaltert () is a municipality located in the Belgian province of East Flanders in the Denderstreek. The municipality comprises the towns of Denderhoutem, Haaltert proper, Heldergem and Kerksken. On January 1, 2006, Haaltert had a total population of 17,255. The total area is 30.30 km² which gives a population density of 569 inhabitants per km². The current mayor of Haaltert is Veerle Baeyens, from the N-VA. There is a 205 hectare nature reserve Den Dotter in the sub-municipalities of Aaigem (municipality Erpe-Mere) and Heldergem (municipality Haaltert). Haaltert is crossed by the Molenbeek-Ter Erpenbeek creek in Heldergem, Kerksken, and Haaltert. Gallery References External links Official website Municipalities of East Flanders

Zvonareva could potentially meet Serena in the single's final if both win their semifinals on Thursday. The Russian plays Tsvetana Pironkova, from Bulgaria, who beat Venus on Tuesday. original_text: Tephrite is an igneous, volcanic (extrusive) rock, with aphanitic to porphyritic texture. Mineral content is usually abundant feldspathoids (leucite or nepheline), plagioclase, and lesser alkali feldspar. Pyroxenes (clinopyroxenes) are common accessory minerals. Quartz and olivine are absent. Occurrences include leucite nepheline tephrite from Hamberg bei Neckarelz near Heidelberg, Germany, phonolite-tephrite at Monte Vulture, Basilicata, Italy and basanite–tephrite intrusions in Namibia. References Volcanic rocks

These are like leucite and nepheline.
It also has plagioclase.
And a little alkali feldspar.
It often has pyroxenes too.
It does not have quartz.
It does not have olivine.
You can find this rock in some places.
Like in Germany.
And in Italy.
And in Namibia.
simplified_primary_school:
Tephrite is a rock that comes from volcanoes. It cools outside the volcano. It can have very tiny parts that are hard to see, or it can have some bigger parts mixed in with the tiny ones.

Inside the rock, there are many small parts called minerals. It usually has a lot of minerals called feldspathoids, like leucite or nepheline. It also has plagioclase and a little bit of alkali feldspar. You can often find other minerals in it too, like pyroxenes. It does not have quartz or olivine minerals.

You can find this rock in different places around the world. For example, a type called leucite nepheprite tephrite is found in Germany, near a place called Heidelberg. Another type, phonolite-tephrite, is found in Italy, at Monte Vulture. And basanite–tephrite is found in Namibia, where it formed by pushing into other rocks underground.
simplified_secondary_school: Tephrite is an igneous rock that forms when lava cools on the Earth's surface (volcanic or extrusive). Its texture can be aphanitic (meaning its crystals are too small to see without a microscope) or porphyritic (meaning it has larger crystals set in a finer-grained background). The rock usually contains abundant feldspathoids (such as leucite or nepheline), plagioclase, and a lesser amount of alkali feldspar. Pyroxenes (specifically clinopyroxenes) are common accessory minerals, meaning they are often present in smaller amounts. Tephrite does not contain quartz or olivine. Examples of where tephrite is found include leucite nepheline tephrite from Hamberg bei Neckarelz near Heidelberg, Germany; phonolite-tephrite at Monte Vulture, Basilicata, Italy; and basanite–tephrite intrusions in Namibia.

original_text: Finland is a sovereign state in Northern Europe. It was a relative latecomer to industrialization, remaining a largely agrarian country until the 1950s. It rapidly developed an advanced economy while building an extensive Nordic-style welfare state, resulting in widespread prosperity and one of the highest per capita incomes in the world. However, Finnish GDP growth was negative in 2012–2014 (−0.698% to −1.426%), with a preceding nadir of −8% in 2009. Finland is a top performer in numerous metrics of national performance, including education, economic competitiveness, civil liberties, quality of life, and human development. For further information on the types of business entities in this country and their abbreviations, see "Business entities in Finland". Notable firms This list includes notable companies with primary headquarters located in the country. The industry and sector follow the Industry Classification Benchmark taxonomy. Organizations which have ceased operations are included and noted as defunct. See also List of largest companies in Finland List of banks in Finland External links Official register of companies and entities References Finland

But Finland's money went down from 2012 to 2014. It went down a lot in 2009.
Finland is good at many things. Like school. And business. Its people are free. Life is good there. People grow and live well.

Finland started making things in factories later than many other countries. Before the 1950s, most people in Finland worked on farms.

Then, Finland quickly grew a strong economy. It also built a good system to help all its people, like in other Nordic countries. This made many people rich and happy. People in Finland earn a lot of money, more than almost anywhere else in the world.

But between 2012 and 2014, Finland's economy got a little smaller. In 2009, it got much smaller, by 8%.

Finland is very good at many things. It has great schools, a strong economy, and people are very free. People there have a good life and grow up well.

If you want to know more about the kinds of businesses in Finland and their short names, look at 'Business entities in Finland'.

Here are some important companies that have their main offices in Finland. We also list companies that used to be open but are now closed. You can also look at 'List of largest companies in Finland' and 'List of banks in Finland'. There are also other links like 'Official register of companies and entities'.
simplified_secondary_school:
Finland is an independent country in Northern Europe. It started industrializing later than many other countries, remaining mainly an agricultural country until the 1950s. It quickly built an advanced economy and, at the same time, created a comprehensive welfare system, typical of Nordic countries. This led to widespread wealth and one of the highest average incomes per person globally. However, Finland's economy (measured by GDP growth) shrank between 2012 and 2014, with growth rates ranging from -0.698% to -1.426%. Before this, it had hit a low point, shrinking by 8% in 2009. Finland ranks highly in many areas of national performance, including education, economic competitiveness, civil liberties, quality of life, and human development.

To learn more about the different types of businesses in Finland and their abbreviations, refer to "Business entities in Finland".

**Notable Companies:**
This section lists important companies whose main offices are in Finland. Their industry and sector are categorized using the Industry Classification Benchmark system. Companies that are no longer operating are also included and marked as defunct (no longer existing).

In this paper, we used computers. We wanted to see how this slower energy growth works.

Computer tests looking at how these wobbles grow from tiny little shakes found that their power first grows super fast. Then, it grows steadily once the special magnetic fields around them get so big that they push and pull on each other in a very complicated way. In this report, we used computer models to find out how this steady power growth works. We found that the big, slow wobbles from these shaky parts stopped getting bigger. simplified_secondary_school:
Magnetic disturbances within plasma seem to be very important during the first steps of a quark-gluon plasma becoming stable, especially when it's at very high energy and its particles interact only a little. Computer simulations that looked at how these disturbances grow from tiny starting points have shown that their energy first increases very rapidly (exponentially). After that, the energy grows at a steady rate (linearly). This linear growth happens when the magnetic fields involved (called 'color magnetic fields') become so strong that their complex interactions can no longer be described by standard mathematical methods.

In this paper, we use simulations to figure out exactly how this linear energy growth happens. We found that the large-scale patterns (long-wavelength modes) linked to the disturbance stop growing. However, they don't just stop; they transfer their energy to much smaller scales, creating 'plasmon excitations' – which are like packets of energy – that become increasingly energetic. We found that this energy transfer (cascade) reaches an almost stable state where the energy is spread out following a specific mathematical pattern, which is described by a number called the spectral index.

original_text: Northeim is a district in Lower Saxony, Germany. It is bounded by (from the northwest and clockwise) the districts of Holzminden, Hildesheim, Goslar and Göttingen, and the state of Hesse (district of Kassel). History In medieval times the area had been part of the Duchy of Brunswick-Lüneburg. Later the majority of it belonged to Hanover and then Prussia. In 1885 the Prussian government created districts in the newly acquired provinces. In 1884 the districts of Einbeck, Northeim, and Uslar were established. Northeim and Uslar were merged in 1932, and they were again merged with Einbeck in 1974. The district's area was further enlarged in 1977, when some municipalities of neighbouring districts (Gandersheim and Osterode am Harz) joined the Northeim district. Geography The district is located in the Weserbergland mountains. The Weser forms the western border of the district. Another river, the Leine, runs through the district from south to north. It is joined by the River Rhume which flows through the town of Northeim and the River Ilme which flows through the town of Einbeck. Coat of arms Towns and municipalities See also Metropolitan region Hannover-Braunschweig-Göttingen-Wolfsburg References External links Official website (German) Districts of Lower Saxony

We have a new test. It is called QUIET. It will use special cameras. These cameras are small chips. They will look for light at two speeds. They will do this in the Atacama Desert.

Using the two light speeds and looking in the right spots. They hope to see past other light. They want to see the tiny sign very, very well.
simplified_secondary_school:
A main goal of future experiments measuring the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) radiation is to find a faint sign of 'inflation' in the way the CMB light is polarized. This requires extremely high sensitivity and careful management of potential errors. Because individual detectors have reached their maximum sensitivity, future experiments will use many detectors grouped together (large receiver arrays) to greatly improve overall sensitivity.

One such experiment is called the Q/U Imaging Experiment (QUIET). QUIET will use special detectors called HEMT-based receivers, packaged as chips, operating at frequencies of 90 and 40 GHz. These will be located in the Atacama Desert. Data collection is planned to start in early 2008, using prototype arrays with 91 and 19 receivers. Eventually, the experiment plans to expand to 1000 receivers. By using these two frequencies and carefully selecting the sky regions to observe, scientists hope to effectively remove interference from other sources (foregrounds) and achieve a sensitivity close to 0.001 for the ratio of tensor to scalar perturbations.

original_text: we present a non - linear numerical model for a geometrically thin accretion disk with the addition of stochastic non - linear fluctuations in the viscous parameter . these numerical realizations attempt to study the stochastic effects on the disk angular momentum transport . we show that this simple model is capable of reproducing several observed phenomenologies of accretion driven systems . the most notable of these is the observed linear rms - flux relationship in the disk luminosity . this feature is not formally captured by the linearized disk equations used in previous work . a fourier analysis of the dissipation and mass accretion rates across disk radii show coherence for frequencies below the local viscous frequency . this is consistent with the coherence behavior observed in astrophysical sources such as cygnus x-1 .

We talk about LDOS. It is about light. It is in empty space near a wall. It is when things are calm and warm. We tell you what LDOS means.

We show LDOS needs a special light part. But LDOS is not always another light part. People often think it is.

We show how LDOS changes. It changes when it is far from a wall. Our idea for LDOS is a bit new.

But our idea for LDOS is still good. It is good for some kinds of light. Like special light waves.

We also see if we can find LDOS. We use a tool called SNOM.

First, we show we can see heat light. It comes from over a thing. This helps us find light LDOS.

SNOM is like another tool. That tool is called STM. STM finds electron LDOS. SNOM finds light LDOS.

We also talk about new SNOM tests. They looked for light LDOS.
simplified_primary_school: In this paper, we explain clearly what "LDOS" means. LDOS is about how many tiny light energy spots are in empty space near a surface when things are still and at a certain warmth. We found that LDOS only depends on a special math tool for electric fields. But it's not always the same as how people usually measure it, even though many books say it is. We show this by looking at how LDOS changes when you move closer or farther from a surface. We also show how our idea is different from the usual idea. But we also show that our idea is still right when the light waves are at certain special speeds that match the material, like "surface polaritons." We also look at if we can find this LDOS using a special way called "apertureless SNOM." First, we show that we should be able to see the heat light coming off a sample very close up. And seeing this light could help us find the electromagnetic LDOS. We also show that apertureless SNOM is like the light version of another tool called a "scanning tunneling microscope." That other tool is good at finding the "electronic LDOS." We also talk about some new SNOM tests that try to find the electromagnetic LDOS.
simplified_secondary_school:
In this article, we present a clear and precise definition for the local density of electromagnetic states (LDOS). This LDOS describes the number of available electromagnetic states in a vacuum close to a surface, when the system is stable and at a specific temperature.

We demonstrate that the LDOS is determined solely by the system's electric field Green function. However, we also show that it's not simply the "trace of its imaginary part," which is a common but often incorrect assumption found in scientific papers. To show this, we examine how the LDOS changes as you move further away from a surface. Our findings highlight how our definition differs from the usual, less accurate definition. Despite these differences, we confirm that the standard definition *is* accurate when looking at frequencies that are very close to a material's natural vibrations, like surface polaritons.

We also investigate whether it's possible to detect this LDOS using a method called apertureless scanning near-field optical microscopy (SNOM). First, we show that the heat-related light emitted very close to a sample (its thermal near-field emission spectrum) can be detected. Measuring this emission could allow us to determine the electromagnetic LDOS. Furthermore, we explain that apertureless SNOM is like the optical version of a scanning tunneling microscope (STM), which is a tool known for detecting the electronic LDOS (the density of electron states). Finally, we discuss recent SNOM experiments that have been designed to detect the electromagnetic LDOS.

original_text: we consider a gas of ultracold two - level atoms confined in a cavity , taking into account for atomic center - of - mass motion and cavity mode variations . we use the generalized dicke model , and analyze separately the cases of a gaussian , and a standing wave mode shape . owing to the interplay between external motional energies of the atoms and internal atomic and field energies , the phase - diagrams exhibit novel features not encountered in the standard dicke model , such as the existence of first and second order phase transitions between normal and superradiant phases . due to the quantum description of atomic motion , internal and external atomic degrees of freedom are highly correlated leading to modified normal and superradiant phases .
simplified_kindergarten:
We look at very cold tiny things. They have two parts. They are in a box. We watch how they move. We watch how the light in the box changes.

We use a special way to think. We look at two kinds of light in the box. One is like a soft cloud. One is like waves that stand still.

The tiny things move. This makes energy. The tiny things have energy inside. The light in the box has energy. All these energies mix.

This mixing makes new things happen. We see new pictures. These pictures are different from old ones.

The tiny things can change how they act. They can go from normal. They can go to super bright. This change can happen in two ways.

The tiny things move in a special way. How they move and what is inside them are very connected. This makes their normal ways different. This makes their super bright ways different too.
simplified_primary_school:
We look at very cold atoms that have two energy levels. These atoms are kept inside a special box. We think about how these atoms move and how the light waves inside the box can change.

We use a special math idea called the "generalized Dicke model." We study two different shapes for the light waves: a "gaussian" shape and a "standing wave" shape.

Because the atoms' movement energy mixes with their inside energy and the light's energy, our maps show new things. These maps help us see different states. We don't see these new things in the usual "standard Dicke model." For example, things can change from a "normal" state to a "superradiant" state. These changes are called "phase transitions" and can be "first order" or "second order."

Because we use special "quantum" rules to describe how atoms move, the atoms' inside parts and their outside movement are very connected. This makes the "normal" and "superradiant" states different from what we usually expect.
simplified_secondary_school: We are studying a gas of very cold atoms, which can exist in two energy states, held inside a cavity. We consider both the overall movement of these atoms and the variations in the light field within the cavity. We use a modified version of the Dicke model, and we specifically analyze two different shapes for the light field: a Gaussian shape and a standing wave shape. Because of how the atoms' external movement energy interacts with their internal energy and the energy of the light field, the system's phase diagrams (which map out its different states) show new characteristics not found in the standard Dicke model. These include the presence of first and second order phase transitions, where the system changes between a "normal" state and a "superradiant" state (where atoms emit light together very strongly). Since we describe the atoms' motion using quantum mechanics, their internal states and external movement are highly connected, resulting in altered "normal" and "superradiant" phases.

We used math and computers to find a special distance called the Dyer-Roeder distance. This was for ideas about 'quintessence,' which is like a perfect liquid. We made a good guess that matched our computer results for all the different numbers for how much stuff there is and how quintessence acts.

We then created an accurate formula that matches our simulation results for all possible values of the universe's density and the way quintessence behaves.

We found that including quintessence significantly changes the probable range of values for @xmath0, usually leading to a lower best estimate compared to models that only consider a pure cosmological constant.

Tellus is a Latin word. It means "Earth". This word can mean different things:

Tellus Mater or Terra Mater: This was an old Roman goddess. She was like a mother to the Earth.
Tellus of Athens: He was a man from old Athens. People thought he was the happiest man.

Tellus A: This is a science magazine. It writes about weather and the ocean.
Tellus B: This is another science magazine. It writes about how chemicals and physics affect the weather.

Tellus is a Latin word meaning "Earth." It can refer to:

*   Tellus Mater or Terra Mater, the ancient Roman goddess of the Earth.
*   Tellus of Athens, a man from ancient Athens believed to be the happiest person.
*   An alternative name for the planet Earth, often used in science fiction, such as in books by E. E. Smith.
*   *Tellus A*, a scientific journal focusing on Dynamic Meteorology and Oceanography.
*   *Tellus B*, a scientific journal covering Chemical and Physical Meteorology.
*   Tellus Audio Cassette Magazine, a former nonprofit project that featured audio art, now available online at UbuWeb.
*   Tellus (comics), a character in comic books who is part of the Legion of Super-Heroes.
*   Tellus Institute, a nonprofit organization that conducts research and develops policies to support environmental sustainability and social fairness.
*   Tellus, a fictional human colony featured in the TV series *Space: Above and Beyond*.
*   Tellus Science Museum, located in Cartersville, Georgia.
*   IK Tellus, a sports club from Tellusborg in Stockholm, Sweden.
*   *Bematistes tellus*, a type of butterfly belonging to the Nymphalidae family.

See also Terra (disambiguation).

original_text: we examine the leading order corrections to the nambu effective action for the motion of a cosmic string , which appear at fourth order in the ratio of the width to radius of curvature of the string . we determine the numerical coefficients of these extrinsic curvature corrections , and derive the equations of motion of the worldsheet . using these equations , we calculate the corrections to the motion of a collapsing loop , a travelling wave , and a helical breather . from the numerical coefficients we have calculated , we discuss whether the string motion can be labelled as ` rigid ' or ` antirigid , ' and hence whether cusp or kink formation might be suppressed or enhanced .
simplified_kindergarten:
We look at how a cosmic string moves. We look at tiny changes to its movement. These changes are part of a rule called the Nambu effective action. They happen because of how wide the string is and how much it bends. They are a very small kind of change.

We find numbers for these changes. These changes make the string bend. We write down rules for how the string's skin moves.

We use these rules. We figure out tiny changes for a loop that gets smaller. We also look at a wave that moves. And a special twisty wave.

From our numbers, we talk about if the string moves stiffly or not stiffly. We also see if sharp points or bends will happen more or less.
simplified_primary_school: We look at small changes in how a cosmic string moves. These changes happen because of how wide the string is compared to how much it bends. We find the numbers for these changes in how the string bends. We also figure out the rules for how the string's surface moves. Using these rules, we figure out the small changes to how these things move: a loop that is getting smaller, a wave that is moving, and a string that twists and untwists like a spring. From the numbers we found, we talk about if the string moves in a 'stiff' way or a 'floppy' way. This helps us see if sharp points or bends in the string will happen more or less often.
simplified_secondary_school: We study the main adjustments to the Nambu effective action, a mathematical model that describes how a cosmic string moves. These adjustments become important when the string's width is very small compared to its radius of curvature, specifically at a "fourth order" level. We calculate the specific numerical values for these corrections, which are related to how the string bends in space. We also develop the equations that describe the movement of the string's "worldsheet" (its path through spacetime). Using these equations, we then calculate how these corrections affect the movement of specific string configurations: a collapsing loop, a travelling wave, and a helical breather. Based on the numerical values we calculated, we discuss whether the string's motion can be described as "rigid" (stiff) or "antirigid" (flexible). This helps us understand if the formation of sharp points (cusps) or sudden bends (kinks) on the string might be reduced or increased.

original_text: Proteles is a genus of distinctive hyenas which contain the aardwolf (Proteles cristatus) and its close fossil relatives. It is the only genus of the subfamily Protelinae. While the oldest fossils definitely belonging to Proteles date back to the Pliocene, material from the Miocene dating to around 10 million years ago has been suggested to belong to the genus, which would significantly increase its temporal range. It has been suggested that the Protelinae subfamily is actually an offshoot of the "running hyenas" (such as Lycyaena) who adapted to an insectivorous diet due to increased competition from canines and felines. References Mammal genera Mammal genera with one living species Hyenas Taxa named by Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire

It has the aardwolf.
It also has old family members.
It is the only group in the Protelinae family.

The oldest Proteles bones are from a very long time ago.
Some other old bones might be Proteles too.
These bones are from 10 million years ago.
This means Proteles lived for a very, very long time.

People think the Protelinae family came from "running hyenas."
Like Lycyaena.
These hyenas learned to eat bugs.
This was because other animals, like dogs and cats, ate their food.
simplified_primary_school:
Proteles is a group of special hyenas. The aardwolf is in this group. Old animals that were like the aardwolf are also in this group. It is the only group in the Protelinae family.

The oldest bones we are sure are from Proteles are from a time called the Pliocene. But some people think other old bones, from about 10 million years ago, might also be from Proteles. If this is true, it means Proteles has been around for a much longer time.

Some people think the Protelinae family came from "running hyenas" (like Lycyaena). These hyenas learned to eat insects. They did this because other animals, like dogs and cats, were eating all their normal food.
simplified_secondary_school:
Proteles is a genus of unique hyenas that includes the aardwolf (Proteles cristatus) and its close relatives found as fossils. It is the only genus within the Protelinae subfamily.

The oldest fossils clearly identified as Proteles date back to the Pliocene epoch. However, some fossil material from the Miocene epoch, dating to about 10 million years ago, has been suggested to also belong to this genus. If confirmed, this would significantly increase the known time Proteles has existed.

Scientists have suggested that the Protelinae subfamily actually branched off from "running hyenas" (such as Lycyaena). These hyenas then adapted to an insect-eating diet, possibly due to increased competition for food from other predators like canines (dogs) and felines (cats).

original_text: the magnetic field structure associated with edge localised ideal ballooning mode ( elm ) bursts is analysed by nonlinear gyrofluid computation . the linear growth phase is characterised by the formation of small scale magnetic islands . ergodic magnetic field regions develop near the end of the linear phase when the instability starts to perturb the equilibrium profiles . the nonlinear blow - out gives rise to an ergodisation of the entire edge region . the time - dependent level of ergodicity is determined in terms of the mean radial displacement of a magnetic field line . the ergodicity decreases again during the nonlinear turbulent phase of the blow - out in dependence on the degrading plasma beta in the collapsing plasma pedestal profile .
simplified_kindergarten:
We look at magnets. This is when "elm bursts" happen. A computer helps us.

Little magnet islands form first.
Messy magnet areas grow later. This is when things get wobbly.
A big "blow-out" makes the whole edge messy.
How messy it is changes over time. We look at how far a magnet line moves.
The messiness goes down again. This happens when the plasma gets weaker.
simplified_primary_school: We study how the magnet lines look when ELM bursts happen at the edge. We use a special computer program to do this. In the first part, tiny loops of magnet lines are made. Near the end of this first part, the magnet lines get messy in some places. This happens when things get wobbly and start to change how everything usually looks. A big burst then makes all the magnet lines at the edge become messy. We find out how messy the magnet lines are by seeing how far they move from their normal path. This messiness changes over time. But then the magnet lines get less messy again. This happens during the wild part of the big burst, because the plasma gets weaker and its shape at the edge changes.
simplified_secondary_school:
The structure of the magnetic field during ELM bursts is analyzed using advanced computer simulations called nonlinear gyrofluid computation. ELM bursts are sudden releases of energy caused by an instability known as the edge localised ideal ballooning mode.

During the initial growth phase of an ELM burst, tiny magnetic islands form. Towards the end of this initial phase, the magnetic field becomes chaotic in certain regions as the instability starts to disrupt the plasma's stable conditions. This powerful, uncontrolled burst then causes the entire edge region of the plasma to become chaotic.

The level of this chaos, which changes over time, is measured by how much a magnetic field line, on average, moves away from its usual path. However, this chaos then lessens during the later, turbulent stage of the burst. This happens because the plasma's pressure (called plasma beta) decreases as the plasma's edge layer collapses.

original_text: we show that in theories with a nontrivial kinetic term the contribution of the gravitational waves to the cmb fluctuations can be substantially larger than that is naively expected in simple inflationary models . this increase of the tensor - to - scalar perturbation ratio leads to a larger b - component of the cmb polarization , thus making the prospects for future detection much more promising . the other important consequence of the considered model is a higher energy scale of inflation and hence higher reheating temperature compared to a simple inflation . lmu - asc 78/05

When space waves wiggle more than other wiggles, it makes a bigger 'B' part in the old light. So, we can find it easier later.

Another big thing is that the universe grew with more power. It got hotter after it grew. This is more than in simple ideas.
simplified_primary_school:
We found that ripples in space, called gravity waves, can make the tiny wobbles in the CMB light much bigger. This happens in some special ideas, much more than people usually think in simple ideas about how the universe grew very fast.

When gravity waves are much stronger than other wobbles, it makes a bigger 'B-part' in the CMB light's pattern. This means we have a much better chance to find them in the future.

Another big thing our idea shows is that the universe grew very fast with more energy. This means it was hotter after it grew, compared to simple ideas about how the universe grew fast.
simplified_secondary_school: Our research shows that in theories where the energy field driving inflation behaves in a more complex way, the effect of gravitational waves on the tiny variations in the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) can be much greater than what is usually expected in simpler models of inflation. This increased ratio of gravitational wave disturbances to other types of disturbances results in a stronger 'B-mode' pattern in the polarization of the CMB, which makes it much more likely that future experiments will be able to detect these gravitational waves. Another important result of this model is that the rapid expansion of the early universe (inflation) happened at a much higher energy level, and therefore, the universe reached a much higher temperature during the 'reheating' phase that followed inflation, compared to what simple inflation models suggest.

original_text: (CNN) -- Five people, including a child, died when their helicopter crashed in Wyoming County, Pennsylvania, that county's coroner said. Three men and one woman also died, said coroner Tom Kukuchka. Authorities were still attempting to contact next of kin. Holly Baker, with the Federal Aviation Administration, said the helicopter was reported missing with five people on board Saturday. It departed from Greater Binghamton Airport in New York and was headed to Jake Arner Memorial Airport in Lehighton, Pennsylvania. The wreckage of the Robinson 66 helicopter was found Sunday afternoon in a wooded area, Baker said. The cause of the crash was not immediately clear. CNN affiliate WNEP reported that controllers at Wilkes-Barre/ Scranton International Airport lost contact with the helicopter around 10:30 p.m. ET Saturday night during bad weather. The National Transportation Safety Board was en route and will investigate the crash, Baker said. The FAA will also investigate. CNN's Sho Wills and Dave Alsup contributed to this report.

original_text: Östhammar Municipality (Östhammars kommun) is a municipality in Uppsala County in east central Sweden. Its seat is located in the city of Östhammar. The present municipality was created during the local government reform in the late sixties and early seventies. Already in 1967 the City of Öregrund joined the City of Östhammar in a common municipality. It was the first time a former city joined another local government unit. By 1971 the new combined city became a municipality of unitary type and in 1974 more units were added. In June 2018 the municipal council voted in favour of the building of Forsmark nuclear waste repository, with the final decision needing to be made by the Swedish government. Localities All towns with over 200 inhabitants in 2000, from Statistics Sweden. Östhammar (seat) Gimo Österbybruk Hargshamn Dannemora Öregrund Alunda Skoby (part of this bimunicipal locality is in Uppsala Municipality) Norrskedika Another notable village is Forsmark (pop. 59), where the Forsmark Nuclear Power Plant is situated. Climate Österbybruk has a humid continental climate that is quite mild throughout the year, especially considering its northerly latitude. References External links Östhammar Municipality - Official site Municipalities of Uppsala County

simplified_secondary_school:
Östhammar Municipality is located in Uppsala County in east-central Sweden. The city of Östhammar serves as its administrative center, or "seat."

The current municipality was formed through a series of local government reforms in the late 1960s and early 1970s. The first step happened in 1967 when the City of Öregrund merged with the City of Östhammar to form a single municipality. This was a notable event, as it was the first time a former city joined another local government area in Sweden. By 1971, this combined area officially became a unified municipality, and more local units were added in 1974.

In June 2018, the municipal council voted to approve the construction of the Forsmark nuclear waste repository. However, the final decision on this project must be made by the Swedish government.

According to Statistics Sweden data from 2000, the municipality includes several towns with over 200 inhabitants. These are: Östhammar (the administrative seat), Gimo, Österbybruk, Hargshamn, Dannemora, Öregrund, Alunda, and Norrskedika. Skoby is also listed, though part of this locality extends into Uppsala Municipality. Another significant village is Forsmark, which had a population of 59 and is home to the Forsmark Nuclear Power Plant.

The climate in Österbybruk is classified as humid continental. It is notably mild throughout the year, especially when considering its northern geographical location.

original_text: (CNN) -- A Singapore Airlines flight to Hong Kong had to turn back in mid-flight Friday after the pilots received an alert about a problem with a cargo door on the plane, the company said. The aircraft, an Airbus A380 superjumbo, returned to Singapore's Changi airport on Friday afternoon "as a result of a cockpit warning relating to a cargo door," the airline said in an e-mailed statement. Flight SQ 866 landed safely back at Changi at 2:20 p.m. local time, Singapore Airlines said, adding that passengers would be transferred to a replacement flight to Hong Kong. The airline didn't provide further details about the incident, nor did it immediately respond to a question from CNN about whether the cargo door issue affected cabin pressure. The replacement aircraft was expected to arrive in Hong Kong at 8:40 p.m., more than three and a half hours after the original scheduled arrival time, according to the Singapore Airlines website. The double-decker A380 is capable of carrying more passengers than any other commercial aircraft. CNN's Euan McKirdy contributed to this report.

original_text: Viscaceae is a taxonomic family name of flowering plants. In this circumscription, the family includes the several genera of mistletoes. This family name is currently being studied and under review as in past decades, several systems of plant taxonomy recognized this family, notably the 1981 Cronquist system. However, the APG II system of 2003 does not recognize the family, treating it as a synonym of Santalaceae. This did not end the taxonomic debate among botanists and there are many that still think Viscaceae should be an accepted family name. According to the APG IV system Der and Nickrent (2008) found seven well supported clades in Santalaceae, but relationships between these clades are still poorly understood. The traditional and cultural uses of species within the Viscaceae merit further attention. Clades or genera treated as belonging to the currently debated family name Viscaceae include: Arceuthobium Dendrophthora Ginalloa Korthalsella Notothixos Phoradendron Viscum References AP-Website: Santalales Historically recognized angiosperm families

The experiment used a photon beam that was 'linearly polarised' (meaning its light waves vibrated in a single direction) and had energies between 1.1 and 2.3 GeV.

On April 11, 2002, the ship stopped being used by the U.S. Navy. It was given to Turkey. In Turkey, its name was changed to TCG Gökova (F 496). It joined other ships that Turkey got from the U.S. Navy. These ships were all from the Oliver Hazard Perry-class.

The USS Samuel Eliot Morison (FFG-13) was the seventh ship of its kind to serve in the United States Navy. It was named after Rear Admiral Samuel Eliot Morison (1887–1976), a highly respected American naval historian who wrote over 40 books on naval history. This was the first U.S. Navy ship to bear his name.

On April 11, 2002, the USS Samuel Eliot Morison was decommissioned (taken out of service) and transferred to Turkey. There, she was renamed TCG Gökova (F 496) and joined other Oliver Hazard Perry-class ships that the Turkish Navy had acquired. In 2013, she contributed to Operation Ocean Shield alongside other NATO Forces ships in the Gulf of Aden. As of 2019, she is still in active service.

original_text: AD 22 (XXII) was a common year starting on Thursday (link will display the full calendar) of the Julian calendar. At the time, it was known as the Year of the Consulship of Agrippa and Galba (or, less frequently, year 775 Ab urbe condita). The denomination AD 22 for this year has been used since the early medieval period, when the Anno Domini calendar era became the prevalent method in Europe for naming years. Events By place Roman Empire Drusus Julius Caesar receives the tribunicia potestas (tribunician power). Births Valeria Messalina, third wife of Emperor Claudius (d. 48 AD) Deaths Daeso, emperor of Dongbuyeo (b. 60 BC) Gaius Ateius Capito, Roman jurist and suffect consul (b. c. 30 BC) Junia Tertia, wife of Gaius Cassius Longinus (b. c. 75 BC) References 0022 als:20er#22

Back then, people called it the year when Agrippa and Galba were the main leaders. Sometimes, they also called it year 775 Ab urbe condita. We have called this year AD 22 since a long, long time ago, in the early Middle Ages. That was when the Anno Domini way of naming years became the most common way in Europe.

In the Roman Empire, Drusus Julius Caesar got a special power. It was called tribunician power (tribunicia potestas).

Someone important was born this year: Valeria Messalina. She later became the third wife of Emperor Claudius. She died in AD 48.

Some important people died this year:
Daeso died. He was the emperor of Dongbuyeo. He was born in 60 BC.
Gaius Ateius Capito died. He was a Roman who knew a lot about laws and was also an important leader (a suffect consul). He was born around 30 BC.
Junia Tertia died. She was the wife of Gaius Cassius Longinus. She was born around 75 BC.
simplified_secondary_school:
The year 22 AD (XXII) was a common year in the Julian calendar, beginning on a Thursday. During that time, people often referred to it as the Year of the Consulship of Agrippa and Galba, or less frequently, year 775 "Ab urbe condita" (from the founding of the city of Rome). The name "AD 22" for this year only became widely used in the early medieval period, when the "Anno Domini" (Year of the Lord) calendar system became the main way to name years in Europe.

In the Roman Empire, Drusus Julius Caesar received the "tribunicia potestas," which means tribunician power.

Among the births this year was Valeria Messalina, who would later become the third wife of Emperor Claudius (she died in 48 AD).

Notable deaths in 22 AD included Daeso, the emperor of Dongbuyeo (born in 60 BC); Gaius Ateius Capito, a Roman legal expert and suffect consul (born around 30 BC); and Junia Tertia, the wife of Gaius Cassius Longinus (born around 75 BC).

original_text: The eyes of world were focussed on the Lindt café in Sydney as a mad lone gunman terrorised 18 innocent hostages. For 17 hours, little was known about the figure that TV cameras caught glimpses of in the window, or the nightmare he was subjecting his victims to. The man grew more agitated as he realised the demands he was forcing his hostages to post online were not being met, or even broadcast. But after his siege ended in gunfire killing two of his captives, details of self-proclaimed sheik Man Haron Monis - and a timeline of events during the terrifying hours he held his hostages captive - started coming together. Here are the events, as they played out. Scroll down for video .

The man got angrier. He made the people inside write what he wanted online. But no one was doing what he asked, or even showing it on TV.

But then, the scary time ended with gunshots. Two of the people he held died. People started to learn about the man. His name was Man Haron Monis, and he called himself a sheik. They also learned what happened during all the scary hours he kept the people.

Here is what happened.
simplified_secondary_school: The world's attention was on the Lindt café in Sydney, where a lone gunman terrorized 18 innocent hostages. For 17 hours, little was known about the gunman, glimpsed by TV cameras in the window, or the terrible ordeal his victims were enduring. The gunman became more agitated as he realised the demands he forced his hostages to post online were not being met or even broadcast. The siege ended in gunfire, which killed two of his captives. Afterward, details emerged about the self-proclaimed sheik, Man Haron Monis, and a timeline of the terrifying hours he held his hostages captive. The following is a timeline of those events.

Imagine a tiny dust speck moving randomly. We call this a "Brownian motion". It can move both forwards and backwards in time.

We study something called an "unbiased shift". This is a special random time. This time is chosen based on how the dust speck moves. When we use this special time, the dust speck still moves like a Brownian motion. And this new movement does not depend on the shift itself.

We explain what these "unbiased shifts" are by looking at how they balance things that build up as the dust speck moves.

We can create such a special random time so that the shift amount can be any way we choose.

We found that if we use a special "local time" clock to move through time, the dust speck always looks like it's moving in a Brownian motion.

A very important part of our work is a new discovery about how to balance certain random measurements.

We also look at how these "unbiased shifts" behave on average, and what the simplest ones are.
simplified_secondary_school:
Let's consider a two-sided standard Brownian motion. An 'unbiased shift' of this motion is a random time, which is a time chosen randomly based on the motion itself. The key property is that the Brownian motion, when shifted by this random time, still behaves like a Brownian motion, and its future path is independent of the specific random time chosen.

We describe these unbiased shifts by using 'allocation rules' that balance how certain cumulative properties of the Brownian motion are distributed. For any given probability distribution, we can create a specific type of random time (called a 'stopping time') that acts as an unbiased shift. This stopping time will have exactly that given probability distribution.

Specifically, we find that if we use 'local time' (which measures how long the Brownian motion stays at a particular point) as our random shift, the shifted motion will always appear as a two-sided Brownian motion. A key part of our method relies on a new mathematical theorem. This theorem proves that certain 'allocation rules' exist which can balance several random quantities that are spread out and whose combined behavior doesn't change over time. We also study the statistical properties (like average values and variability) and the 'minimal' or most efficient aspects of these unbiased shifts.

original_text: In DJing, phrasing, also called stage matching, refers to alignment of phrases of two tracks in a mix. This allows the transition between the tracks to be done without breaking the musical structure. Phrasing is an aspect of beatmixing, not a separate technique. Because most electronic dance music tracks have 4/4 time signature and a simple structure of 16-bar phrases, to align the phrases of two tracks it is often enough to start the track to be mixed in at a phrase boundary in the track currently playing. Careful phrasing can produce a seamless mix by making the breaks in two tracks coincide, or aligning the break in one track with the start of the beat in the other. See also Beatmatching Beatmixing Segue References Stage Matching from the Advanced Vinyl Handling DJing tutorial DJing

This helps the songs change smoothly.
The music still sounds good.
It does not sound broken.

Phrasing is part of beatmixing.
It is not a different trick.

Most dance songs have a simple beat.
They have parts that are 16 bars long.
To line up two songs, you can start the new song.
Start it when the first song finishes a part.

Good phrasing makes songs mix perfectly.
You can make quiet parts of songs happen together.
Or you can line up a quiet part with a new beat.
simplified_primary_school:
When a DJ plays music, "phrasing" (or "stage matching") means making parts of two songs line up in a mix. This helps the DJ change from one song to the next without making the music sound wrong.

Phrasing is part of something called "beatmixing." It is not a different trick on its own. Most dance music songs have simple parts, often 16 beats long. So, to line up two songs, a DJ often just starts the new song when a part of the first song ends.

When a DJ does phrasing well, it makes the music mix smoothly. This can mean making the quiet parts of two songs happen at the same time. Or, it can mean lining up a quiet part in one song with the start of the beat in the other.
simplified_secondary_school:
In DJing, phrasing, also known as stage matching, means lining up the musical phrases of two different songs in a mix. This ensures that when you switch between songs, the music flows smoothly without interrupting its structure. Phrasing is a part of beatmixing, not a separate technique on its own.

Most electronic dance music (EDM) tracks have a 4/4 time signature and a simple structure, often built from 16-bar phrases. Because of this, to line up the phrases of two songs, DJs often just need to start the new song at the beginning of a phrase in the song that's currently playing. When done carefully, phrasing can create a seamless mix. This can involve making the "breaks" (sections where the beat drops out) in both songs happen at the same time, or aligning a break in one song with the start of the beat in the other.

We say how things are spread out is important. This is for the open things that are not calm but stay the same. It is like how things are spread out when they are calm. This helps us figure out how fast things happen.

simplified_primary_school: Kramers theory helps us understand how things start to happen. We are making this theory work for systems that are not balanced, are open, and are like a line. We look at wobbles from inside (from heat) and wobbles from outside. These wobbles stay the same over time, are a special kind, and their connections slowly fade away. We show how important it is to know how things are spread out in this open system when it's not balanced but stays the same. This is like how things are spread out when balanced, and it helps us figure out how fast things happen. The new way we found to figure out how fast things happen can also be used for simpler situations. This includes systems that are closed (nothing goes in or out) or open, and for things that start with heat or without heat, and stay the same overall.
simplified_secondary_school: We are extending Kramers theory, which explains how processes overcome energy barriers, to include systems that are not in balance, are open to their surroundings, and exist in one dimension. We consider two types of random disturbances: internal noise from the system's heat environment and external noise from outside. Both types of noise are steady, follow a bell-curve pattern, and their past effects gradually fade over time in various ways. We emphasize the importance of how the system's states are distributed when it's not in balance but stable. This distribution is similar to the well-known Boltzmann distribution, which is used to calculate how fast processes happen in balanced systems. The new formula we created for the rate of these processes can be simplified to match existing formulas for specific situations, including both closed and open systems, and processes driven by heat or other factors, all in a stable state where an energy barrier must be overcome.

original_text: Ant Broads & Marshes NNR is a national nature reserve in Norfolk, England established by English Nature. It is named after the River Ant. It is part of the "Ant Broads and Marshes" Site of Special Scientific Interest and is within The Broads National Park. The NNR includes: Barton Broad and Catfield Fen, owned and managed by Norfolk Wildlife Trust Catfield Fen Reserve, owned and managed by the British Butterfly Conservation Society. The fen is the best example of unpolluted valley fen in Western Europe. original_text: Lagos, Nigeria (CNN) -- The Nigerian army has arrested a militant gang leader believed to be behind the kidnapping of 19 people, authorities said Saturday. Fifty-one of his followers were also arrested, said Lt Col Timothy Antigha, a spokesman for the military taskforce in the Niger Delta. Nigerian authorities on Wednesday freed the 19 hostages seized in the oil-rich Niger Delta region. They were 12 Nigerians, two Americans, two Frenchmen, one Canadian and two Indonesians. Nigeria has launched an offensive targeting Delta, Bayelsa and Rivers states, the largest military campaign in the Niger Delta region since the government offered an amnesty program in 2007. The Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta is one of several militant groups in the region that has been battling the government for years over the distribution of the country's oil wealth. CNN's Christian Purefoy contributed to this report.

There is a group. Its name is The Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta. It is one of many bad groups in that place. They have fought the government for many years. They want more of the country's oil money.

CNN's Christian Purefoy contributed to this report.

original_text: we consider the problem of detecting communities or modules in networks , groups of vertices with a higher - than - average density of edges connecting them . previous work indicates that a robust approach to this problem is the maximization of the benefit function known as `` modularity '' over possible divisions of a network . here we show that this maximization process can be written in terms of the eigenspectrum of a matrix we call the modularity matrix , which plays a role in community detection similar to that played by the graph laplacian in graph partitioning calculations . this result leads us to a number of possible algorithms for detecting community structure , as well as several other results , including a spectral measure of bipartite structure in networks and a new centrality measure that identifies those vertices that occupy central positions within the communities to which they belong . the algorithms and measures proposed are illustrated with applications to a variety of real - world complex networks .
