We look for groups in big webs. A group has many dots. Many lines connect these dots. More lines than usual.

Other people found a good way. They make "modularity" very big. This happens when they split the web.

We show how to make "modularity" big. We use a special math table. It is called the "modularity matrix". This table helps find groups. It is like another table that helps split webs.

This helps us find new ways to find groups. It also helps us find other new things. We can find special webs with two parts. We can find the most important dots in the groups.

We show these new ways. We use them on many real webs.
simplified_primary_school:
We want to find special groups in networks. These groups are called communities or modules. They are made of dots that have more connections to each other than to other dots in the network.

Other people have found a good way to do this. It means making a score called "modularity" as big as possible. This score tells us how good the groups are when we split the network in different ways.

We found that making modularity as big as possible can be understood using special numbers from a math table we call the "modularity matrix". This modularity matrix helps find communities, much like another math tool helps split networks into parts.

Because of this, we now have new ways to find these community groups. We also found other things. For example, a way to measure a special pattern in networks. And a new way to find the most important dots inside each community. These are the dots that are in the middle of their own group.

We show how these new ways and measures work by using them on many real networks from the world.
simplified_secondary_school:
We are looking at how to find "communities" or "modules" within networks. These are groups of points (called vertices) that have more connections (edges) among themselves than you would typically find in the rest of the network. Earlier research suggests a reliable way to do this is by maximizing a special calculation called "modularity," which means finding the network division that gives the highest modularity score.

We show that this process of maximizing modularity can be described using the "eigenspectrum" – a set of special numbers – of a matrix we've named the "modularity matrix." This modularity matrix plays a role in finding communities similar to how the "graph Laplacian" matrix is used in dividing graphs into parts.

This discovery allows us to develop several new methods (algorithms) for finding community structures. It also leads to other findings, such as a "spectral measure" for identifying "bipartite structure" in networks (networks that can be divided into two distinct sets of nodes). Additionally, it provides a new way to measure "centrality," which helps identify the most important nodes *within* their specific communities. We demonstrate these proposed algorithms and measures by applying them to various complex networks found in the real world.

original_text: Australian fast bowler Mitchell Johnson is doubtful for the fourth and final test against India next week after a hamstring problem forced him out of training on Saturday. The 33-year-old reigning ICC Cricketer of the Year's decision to sit out Saturday's session is thought to be precautionary, with an eye on the World Cup starting in Australia next month. He had warned on Friday he was feeling the strain after taking 13 wickets to help Australia to an unassailable 2-0 series lead ahead of the final test which starts in Sydney on Tuesday. Australian fast bowler Mitchell Johnson is doubtful for the fourth Test with India due to a hamstring problem . 'Definitely, all us bowlers feel like we need to have a bit of a freshen up,' Johnson said, noting the short turnaround before the one-day triangular series with England and India later this month. 'Hopefully I can get a bit of a break at some stage.' Johnson celebrates claiming the wicket of Cheteshwar Pujara during the Boxing Day Test with India .

A general modular-invariant Scherk-Schwarz deformation is found. This comes from the properties of the @xmath0 theories at their endpoints. It significantly extends examples known before.

*   Two supersymmetric @xmath0 theories are linked by a @xmath2-duality transformation. They have an intermediate @xmath1 maximum or minimum at the string scale.

This research explores how supersymmetric heterotic theories can transform into non-supersymmetric ones. This transformation happens through a process called Scherk-Schwarz compactification, which reduces supersymmetric @xmath0 theories down to @xmath1 theories. We developed a general way to describe this Scherk-Schwarz deformation, ensuring it remains 'modular-invariant'. This new description was derived from understanding the @xmath0 theories at the beginning and end of the transformation, and it greatly expands on previous examples of such deformations. This broader group of non-supersymmetric @xmath1 theories looks promising for creating realistic models that resemble the Standard Model of particle physics.

We also calculated the complete 'one-loop cosmological constant' for these theories, looking at how it changes with the 'compactification radius' in several situations. From this, we identified different ways these theories can transform:
*   First, two supersymmetric @xmath0 theories that are connected by a @xmath2-duality transformation. In this case, the intermediate @xmath1 theory shows either a maximum or minimum value at the fundamental 'string scale'.
*   Second, a non-supersymmetric @xmath0 theory that transforms into a supersymmetric @xmath0 theory. Here, the @xmath1 theory might have an 'AdS minimum' (a specific type of stable state).
*   Third, a 'metastable' non-supersymmetric @xmath0 theory that transforms through a @xmath1 theory into a supersymmetric @xmath0 theory.

The word Torrington can mean many things.

Some people are called Torrington. Arthur Torrington helped start two groups: the Windrush Foundation and the Equiano Society. He was born in Guyana. Jeff Torrington was a writer from Scotland. John Torrington was an explorer from England. He also worked on a Royal Navy ship. George Byng was a British naval officer and a statesman. He was the first Viscount Torrington.

Torrington is also the name of many places. In Australia, there are places called Torrington in New South Wales and Queensland. In Canada, there is Torrington, Alberta. In the United Kingdom, you can find Black Torrington, Great Torrington, and Little Torrington in Devon. There are also East Torrington and West Torrington in Lincolnshire. There is also Torrington Lane in Westford-Nabnasset, Massachusetts.

Other things are also called Torrington. The Battle of Torrington was a fight in 1646 during the English Civil War. Earl of Torrington and Viscount Torrington are special titles in Britain. Four ships in the Royal Navy were named HMS Torrington. A sailing ship called Torrington was built in Nova Scotia in 1847. It sank in New Zealand in 1851. Torrington F.C. is a football club in Great Torrington, Devon. There is a Torrington railway station in Devon. The Torrington Company makes special bearings.

You can also look up Thorrington.

    *   Sack, a village in the Swiss canton of Zurich, Switzerland.

*   **Related terms and concepts:**
    *   Sack of Rome, referring to various historical events.

original_text: Vietglish, Vietnaminglish, Vietlish or Vietnamese English, is an informal term for a mixture of elements from Vietnamese and English in conversational contexts or in writing and speaking. The term Vietlish is first recorded in 1967. Other colloquial portmanteau words for Vietlish include (chronologically): Vietglish (1992), Vinish (2003), Vinglish (2010) and Vietnamiglish (2016). This usage is said to be found in immigrant communities in majority-English-speaking countries or from people who are bilingual in Vietnamese and English or grew up in such multilingual environments. English words are also commonly used in everyday Vietnamese both inside and outside Vietnam in informal contexts. External links Viet Voice Magazine - Very Vietglish What are you speaking? Hey, it's just Vietglish References Vietnamese language Macaronic language

We can also call it "Vietlish."
Or "Vietnaminglish."
Or "Vietnamese English."
This is a casual name.
People first wrote "Vietlish" in 1967.
Other fun names for it are "Vietglish," "Vinish," "Vinglish," and "Vietnamiglish."
People use this mix when they live in a country where many speak English.
Or when they speak both Vietnamese and English.
Or when they grew up with many languages.
English words are also used in everyday Vietnamese.
This happens in Vietnam and other places.

People first wrote down the word 'Vietlish' in the year 1967. Other words for this mix were made later. They are Vietglish (in 1992), Vinish (in 2003), Vinglish (in 2010), and Vietnamiglish (in 2016). People say this mix of words is used by groups of people who moved to countries where most people speak English. It is also used by people who speak both Vietnamese and English, or who grew up hearing many languages. Also, people often use English words in normal Vietnamese talk. This happens both in Vietnam and in other countries, when people are speaking casually.
simplified_secondary_school:
Vietlish, also known as Vietglish, Vietnaminglish, or Vietnamese English, is an informal term for when people mix elements from Vietnamese and English in conversations, writing, or speaking. The term "Vietlish" was first recorded in 1967. Other informal words that blend Vietnamese and English, known as portmanteaus, include (in order of their first recording): Vietglish (1992), Vinish (2003), Vinglish (2010), and Vietnamiglish (2016).

This blend of languages is commonly used by Vietnamese immigrant communities in countries where English is the main language. It's also used by people who are bilingual in Vietnamese and English, or those who grew up in environments where multiple languages are spoken. Separately, English words are also frequently used in everyday Vietnamese conversations, both inside and outside Vietnam, in informal situations.

original_text: Dilbert and the Way of the Weasel is a satirical Dilbert book written by Scott Adams. It was originally published in 2002. Premise The chief premise of the book is that everyday actions of humanity are neither consistently honest nor criminal, but rather weasel. He dissects the aspects of weasel behavior and explores its benefits and ramifications, sometimes referring to his own life experiences. The book is divided up into many sections and chapters which show different types of weasels, how to be a weasel, weasel words, and methods for countering the weasels you may encounter in everyday life. Reception Sales of the book were hampered by the backfiring of Adams' announcement that he would dedicate a strip to Dilbert having sex if the book became the number one seller on Amazon.com, a marketing promotion that Adams himself characterized as "what will someday be hailed as the worst idea of the century." References Dilbert books 2002 books

The book has many parts. It shows different kinds of weasels. It shows how to be a weasel. It shows weasel words. It shows how to stop weasels you see every day.

The book did not sell well. This was because of something Scott Adams said. He said he would draw Dilbert having sex. This would happen if the book sold the most on Amazon.com. This was a way to sell the book. Scott Adams said it was "the worst idea ever."

Scott Adams looks closely at how people act tricky. He talks about the good things and the bad things that happen when people are tricky. Sometimes, he shares stories from his own life.

The book has many parts. It shows different kinds of tricky people, how to be tricky, tricky words, and ways to stop tricky people you might meet.

The book did not sell very well. This was because Scott Adams said he would draw a Dilbert comic about Dilbert doing something for grown-ups if the book sold the most on Amazon.com. This idea to help sell the book went wrong. Scott Adams even said it was "the worst idea of the century."
simplified_secondary_school:
"Dilbert and the Way of the Weasel" is a satirical Dilbert book written by Scott Adams, originally published in 2002.

The book's main idea is that people's everyday actions are not consistently honest or criminal, but rather "weasel-like." Adams examines the different aspects of weasel behavior, exploring its benefits and consequences. He sometimes refers to his own life experiences to illustrate these points.

The book is organized into many sections and chapters. These show different types of "weasels," explain how to be a "weasel," discuss "weasel words," and offer methods for countering the "weasels" you might encounter in daily life.

Sales of the book were negatively affected by a marketing announcement from Adams that backfired. He had stated he would dedicate a Dilbert comic strip to the character having sex if the book became the number one seller on Amazon.com. Adams himself later described this promotion as "what will someday be hailed as the worst idea of the century."

original_text: The levator labii superioris alaeque nasi muscle is, translated from Latin, the "lifter of both the upper lip and of the wing of the nose". It has the longest name of any muscle in an animal. The muscle is attached to the upper frontal process of the maxilla and inserts into the skin of the lateral part of the nostril and upper lip. Overview Historically known as Otto's muscle, it dilates the nostril and elevates the upper lip, enabling one to snarl. Elvis Presley is famous for his use of this expression, earning the muscle's nickname "The Elvis muscle". A mnemonic to remember its name is, "Little Ladies Snore All Night." Snore- because it is the labial elevator closest to the nose. The levator labii superioris alaeque nasi is sometimes referred to as the "angular head" of the levator labii superioris muscle. See also Levator labii superioris frontalis muscle Saint Timothy References Muscles of the head and neck

Its name means "lifts the top lip and the side of the nose."

It starts at a bone near your nose.
It goes to your nose skin and your top lip skin.
Long ago, people called it "Otto's muscle."
It makes your nose bigger.
It lifts your top lip.
This helps you snarl.
Elvis Presley used this muscle.
He made a special face with it.
So, people call it "The Elvis muscle."
There is a trick to remember its name.
It is "Little Ladies Snore All Night."
The word "Snore" helps you remember it is near your nose.
It lifts the lip closest to your nose.
Sometimes, it is called the "angular head."
It is part of a bigger muscle.
simplified_primary_school:
This muscle has a very long name. Its name means "the muscle that lifts the upper lip and the side of the nose." It has the longest name of any muscle in any animal. This muscle is connected to the top part of your upper jaw bone. It goes into the skin on the side of your nose and your upper lip.

Long ago, people called it Otto's muscle. It helps make your nostril wider and lifts your upper lip. This lets you make a face like a snarl. Elvis Presley was famous for making this face. So, people also call it "The Elvis muscle."

To help remember its name, some people use a trick: "Little Ladies Snore All Night." The word "Snore" helps because this muscle lifts the lip closest to the nose. Sometimes, people call it the "angular head" of another muscle that lifts the upper lip.
simplified_secondary_school:
The levator labii superioris alaeque nasi muscle, translated from Latin, means "lifter of both the upper lip and of the wing of the nose." It holds the record for the longest name of any muscle in an animal. This muscle attaches to the upper frontal process of the maxilla and inserts into the skin of the lateral part of the nostril and upper lip.

Historically known as Otto's muscle, its function is to dilate the nostril and elevate the upper lip, which allows a person to snarl. Elvis Presley was famous for using this expression, leading to the muscle's nickname, "The Elvis muscle." To help remember its long name, a mnemonic is "Little Ladies Snore All Night." The "Snore" part is a reminder because it is the labial elevator closest to the nose. The levator labii superioris alaeque nasi is sometimes also referred to as the "angular head" of the levator labii superioris muscle.

simplified_kindergarten:

simplified_primary_school:
Most times, when we say 'pots,' we mean pottery. Pottery is things like bowls and plates made from clay by people called potters.

But 'POTS' or 'Pots' can also mean other things:
Plain old telephone service. This is the simple way phones used to connect with a wire.
A POTS codec. This is a machine that helps with sounds.
A DSL filter. People also call it a POTS filter.
A Packet-Optical Transport System. This is a way to send very fast internet signals using light. It can carry many different types of information at the same time.

simplified_secondary_school:
Most commonly, "pots" refers to pottery – ceramic items created by potters. However, the acronym POTS, or sometimes "Pots," can also refer to several other things:

*   **Plain Old Telephone Service (POTS):** This is the basic, traditional telephone connection that uses a physical wire.
*   **POTS codec:** A digital device that handles audio signals.
*   **DSL filter:** Sometimes known as a POTS filter, this device separates internet signals from phone line signals.
*   **Packet-Optical Transport System (POTS):** A technology that uses light to transport very fast 100 Gigabit Ethernet data. It's flexible, meaning it can work with and combine different types of network technologies at lower levels.
*   **Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS):** A medical condition where a person's heart rate increases significantly when they stand up.
*   **Poverty of the Stimulus (POTS):** An argument in linguistics that supports the idea of innate (inborn) grammar, suggesting that the language children hear isn't enough to explain how they learn complex grammar.

original_text: ISO 3166-2:UZ is the entry for Uzbekistan in ISO 3166-2, part of the ISO 3166 standard published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), which defines codes for the names of the principal subdivisions (e.g., provinces or states) of all countries coded in ISO 3166-1. Currently for Uzbekistan, ISO 3166-2 codes are defined for 1 city, 12 regions, and 1 republic. The city Tashkent is the capital of the country and has special status equal to the regions. Each code consists of two parts, separated by a hyphen. The first part is , the ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 code of Uzbekistan. The second part is two letters. Current codes Subdivision names are listed as in the ISO 3166-2 standard published by the ISO 3166 Maintenance Agency (ISO 3166/MA). Click on the button in the header to sort each column. For Uzbekistan, ISO 3166-2 codes are currently defined for one city, twelve regions, and one republic. Tashkent, the capital city, has a special status that is equal to the regions.

original_text: a powerful mathematical method for front instability analysis that was recently developed in the field of nonlinear dynamics is applied to the 1 + 1 ( spatial and time ) dimensional landau - lifshitz - gilbert ( llg ) equation . from the essential spectrum of the llg equation , it is shown that the famous walker rigid body propagating domain wall ( dw ) is not stable against the spin wave emission . in the low field region only stern spin waves are emitted while both stern and bow waves are generated under high fields . by using the properties of the absolute spectrum of the llg equation , it is concluded that in a high enough field , but below the walker breakdown field , the walker solution could be convective / absolute unstable if the transverse magnetic anisotropy is larger than a critical value , corresponding to a significant modification of the dw profile and dw propagating speed . since the walker solution of 1 + 1 dimensional llg equation can be realized in experiments , our results could be also used to test the mathematical method in a controlled manner .
simplified_kindergarten:
A new math way helps us look at things.
We use it on a math rule called LLG.
A special thing, a Walker wall (DW), does not stay strong.
It does not stay strong when little waves come out.

When the push is small, only one kind of wave comes out.
When the push is big, two kinds of waves come out.

If the push is big enough, but not too big, the Walker wall might break.
This happens if a special pull is too big.
Then the wall changes how it looks.
It also changes how fast it moves.

We can make the Walker wall happen in real tests.
So, our findings can help us check if the new math way is good.
simplified_primary_school:
A strong math way to see how things break apart was recently made. This math way is used on a special math rule called the LLG equation. This rule looks at both space and time.

By looking at the LLG rule, we see that a well-known moving thing called a "domain wall" (DW) does not stay steady when little spin waves come out. When the field is not strong, only "stern" spin waves come out. But when the field is strong, both "stern" and "bow" waves come out.

Using what we know about another part of the LLG rule, we can say this: If the field is strong enough (but not too strong), the "walker solution" might not be steady. This happens if a certain magnetic strength is bigger than a special number. If this happens, the DW's shape and how fast it moves would change a lot.

We can make the "walker solution" from the LLG rule happen in real tests. So, our findings can also help us check if the math way works well in a careful way.
simplified_secondary_school: A powerful mathematical method for analyzing front instability, recently developed in nonlinear dynamics, is applied to the 1+1 (spatial and time) dimensional Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equation. From the essential spectrum of the LLG equation, it is shown that the famous Walker rigid body propagating domain wall (DW) is not stable when it emits spin waves. In low magnetic fields, only "stern" spin waves are emitted, while both "stern" and "bow" waves are generated under high fields. By using the properties of the absolute spectrum of the LLG equation, it is concluded that in a high enough magnetic field (but below the Walker breakdown field), the Walker solution could become "convectively" or "absolutely" unstable. This happens if the transverse magnetic anisotropy is larger than a critical value, leading to a significant modification of the DW's profile and propagating speed. Since the Walker solution of the 1+1 dimensional LLG equation can be realized in experiments, our results could also be used to test this mathematical method in a controlled manner.

original_text: Jasper County is the name of eight counties in the United States of America. All are named in honor of Sergeant William Jasper, a hero of the Revolutionary War. original_text: the pressure induced bcc to hcp transition in fe has been investigated via ab - initio electronic structure calculations . it is found by the disordered local moment ( dlm ) calculations that the temperature induced spin fluctuations result in the decrease of the energy of burgers type lattice distortions and softening of the transverse @xmath0-point @xmath1 phonon mode with @xmath2 $ ] polarization . as a consequence , spin disorder in an system leads to the increase of the amplitude of atomic displacements . on the other hand , the exchange coupling parameters obtained in our calculations strongly decrease at large amplitude of lattice distortions . this results in a mutual interrelation of structural and magnetic degrees of freedom leading to the instability of the bcc structure under pressure at finite temperature .
simplified_kindergarten:
Pressure makes iron change shape. It goes from bcc to hcp. We used computers to look at this.

Our computer math showed something. When it gets warm, tiny parts inside iron move around. This makes the iron's shape bend more easily. It also makes some parts inside the iron move more easily.

So, when the tiny parts inside are messy, the iron's tiny pieces move around more.

But our computer math also showed something else. The way tiny parts talk gets weaker. This happens when the iron's shape bends a lot.

So, the iron's shape and its tiny moving parts are linked. They affect each other. This makes the bcc shape of iron not strong. It can change easily when there is pressure and it is warm.
simplified_primary_school:
Scientists studied how iron changes its shape from bcc to hcp when there is a lot of pressure. They used special computer math to understand this.

Their computer math showed that when it gets warm, tiny parts inside the iron (called spins) wiggle around. This wiggling makes it easier for the iron's shape to twist a little. It also makes some tiny vibrations inside the iron become weaker.

Because of this, when the tiny wiggling spins are messy, the atoms in the iron move around more.

But the computer math also showed that when the iron's shape is very twisted, the tiny wiggling spins don't connect as strongly.

This means the iron's shape and how its tiny wiggling spins behave affect each other. This makes the bcc shape of iron not stable anymore when there is pressure and it is not super cold.
simplified_secondary_school:
Scientists used advanced computer simulations, called ab-initio electronic structure calculations, to investigate how iron (Fe) changes from a body-centered cubic (bcc) structure to a hexagonal close-packed (hcp) structure when under pressure.

Using calculations that account for disordered magnetic moments (DLM calculations), it was discovered that temperature-induced changes in electron spins cause two things:
1. The energy required for specific crystal deformations, known as Burgers type lattice distortions, decreases.
2. A specific transverse phonon mode (a particular type of atomic vibration) becomes less stiff.

Consequently, when electron spins are disordered within the system, atoms move more significantly from their usual positions. Conversely, the magnetic forces (exchange coupling parameters) between atoms, as determined by our calculations, significantly weaken when the crystal lattice is highly distorted.

This reveals a strong, interconnected relationship between the material's structure and its magnetic properties. This relationship ultimately leads to the bcc structure becoming unstable under pressure at temperatures above absolute zero.
